sections.title: Argument Forms and Informal Proof Patterns

nodes.name: Axiom
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order language, and let
$\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ denote the set of sentences of
$\mathcal{L}$. If $T\subseteq \operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ is a theory,
then an \emph{axiom of $T$} is an element $\alpha\in T$.

nodes.name: Axiom System
nodes.statement_display:
An \emph{axiom system} is a collection of axioms expressed in a common
language.

An \emph{axiom system} is a collection of axioms expressed in a common
language.

nodes.name: Assumption
nodes.statement_display:
An \emph{assumption} is a statement temporarily accepted for purposes of
reasoning.

nodes.name: Contradiction
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{contradiction} is a statement that cannot be true under a given
interpretation.

A \emph{contradiction} is a statement that cannot be true under a given
interpretation.

nodes.name: Consistent Axiom System
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{consistent axiom system} is an axiom system that does not lead to
contradiction.
nodes.statement_tex:
A \emph{consistent axiom system} is an axiom system that does not lead to
contradiction.

nodes.name: Inconsistent Axiom System
nodes.statement_display:
An \emph{inconsistent axiom system} is an axiom system containing or producing
contradictory assertions.

nodes.name: Model Existence Criterion (Informal)
nodes.statement_display:
Informally, if an axiom system has a model, then the axioms are mutually
compatible.

compatible.

nodes.name: Relative Consistency
nodes.statement_display:
Let $T_0$ and $T_1$ be theories. The theory $T_1$ is
\emph{consistent relative to $T_0$} if
\[
  \operatorname{Con}(T_0)\implies \operatorname{Con}(T_1).
\]
Equivalently, every contradiction in $T_1$ would imply a contradiction in
$T_0$.

nodes.name: Interpretation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar})$ be a
first-order signature, and let $M$ be a nonempty set. An
\emph{interpretation of $\Sigma$ on $M$} is an assignment $\mathcal{I}$ that
maps each symbol of $\Sigma$ to concrete data over $M$: constants are assigned
elements of $M$, function symbols are assigned operations on $M$, and relation
symbols are assigned relations on $M$.

nodes.name: $\mathcal{L}$-Structure
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order signature. An
\emph{$\mathcal{L}$-structure} is a pair
\[
  \mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I}),
\]
where $M$ is a nonempty domain and $\mathcal{I}$ is an interpretation of the
symbols of $\mathcal{L}$ on $M$.
nodes.statement_tex:

symbols of $\mathcal{L}$ on $M$.

nodes.name: Model
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order signature, let $T$ be a theory over
$\mathcal{L}$, and let $\mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I})$ be an
$\mathcal{L}$-structure. The structure $\mathfrak{M}$ is a \emph{model of
$T$}, written $\mathfrak{M}\vDash T$, if every sentence in $T$ is true in
$\mathfrak{M}$.
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Satisfaction
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I})$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure. A
\emph{variable assignment} is a function $s:\operatorname{Var}\to M$. The
assignment extends recursively to a term-evaluation map $\bar{s}$ by
\[
  \bar{s}(x)=s(x),\qquad
  \bar{s}(c)=\mathcal{I}(c),
\]
and
\[
  \bar{s}\bigl(f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\bigr)
  =
  \mathcal{I}(f)\bigl(\bar{s}(t_1),\ldots,\bar{s}(t_n)\bigr).
\]
The satisfaction relation $\mathfrak{M}\vDash\varphi[s]$ is then defined by
induction on formulas: atomic relations are tested inside the interpreted
relation, equality is interpreted as equality in $M$, Boolean connectives are
interpreted by the corresponding truth operations, and quantifiers range over
all elements of $M$.

nodes.name: Countermodel
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\Gamma\subseteq\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ be a set of premises,
let $\varphi\in\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$, and let $\mathfrak{M}$ be
an $\mathcal{L}$-structure. A \emph{countermodel to the entailment
$\Gamma\vDash\varphi$} is a structure $\mathfrak{M}$ that satisfies every
sentence in $\Gamma$ but does not satisfy $\varphi$.
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: First-Order Signature
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{first-order signature} is a tuple
\[
  \Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}),
\]
where $\mathcal{C}$ is a set of constant symbols, $\mathcal{F}$ is a set of
function symbols, $\mathcal{R}$ is a set of relation symbols, and
\[
  \operatorname{ar}:\mathcal{F}\cup\mathcal{R}\to \mathbb{Z}_{>0}
\]
assigns to each function or relation symbol its number of arguments.

nodes.name: Arity
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar})$ be a
first-order signature. The \emph{arity function} is the map
\[
  \operatorname{ar}:\mathcal{F}\cup\mathcal{R}\to\mathbb{Z}_{>0}
\]
that assigns to each function or relation symbol the number of arguments it
requires. When constants are treated as $0$-ary function symbols, the codomain
is enlarged to include $0$.

nodes.name: Language
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{first-order language} has two standard readings. As vocabulary, it is
the same data as a signature,
\[
  \mathcal{L}=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}).
\]
As a formal language generated by a signature $\Sigma$, it is the set of
well-formed strings built from the alphabet
\[
  A=\Sigma\cup V\cup\Lambda,
\]
where $V$ is a countable set of variables and $\Lambda$ is the fixed stock of
logical symbols and punctuation. In this second reading,
\[
  \mathcal{L}(\Sigma)
  =\{s\in A^* \mid s\in\operatorname{Terms}(\Sigma)
    \text{ or } s\in\operatorname{WFF}(\Sigma)\}.
\]

nodes.name: Constant Symbol
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{constant symbol} is a non-logical symbol that behaves syntactically as
a $0$-ary function symbol. In the streamlined convention where constants are
included among function symbols, this is the condition
\[
  \operatorname{Constant}(c)
  \iff c\in\mathcal{F}\text{ and }\operatorname{ar}(c)=0.
\]
Because its arity is $0$, the symbol $c$ is already a well-formed term and
takes no input terms.

nodes.name: Function Symbol
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{function symbol} is a non-logical symbol $f\in\mathcal{F}$ whose
assigned arity determines how many terms it must take as inputs. If
$\operatorname{ar}(f)=n$, then $f$ is an $n$-ary term constructor: it takes
exactly $n$ terms and produces a new term, not a truth value.

nodes.name: Relation Symbol
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{relation symbol}, or predicate symbol, is a non-logical symbol
$R\in\mathcal{R}$ whose assigned arity determines how many terms it must take
as inputs. If $\operatorname{ar}(R)=n$, then $R$ is an $n$-ary formula
constructor: it takes exactly $n$ terms and produces an atomic formula, hence
something with a truth value rather than an object.

nodes.name: Theory
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{theory} over a first-order language $\mathcal{L}$ is a collection of
closed sentences in that language:
\[
  T\subseteq\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L}).
\]
If $\Gamma$ is a chosen set of axioms, the theory generated by $\Gamma$ is the
deductive closure
\[
  T=\{\varphi\in\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})\mid
      \Gamma\vdash\varphi\}.
\]

nodes.name: Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{theorem} of a theory $T$ is a sentence $\varphi$ for which there is a
finite formal derivation from $T$, written
\[
  T\vdash\varphi.
\]
Equivalently, in proof-theoretic terms, $\varphi$ is a theorem of $T$ exactly
when some derivation has leaves drawn from logical axioms or sentences of
$T$, uses valid inference rules, and has $\varphi$ as its root.

nodes.name: Consequence
nodes.statement_display:
A sentence $\varphi$ is a \emph{semantic consequence} of a set of premises
$\Gamma$, written $\Gamma\vDash\varphi$, if every model of $\Gamma$ is also a
model of $\varphi$. Equivalently, there is no structure in which all sentences
of $\Gamma$ are true while $\varphi$ is false.

model of $\varphi$. Equivalently, there is no structure in which all sentences
of $\Gamma$ are true while $\varphi$ is false.

nodes.name: Deductive Closure
nodes.statement_display:

premises. The \emph{deductive closure} of $\Gamma$, denoted
$\operatorname{Cn}(\Gamma)$, is the set of all sentences derivable from
$\Gamma$:
\[
  \operatorname{Cn}(\Gamma)
  =
  \{\varphi\in\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})\mid
    \Gamma\vdash\varphi\}.
\]

nodes.name: Universal Instantiation
nodes.statement_display:
For every formula $\varphi(x)$ and every term $t$ that is free for $x$ in
$\varphi$,
\[
\forall x\,\varphi(x) \Longrightarrow \varphi(t).
\]

nodes.name: Quantifier Distribution
nodes.statement_display:
For formulas $\varphi$ and $\psi(x)$, if $x$ is not free in $\varphi$, then
\[
\forall x\,(\varphi \Longrightarrow \psi(x))
\Longrightarrow
\bigl(\varphi \Longrightarrow \forall x\,\psi(x)\bigr).
\]

\]

nodes.name: Equality Introduction — Reflexivity
nodes.statement_display: For any term $t$, one may infer $t = t$.
nodes.statement_tex: For any term $t$, one may infer $t = t$.
nodes.source_text: For any term $t$, one may infer $t = t$.

nodes.name: Equality Elimination — Substitution of Equals
nodes.statement_display: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $\varphi[t_1/x]$, one may infer $\varphi[t_2/x]$.
nodes.statement_tex: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $\varphi[t_1/x]$, one may infer $\varphi[t_2/x]$.

nodes.name: Symmetry of Equality
nodes.statement_display: From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.
nodes.statement_tex: From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.
nodes.source_text: From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.

nodes.name: Transitivity of Equality
nodes.statement_display: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.

nodes.name: Term Substitution under Equality
nodes.statement_display:
If $t_1 = t_2$, then for any function symbol $f$,
$f(\dots,t_1,\dots) = f(\dots,t_2,\dots)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $t_1 = t_2$, then for any function symbol $f$,
$f(\dots,t_1,\dots) = f(\dots,t_2,\dots)$.

nodes.name: Predicate Substitution under Equality
nodes.statement_display:
If $t_1 = t_2$ and $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol, then
$P(\dots,t_1,\dots) \;\Leftrightarrow\; P(\dots,t_2,\dots)$.

nodes.name: Universal Instantiation — UI
nodes.statement_display:
From a universally quantified statement, infer any instance obtained by
substituting a term for the bound variable:
\[
\forall x\,\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \varphi[t/x].
\]

nodes.name: Universal Generalization — UG

\[
\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \forall x\,\varphi.

nodes.name: Existential Instantiation — EI
nodes.statement_display:
From an existentially quantified statement, infer an instance with a fresh
constant (witness):
\[
\exists x\,\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \varphi[c/x].
\]

\varphi[t/x] \;\Rightarrow\; \exists x\,\varphi.

nodes.name: Soundness
nodes.statement_display:
A proof system is \emph{sound} if every provable formula is valid:
\[
\Gamma \vdash \varphi \implies \Gamma \models \varphi.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
A proof system is \emph{sound} if every provable formula is valid:
\[
\Gamma \vdash \varphi \implies \Gamma \models \varphi.
\]

nodes.name: Completeness
nodes.statement_display:
A proof system is \emph{complete} if every valid formula is provable:
\[
\Gamma \models \varphi \implies \Gamma \vdash \varphi.
\]

A proof system is \emph{complete} if every valid formula is provable:

nodes.name: Soundness of First-Order Logic
nodes.statement_display:
The standard inference rules for first-order logic (UI, UG, EI, EG, and the
propositional rules) are sound: they preserve truth in all structures.

If $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$, then $\Gamma \models \varphi$.

nodes.name: Universal Formula
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{universal formula} is a well-formed formula of the form
\[
\forall x\,\varphi,
\]
where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.

nodes.name: Existential Formula
nodes.statement_display:
An \emph{existential formula} is a well-formed formula of the form
\[
\exists x\,\varphi,
\]
where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.

nodes.name: Bounded Quantifier
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{bounded quantifier} is an abbreviation that restricts a quantifier to a
specified set or predicate:
\[
\forall x\in A\,\varphi
\quad\coloneqq\quad
\forall x\,(x\in A\to\varphi),
\]
\[
\exists x\in A\,\varphi
\quad\coloneqq\quad
\exists x\,(x\in A\land\varphi).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

specified set or predicate:

\quad\coloneqq\quad

\]
\[
\exists x\in A\,\varphi
\quad\coloneqq\quad
\exists x\,(x\in A\land\varphi).
\]

nodes.name: Quantifier Scope
nodes.statement_display:
In a formula \(Qx\,\varphi\), where \(Q\in\{\forall,\exists\}\), the
\emph{scope} of the quantifier \(Qx\) is the formula \(\varphi\). Occurrences
of \(x\) inside that scope are bound by the quantifier unless an inner
quantifier for \(x\) intervenes.

quantifier for \(x\) intervenes.

nodes.name: Negation of Bounded Quantifiers
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A\) be a set and let \(\varphi\) be a formula. Then
\[
\neg(\forall x\in A\,\varphi)\equiv \exists x\in A\,\neg\varphi,
\qquad
\neg(\exists x\in A\,\varphi)\equiv \forall x\in A\,\neg\varphi.
\]

\[

\qquad
\neg(\exists x\in A\,\varphi)\equiv \forall x\in A\,\neg\varphi.
\]

eg\varphi. \]

nodes.name: Stronger Formula
nodes.statement_display:
A sentence \(A\) is \emph{stronger than} a sentence \(B\) exactly when
\[
A\models B
\quad\text{and}\quad
B\not\models A.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Weaker Formula
nodes.statement_display:
A sentence \(B\) is \emph{weaker than} a sentence \(A\) exactly when \(A\) is
stronger than \(B\).

nodes.name: Quantifier Order
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{quantifier order} of a prenex formula is the ordered list of
quantifiers in its prefix. A change of order is significant when it changes
which witnesses may depend on earlier variables.

nodes.name: Universal Implies Existential
nodes.statement_display:
Over nonempty domains,
\[
\forall x\,\varphi(x)\models \exists x\,\varphi(x).
\]

\[

\]

nodes.statement_display:
In general,
\[
\exists x\,\varphi(x)\not\models \forall x\,\varphi(x).
\]

\[

nodes.name: Uniform Witness Implies Dependent Witness

\[
\exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi(x,y)
\models
\forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi(x,y).
\]

nodes.name: Quantifier Prefix
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{quantifier prefix} is a finite string
\[
Q_1x_1\,Q_2x_2\cdots Q_nx_n,
\]
where each \(Q_i\in\{\forall,\exists\}\) and each \(x_i\) is a variable.

A \emph{quantifier prefix} is a finite string

nodes.name: Matrix
nodes.statement_display:
In a formula \(Q_1x_1\cdots Q_nx_n\,\psi\), the \emph{matrix} is the trailing
formula \(\psi\). For prenex normal form, the matrix is required to be
quantifier-free.

nodes.name: Prenex Formula
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{prenex formula} is a formula of the form
\[
Q_1x_1\,Q_2x_2\cdots Q_nx_n\,\psi,
\]
where \(Q_1x_1\cdots Q_nx_n\) is a quantifier prefix and \(\psi\) is
quantifier-free.

Q_1x_1\,Q_2x_2\cdots Q_nx_n\,\psi,
\]
where \(Q_1x_1\cdots Q_nx_n\) is a quantifier prefix and \(\psi\) is
quantifier-free.

nodes.name: Prenex Normal Form
nodes.statement_display:
A formula \(\psi\) is a \emph{prenex normal form} of a formula \(\varphi\)
exactly when \(\psi\) is prenex and \(\varphi\equiv\psi\).
nodes.statement_tex:

exactly when \(\psi\) is prenex and \(\varphi\equiv\psi\).
nodes.source_text:
A formula \(\psi\) is a \emph{prenex normal form} of a formula \(\varphi\)
exactly when \(\psi\) is prenex and \(\varphi\equiv\psi\).
nodes.text_preview: A formula \(\psi\) is a \emph{prenex normal form} of a formula \(\varphi\) exactly when \(\psi\) is prenex and \(\varphi\equiv\psi\).

nodes.name: Existence of Prenex Normal Form
nodes.statement_display:
Every first-order formula is logically equivalent to some formula in prenex
normal form.
nodes.statement_tex:
Every first-order formula is logically equivalent to some formula in prenex
normal form.

nodes.name: Quantifier Negation Laws
nodes.statement_display:
For every formula \(\varphi\),
\[
\neg\forall x\,\varphi \equiv \exists x\,\neg\varphi,
\qquad
\neg\exists x\,\varphi \equiv \forall x\,\neg\varphi.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

\[

\qquad
\neg\exists x\,\varphi \equiv \forall x\,\neg\varphi.

eg\varphi, \qquad

eg\varphi. \]

nodes.name: Double Negation and Quantifiers
nodes.statement_display:
For every formula \(\varphi\),
\[
\neg\neg\forall x\,\varphi \equiv \forall x\,\varphi,
\qquad
\neg\neg\exists x\,\varphi \equiv \exists x\,\varphi.
\]

nodes.name: Same-Type Quantifier Commutation
nodes.statement_display:
Quantifiers of the same type commute:
\[
\forall x\,\forall y\,\varphi \equiv \forall y\,\forall x\,\varphi,
\qquad
\exists x\,\exists y\,\varphi \equiv \exists y\,\exists x\,\varphi.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

\[

\qquad
\exists x\,\exists y\,\varphi \equiv \exists y\,\exists x\,\varphi.
\]

nodes.name: Mixed Quantifiers Do Not Commute
nodes.statement_display:
In general,
\[
\forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi
\not\equiv
\exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi.
\]

nodes.name: Valid Quantifier Distribution
nodes.statement_display:
For all formulas \(\varphi,\psi\),
\[
\forall x\,(\varphi\land\psi)
\equiv
(\forall x\,\varphi)\land(\forall x\,\psi),
\]
\[
\exists x\,(\varphi\lor\psi)
\equiv
(\exists x\,\varphi)\lor(\exists x\,\psi).
\]

nodes.name: Vacuous Quantification
nodes.statement_display:
If \(x\) does not occur free in \(\varphi\), then
\[
\forall x\,\varphi\equiv\varphi,
\qquad
\exists x\,\varphi\equiv\varphi.
\]

nodes.name: Renaming Bound Variables
nodes.statement_display:
If \(y\) is free for \(x\) in \(\varphi\) and no capture is introduced, then
\[
\forall x\,\varphi\equiv\forall y\,\varphi[y/x],
\qquad
\exists x\,\varphi\equiv\exists y\,\varphi[y/x].
\]

\]

nodes.name: Model of a Sentence
used_by_titles: Model of a Set of Sentences
nodes.text_preview: Let \(\varphi\) be a sentence and let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure for the language of \(\varphi\). The structure \(\mathcal M\) is a \emph{model of} \(\varphi\) exactly when \(\mathcal M\models\varphi\).

nodes.name: Model of a Set of Sentences
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(T\) be a set of sentences and let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure for their
common language. The structure \(\mathcal M\) is a \emph{model of} \(T\),
written \(\mathcal M\models T\), exactly when
\(\mathcal M\models\theta\) for every \(\theta\in T\).

nodes.name: Theory
nodes.statement_display:
In Volume I, a \emph{theory} is a set of sentences in a fixed first-order
language.

nodes.name: Logical Consequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(T\) be a set of sentences and let \(\varphi\) be a sentence in the same
language. The sentence \(\varphi\) is a \emph{logical consequence} of \(T\),
written \(T\models\varphi\), exactly when every model of \(T\) is a model of
\(\varphi\).

nodes.name: Logical Equivalence
nodes.statement_display:
Two sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are \emph{logically equivalent}, written
\(\varphi\equiv\psi\), exactly when each is a logical consequence of the other:
\[
\varphi\models\psi
\quad\text{and}\quad
\psi\models\varphi.
\]

nodes.name: Open Formula
nodes.statement_display:
An \emph{open formula} is a well-formed formula with at least one free
variable. When the free variables are among \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\), it may be
displayed as \(\varphi(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\).
nodes.statement_tex:

displayed as \(\varphi(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\).

nodes.name: Definable Relation
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure and let
\(\varphi(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\) be an open formula. The \emph{relation defined by}
\(\varphi\) in \(\mathcal M\) is the set
\[
R_{\varphi}^{\mathcal M}
=
\{(a_1,\ldots,a_n)\in D^n:
\mathcal M,s[x_1\mapsto a_1,\ldots,x_n\mapsto a_n]\models\varphi\}.
\]

nodes.name: Substitution Lemma for Terms
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure, let \(s\) be a variable assignment, let
\(x\) be a variable, and let \(t\) and \(u\) be terms. Then
\[
\llbracket t[u/x]\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}
=
\llbracket t\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,\,
s[x\mapsto\llbracket u\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}]}.
\]

nodes.name: Substitution Lemma for Formulas
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\varphi\) be a formula and let \(u\) be a term free for \(x\) in
\(\varphi\). For every structure \(\mathcal M\) and assignment \(s\),
\[
\mathcal M,s\models\varphi[u/x]
\Longleftrightarrow
\mathcal M,s[x\mapsto\llbracket u\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}]\models\varphi.
\]

\]

nodes.name: Satisfaction
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure, let \(s\) be a variable
assignment, and let \(\varphi\) be a well-formed formula. The relation
\(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\) is defined recursively by the usual clauses:
atomic formulas are evaluated by term values and interpreted predicate
relations; equality compares term values; connectives follow the truth
conditions for \(\neg,\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\); and
\[
\mathcal M,s\models\forall x\,\psi
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall d\in D\;(\mathcal M,s[x\mapsto d]\models\psi),
\]
\[
\mathcal M,s\models\exists x\,\psi
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists d\in D\;(\mathcal M,s[x\mapsto d]\models\psi).
\]

nodes.name: Truth in a Structure
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\varphi\) be a sentence and let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure. The
sentence \(\varphi\) is \emph{true in} \(\mathcal M\), written
\(\mathcal M\models\varphi\), exactly when
\(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\) for every variable assignment \(s\) for
\(\mathcal M\).

\(\mathcal M\).

nodes.name: Validity
nodes.statement_display:
A formula \(\varphi\) is \emph{valid}, or \emph{logically true}, exactly when
\(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\) for every structure \(\mathcal M\) for the
language of \(\varphi\) and every variable assignment \(s\) for \(\mathcal M\).

nodes.name: Satisfiability
nodes.statement_display:
A formula \(\varphi\) is \emph{satisfiable} exactly when there are a structure
\(\mathcal M\) and a variable assignment \(s\) such that
\(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\).

nodes.name: First-Order Language
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{first-order language} \(\mathcal L\) consists of variables, logical
symbols, and a signature of non-logical symbols: constant symbols, function
symbols with assigned arities, and predicate symbols with assigned arities.
Equality may be included as a distinguished logical predicate.

nodes.name: Structure
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal L\) be a first-order language. A \emph{structure} for
\(\mathcal L\) is a pair
\[
\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle
\]
where \(D\) is a nonempty set and \(I\) assigns each constant symbol an element
of \(D\), each \(n\)-ary function symbol a function \(D^n\to D\), and each
\(n\)-ary predicate symbol a relation on \(D^n\).

\[
s:\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\to D.
\]

nodes.name: Modified Assignment
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(s:\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\to D\), let \(x\in\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\),
and let \(d\in D\). The \emph{modified assignment} \(s[x\mapsto d]\) is the
assignment defined by
\[
s[x\mapsto d](y)=
\begin{cases}
d, & y=x,\\
s(y), & y\neq x.
\end{cases}
\]

nodes.name: Term Evaluation
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure and \(s\) a variable
assignment. The \emph{value} of a term \(t\), denoted
\(\llbracket t\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}\), is defined recursively by
\[
\llbracket x\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}=s(x),\qquad
\llbracket c\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}=I(c),
\]
and
\[
\llbracket f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}
=
I(f)\bigl(\llbracket t_1\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s},\ldots,
\llbracket t_n\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Formula Depth
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{depth} (or \emph{complexity}) of a formula $\varphi$, denoted
$\mathrm{depth}(\varphi)$, is a natural number defined recursively:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Atomic case.} If $\varphi$ is atomic, then $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = 0$.

  \item \textbf{Negation.} If $\varphi = \neg \psi$, then
  $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = \mathrm{depth}(\psi) + 1$.

  \item \textbf{Binary connectives.} If $\varphi = (\psi \circ \chi)$
  for $\circ \in \{\wedge,\vee,\rightarrow,\leftrightarrow\}$, then
  \[
  \mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = \max\{\mathrm{depth}(\psi),\,\mathrm{depth}(\chi)\} + 1.
  \]

  \item \textbf{Quantifiers.} If $\varphi = \forall x\,\psi$ or $\varphi = \exists x\,\psi$,
  then $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = \mathrm{depth}(\psi) + 1$.
\end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Variable
nodes.statement_display:
For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{variable} is a symbol drawn
from the distinguished variable stock of \(\mathcal L\). Variables are usually
written
\[
x,y,z,x_0,x_1,x_2,\ldots .
\]

nodes.name: Constant Symbol
nodes.statement_display:
For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{constant symbol} is a
non-logical symbol of arity \(0\). A constant symbol may occur as a term without
taking input terms.

taking input terms.

nodes.name: Function Symbol
nodes.statement_display:
For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), an \(n\)-ary \emph{function symbol}
is a non-logical symbol of arity \(n\geq 1\). If \(f\) has arity \(n\), then it
forms terms by the pattern
\[
f(t_1,\ldots,t_n).
\]

forms atomic formulas by the pattern
\[
P(t_1,\ldots,t_n).
\]

nodes.name: Atomic Formula
nodes.statement_display:
An \emph{atomic formula} of a first-order language \(\mathcal L\) is a string
of the form
\[
P(t_1,\ldots,t_n),
\]
where \(P\) is an \(n\)-ary predicate symbol and
\(t_1,\ldots,t_n\) are terms.

nodes.name: Formula
nodes.statement_display:
For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{formula} is an expression
formed by the formula grammar of \(\mathcal L\). The grammar starts from atomic
formulas and closes under the logical connectives and quantifiers of the
language.

nodes.name: Molecular Formula
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{molecular formula} is a well-formed formula that is not atomic.
Equivalently, it is a well-formed formula whose final formation step uses a
logical connective or a quantifier.

nodes.name: Scope of a Quantifier
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\varphi$ be a formula of a first-order language.

If $\varphi$ is of the form $\forall x\,\psi$ or $\exists x\,\psi$, then the
formula $\psi$ is called the \emph{scope} of the quantifier.

The quantifier is said to \emph{bind} all occurrences of the variable $x$ that
appear within its scope.

nodes.name: Bound and Free Occurrences
nodes.statement_display:
An occurrence of a variable $x$ in a formula $\varphi$ is \emph{bound} if it
lies within the scope of a quantifier $\forall x$ or $\exists x$.

An occurrence of $x$ is \emph{free} if it is not bound by any quantifier in
$\varphi$.

$\varphi$.

nodes.name: Free Variables of a Formula
nodes.statement_display:
The set $\mathrm{FV}(\varphi)$ of \emph{free variables} of a formula $\varphi$
is defined recursively:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item If $\varphi = P(t_1,\dots,t_n)$ is atomic, then
  $\mathrm{FV}(\varphi) = \bigcup_{i=1}^n \mathrm{Var}(t_i)$,
  where $\mathrm{Var}(t_i)$ is the set of variables in term $t_i$.

  \item $\mathrm{FV}(\neg \varphi) = \mathrm{FV}(\varphi)$.

  \item $\mathrm{FV}(\varphi \circ \psi) = \mathrm{FV}(\varphi) \cup \mathrm{FV}(\psi)$
  for any binary connective $\circ$.

  \item $\mathrm{FV}(\forall x\,\varphi) = \mathrm{FV}(\varphi) \setminus \{x\}$.

  \item $\mathrm{FV}(\exists x\,\varphi) = \mathrm{FV}(\varphi) \setminus \{x\}$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.statement_tex:

  \item $\mathrm{FV}(\forall x\,\varphi) = \mathrm{FV}(\varphi) \setminus \{x\}$.

  \item $\mathrm{FV}(\exists x\,\varphi) = \mathrm{FV}(\varphi) \setminus \{x\}$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Sentence
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{sentence} (or \emph{closed formula}) is a formula with no free variables.

Formally, $\varphi$ is a sentence if and only if $\mathrm{FV}(\varphi) = \varnothing$.

nodes.name: Substitution Notation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\varphi$ be a formula, $x$ a variable, and $t$ a term.

The expression $\varphi[t/x]$ denotes the formula obtained from $\varphi$ by
replacing every \emph{free} occurrence of $x$ with the term $t$, leaving bound
occurrences of $x$ unchanged.

occurrences of $x$ unchanged.

nodes.name: Free for Substitution
nodes.statement_display:
A term $t$ is \emph{free for} $x$ in $\varphi$ if no free occurrence of $x$ in
$\varphi$ lies within the scope of a quantifier $\forall y$ or $\exists y$
where $y$ is a variable occurring in $t$.

Equivalently, $t$ is free for $x$ in $\varphi$ if the substitution $\varphi[t/x]$
does not result in any variable in $t$ becoming bound.

nodes.name: Alpha-Equivalence
nodes.statement_display:
Formulas that differ only by the names of bound variables are logically
equivalent. This is called \emph{alpha-equivalence} (or \emph{alphabetic
variance}):
\[
\forall x\,\varphi \;\equiv\; \forall y\,\varphi[y/x]
\quad\text{(provided $y$ is not free in $\varphi$).}
\]

nodes.name: Translation Key
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{translation key} is a finite record that fixes a domain of discourse and
assigns intended English readings to the constant symbols, predicate symbols,
and relation symbols used in a translation.
nodes.statement_tex:

and relation symbols used in a translation.

nodes.name: Domain of Discourse
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{domain of discourse} for a translation is the collection of objects
over which the variables of the translated formulas range.
nodes.statement_tex:
The \emph{domain of discourse} for a translation is the collection of objects
over which the variables of the translated formulas range.

nodes.name: Predicate and Relation Symbols in a Translation
nodes.statement_display:
In a translation key, a unary predicate symbol names a property of one object,
and an \(n\)-ary relation symbol names a relation among \(n\) objects.
nodes.statement_tex:
In a translation key, a unary predicate symbol names a property of one object,
and an \(n\)-ary relation symbol names a relation among \(n\) objects.

nodes.name: Constants and Variables in a Translation
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{constant} names a fixed object from the translation key, while a
\emph{variable} marks a place bound by a quantifier or left open for later
binding.

\emph{variable} marks a place bound by a quantifier or left open for later
binding.

\emph{variable} marks a place bound by a quantifier or left open for later
binding.

nodes.name: Universal Translation Pattern
nodes.statement_display:
In a broad domain, an English statement of the form ``All \(A\) are \(B\)''
is translated by
\[
\forall x\,(A(x)\to B(x)).
\]

nodes.name: Existential Translation Pattern

is translated by
\[
\exists x\,(A(x)\land B(x)).
\]

nodes.name: Conditional Translation Pattern
nodes.statement_display:
An English statement of the form ``If \(A\), then \(B\)'' is translated by
\[
A\to B,
\]
with quantifiers placed according to the variables occurring in \(A\) and \(B\).

nodes.name: Negation Translation Pattern
nodes.statement_display:
The negation of a translated quantified statement is obtained by negating the
whole formula and then pushing the negation inward using the quantifier
negation laws.

nodes.name: Scope Ambiguity
nodes.statement_display:
A sentence has \emph{scope ambiguity} when the same ordinary-language wording
admits two or more translations with different quantifier nesting or different
connective scope.

nodes.name: A-O Contradiction
nodes.statement_display:
The \(A\)-form \(\forall x\,(A(x)\to B(x))\) and the \(O\)-form
\(\exists x\,(A(x)\land \neg B(x))\) are contradictory: exactly one of them is
true.

\(\exists x\,(A(x)\land \neg B(x))\) are contradictory: exactly one of them is
true.

nodes.name: Propositional Equivalence Law
nodes.statement_display:
A propositional equivalence law is a valid schema of the form
\[
\varphi\equiv\psi,
\]
with \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\). Each instance of the schema says that the two formulas have the same truth value under every truth assignment.

nodes.name: Logical Equivalence is an Equivalence Relation
nodes.statement_display:
Logical equivalence is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive on \(\WFF\):
\[
\varphi\equiv\varphi,
\qquad
\varphi\equiv\psi\Longrightarrow\psi\equiv\varphi,
\]
and
\[
(\varphi\equiv\psi\land\psi\equiv\chi)
\Longrightarrow
\varphi\equiv\chi.
\]

nodes.name: Basic Boolean Equivalence Laws
nodes.statement_display:
For all formulas \(\varphi,\psi,\chi\in\WFF\), the following logical equivalences hold.

Commutativity:
\[
\varphi\land\psi\equiv\psi\land\varphi,
\qquad
\varphi\lor\psi\equiv\psi\lor\varphi.
\]

Associativity:
\[
(\varphi\land\psi)\land\chi
\equiv
\varphi\land(\psi\land\chi),
\qquad
(\varphi\lor\psi)\lor\chi
\equiv
\varphi\lor(\psi\lor\chi).
\]

Idempotence:
\[
\varphi\land\varphi\equiv\varphi,
\qquad
\varphi\lor\varphi\equiv\varphi.
\]

Double negation:
\[
\neg\neg\varphi\equiv\varphi.
\]

nodes.name: Identity and Domination Laws

Let \(\top\) be any tautological formula and let \(\bot\) be any contradictory formula. Then for every \(\varphi\in\WFF\):

\qquad

\]
and
\[
\varphi\land\bot\equiv\bot,
\qquad
\varphi\lor\top\equiv\top.
\]

nodes.name: Formula Context
nodes.statement_display: A formula context \(C[-]\) is a well-formed formula with one distinguished placeholder occurrence \([-]\). For each well-formed formula \(\varphi\), the expression \(C[\varphi]\) denotes the formula obtained by replacing the placeholder with \(\varphi\).

nodes.name: Replacement of Logical Equivalents
nodes.statement_display:
Replacing logically equivalent formulas inside a formula context preserves logical equivalence:
\[
\varphi\equiv\psi
\Longrightarrow
C[\varphi]\equiv C[\psi].
\]

nodes.name: Argument Form
nodes.statement_display:
An \emph{argument form} is a schematic pattern consisting of a finite list of
premise formulas and one conclusion formula.
nodes.statement_tex:
An \emph{argument form} is a schematic pattern consisting of a finite list of
premise formulas and one conclusion formula.

nodes.name: Valid Argument Form
nodes.statement_display:
An argument form with premises \(\Gamma\) and conclusion \(\varphi\) is
\emph{valid} if every truth assignment that satisfies every formula in
\(\Gamma\) also satisfies \(\varphi\).
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Counterexample Assignment
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{counterexample assignment} to an argument form \((\Gamma,\varphi)\) is a
truth assignment that satisfies every premise in \(\Gamma\) and does not satisfy
\(\varphi\).

nodes.name: Truth-Table Validity Test for Argument Forms
nodes.statement_display:
An argument form \((\Gamma,\varphi)\) is valid if and only if no row of its truth
table makes every formula in \(\Gamma\) true and \(\varphi\) false.
nodes.statement_tex:

table makes every formula in \(\Gamma\) true and \(\varphi\) false.

An argument form \((\Gamma,\varphi)\) is valid if and only if no row of its truth
table makes every formula in \(\Gamma\) true and \(\varphi\) false.

nodes.name: Rule of Replacement
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{rule of replacement} is a schematic permission to replace a subformula
by a logically equivalent formula inside a formula context.

nodes.name: Replacement Rules Preserve Logical Equivalence
nodes.statement_display:
If a formula \(\psi\) is obtained from a formula \(\varphi\) by finitely many
applications of rules of replacement, then \(\varphi\equiv\psi\).

nodes.name: Tautological Implication Gives a Valid Argument Form
nodes.statement_display:

\(\varphi\in\WFF\). If
\[
(\gamma_1\land\cdots\land\gamma_n)\to\varphi
\]
is a tautology, then the argument form \((\Gamma,\varphi)\) is valid.

\Longleftrightarrow
\forall\Delta\,\Bigl((\Delta\subseteq\Gamma\land \Delta\text{ is finite})
\Rightarrow \operatorname{SatisfiableSet}(\Delta)\Bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Representation of a Boolean Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(f:\mathbb{B}^n\to\mathbb{B}\) be a Boolean function. A formula
\(\varphi\in\WFF\) with variables among \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\) \emph{represents}
\(f\) if for every truth assignment \(v\),
\[
\widehat v(\varphi)=f(v(P_1),\ldots,v(P_n)).
\]

nodes.name: Connective Basis
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{connective basis} is a specified set \(B\) of propositional connectives
allowed as primitive connectives for building formulas.

nodes.name: Definable Connective
depends_on_titles: Truth Function Induced by a Formula
nodes.text_preview: Let \(B\) be a connective basis. A connective \(\circ\) is \emph{definable from} \(B\) if there is a formula built using only connectives from \(B\) whose truth function agrees with the truth function of \(\circ\).

nodes.name: Functionally Complete Connective Set
nodes.statement_display:
A connective basis \(B\) is \emph{functionally complete} if every Boolean
function \(f:\mathbb{B}^n\to\mathbb{B}\), for every \(n\ge 1\), is represented
by some formula using only connectives from \(B\).

nodes.text_preview: A connective basis \(B\) is \emph{functionally complete} if every Boolean function \(f:\mathbb{B}^n\to\mathbb{B}\), for every \(n\ge 1\), is represented by some formula using only connectives from \(B\).

nodes.name: Expressively Equivalent Connective Sets
nodes.statement_display:
Two connective bases \(B\) and \(C\) are \emph{expressively equivalent} if every
connective in \(B\) is definable from \(C\), and every connective in \(C\) is
definable from \(B\).
nodes.statement_tex:

connective in \(B\) is definable from \(C\), and every connective in \(C\) is
definable from \(B\).

nodes.name: Monotone Boolean Function
nodes.statement_display:
A Boolean function \(f:\mathbb{B}^n\to\mathbb{B}\) is \emph{monotone} if
changing input coordinates from \(\False\) to \(\True\) never changes the output
from \(\True\) to \(\False\).
nodes.statement_tex:

changing input coordinates from \(\False\) to \(\True\) never changes the output
from \(\True\) to \(\False\).

nodes.name: Minimal Complete Basis
nodes.statement_display:
A functionally complete connective basis \(B\) is \emph{minimal complete} if no
proper subset of \(B\) is functionally complete.
nodes.statement_tex:
A functionally complete connective basis \(B\) is \emph{minimal complete} if no
proper subset of \(B\) is functionally complete.

nodes.name: NAND Connective
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{NAND} connective, written \(\uparrow\), is the binary connective
defined by
\[
\varphi\uparrow\psi\equiv\neg(\varphi\land\psi).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
The \emph{NAND} connective, written \(\uparrow\), is the binary connective
defined by

\]

nodes.name: Connective Reduction
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{connective reduction} replaces a formula using one connective basis by
a logically equivalent formula using a smaller or different connective basis.

nodes.name: Truth-Table Row Conjunction
nodes.statement_display:
Fix a finite variable list \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\) and a row \(r\) of the
\hyperref[def:truth-table-propositional-logic]{truth table} for those
variables. The \emph{truth-table row conjunction} for \(r\) is the conjunction
\[
L_1\land L_2\land\cdots\land L_n,
\]
where
\[
L_i=
\begin{cases}
P_i, & \text{if row }r\text{ makes }P_i\text{ true},\\
\neg P_i, & \text{if row }r\text{ makes }P_i\text{ false}.
\end{cases}
\]

nodes.name: Truth-Table Row Clause
nodes.statement_display:
Fix a finite variable list \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\) and a row \(r\) of the
\hyperref[def:truth-table-propositional-logic]{truth table} for those
variables. The \emph{truth-table row clause} for \(r\) is the clause
\[
M_1\lor M_2\lor\cdots\lor M_n,
\]
where
\[
M_i=
\begin{cases}
P_i, & \text{if row }r\text{ makes }P_i\text{ false},\\
\neg P_i, & \text{if row }r\text{ makes }P_i\text{ true}.
\end{cases}
\]

nodes.name: Canonical Disjunctive Normal Form
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\), and fix an ordering \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\) of the finite
variable set of \(\varphi\). The \emph{canonical disjunctive normal form} of
\(\varphi\) relative to this ordering is the disjunction of the
\hyperref[def:truth-table-row-conjunction-propositional-logic]{truth-table row conjunctions}
corresponding to the rows on which \(\varphi\) is true.

nodes.name: Canonical Disjunctive Normal Form Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Every formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is
\hyperref[def:logical-equivalence-propositional-logic]{logically equivalent}
to its
\hyperref[def:canonical-disjunctive-normal-form-propositional-logic]{canonical disjunctive normal form}
relative to a fixed ordering of its finitely many variables.
\[
\varphi\equiv \operatorname{CDNF}_{P_1,\ldots,P_n}(\varphi).
\]

nodes.name: Elementary Disjunction
nodes.statement_display:
An \emph{elementary disjunction} is a finite disjunction of
\hyperref[def:literal-propositional-logic]{literals}. That is, it is a
\hyperref[def:clause-propositional-logic]{clause} of the form
\[
L_1\lor L_2\lor\cdots\lor L_k,
\]
where each \(L_i\) is a literal. The one-literal case is permitted.

nodes.name: Maxterm
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\) be a finite list of propositional variables. A
\emph{maxterm} relative to \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\) is an
\hyperref[def:elementary-disjunction-propositional-logic]{elementary disjunction}
that contains exactly one of \(P_i\) or \(\neg P_i\) for each
\(i=1,\ldots,n\).

\(i=1,\ldots,n\).

nodes.name: Conjunctive Normal Form
nodes.statement_display:
A formula is in \emph{conjunctive normal form}, abbreviated \emph{CNF}, when it
is a finite conjunction of
\hyperref[def:clause-propositional-logic]{clauses}. Equivalently, it has the
shape
\[
C_1\land C_2\land\cdots\land C_m,
\]
where each \(C_j\) is a clause.

nodes.name: Existence of Conjunctive Normal Form
nodes.statement_display:
For every formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\), there exists a formula
\(\psi\in\WFF\) such that \(\psi\) is in
\hyperref[def:conjunctive-normal-form-propositional-logic]{conjunctive normal form}
and
\[
\varphi\equiv\psi.
\]

\]

that contains exactly one of \(P_i\) or \(\neg P_i\) for each
\(i=1,\ldots,n\).

nodes.name: Disjunctive Normal Form
nodes.statement_display:
A formula is in \emph{disjunctive normal form}, abbreviated \emph{DNF}, when it
is a finite disjunction of
\hyperref[def:elementary-conjunction-propositional-logic]{elementary conjunctions}.
Equivalently, it has the shape
\[
C_1\lor C_2\lor\cdots\lor C_m,
\]
where each \(C_j\) is an elementary conjunction.

\]

nodes.name: DNF Satisfiability Criterion
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\psi\) be a formula in
\hyperref[def:disjunctive-normal-form-propositional-logic]{disjunctive normal form}.
Then \(\psi\) is
\hyperref[def:satisfiable-formula-propositional-logic]{satisfiable} if and only
if at least one conjunction term in \(\psi\) contains no
\hyperref[def:complementary-literals-propositional-logic]{complementary pair of literals}.

nodes.name: Literal
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{literal} is either a propositional variable \(P\) or the negation
\(\neg P\) of a propositional variable \(P\).
nodes.statement_tex:

A \emph{positive literal} is a literal of the form \(P\), where \(P\) is a
propositional variable.

A \emph{negative literal} is a literal of the form \(\neg P\), where \(P\) is a
propositional variable.

nodes.name: Complementary Literals
nodes.statement_display:
Two literals are \emph{complementary} if they are \(P\) and \(\neg P\) for the
same propositional variable \(P\).

nodes.name: Clause
nodes.statement_display: A \emph{clause} is a finite disjunction of literals.

A \emph{term}, also called a \emph{conjunction term}, is a finite conjunction
of literals.

nodes.name: Empty Clause
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{empty clause} is the empty disjunction of literals. Semantically, it
is read as false.

nodes.name: Empty Conjunction
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{empty conjunction} is the conjunction over no literals. Semantically,
it is read as true.

nodes.name: Negation Normal Form
nodes.statement_display:
A formula is in \emph{negation normal form} if it uses only propositional
variables, negated propositional variables, conjunction, and disjunction, and
every negation occurs immediately before a propositional variable.

nodes.name: Semantic Classification by Normal Forms
nodes.statement_display:
Normal forms give finite certificates for semantic classification. For any
formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\):
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \(\varphi\) is
  \hyperref[def:satisfiable-formula-propositional-logic]{satisfiable} if and
  only if some
  \hyperref[def:disjunctive-normal-form-propositional-logic]{DNF} formula
  equivalent to \(\varphi\) has a satisfiable conjunction term.
  \item \(\varphi\) is a
  \hyperref[def:contradiction-propositional-logic]{contradiction} if and only
  if some DNF formula equivalent to \(\varphi\) has no satisfiable conjunction
  term.
  \item \(\varphi\) is a
  \hyperref[def:tautology-propositional-logic]{tautology} if and only if some
  \hyperref[def:conjunctive-normal-form-propositional-logic]{CNF} formula
  equivalent to \(\varphi\) has no falsifiable clause.
  \item \(\varphi\) is a
  \hyperref[def:contingency-propositional-logic]{contingency} if and only if it
  is satisfiable and not a tautology.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Truth Assignment
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{truth assignment} is a function
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}.
\]
For each propositional variable \(P\in\Prop\), the value \(v(P)\) is the truth
value assigned to \(P\).

nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{domain} of a truth assignment
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}
\]
is the set \(\Prop\) of propositional variables.

nodes.name: Extension of a Truth Assignment to Formulas
nodes.statement_display:
Let
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}
\]
be a truth assignment. The \emph{extension} of \(v\) to well-formed formulas is
the function
\[
\widehat v:\WFF\to\mathbb{B}
\]
defined recursively as follows:
\[
\widehat v(P)=v(P)
\quad\text{for every }P\in\Prop,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\neg\varphi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\False,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\land\psi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\True
\text{ and }
\widehat v(\psi)=\True,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\lor\psi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\True
\text{ or }
\widehat v(\psi)=\True,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\to\psi)=\False
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\True
\text{ and }
\widehat v(\psi)=\False,
\]
and
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\leftrightarrow\psi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\widehat v(\psi).
\]

nodes.name: Satisfaction of a Formula
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(v\) be a truth assignment and let \(\varphi\in\WFF\). We say that \(v\) satisfies \(\varphi\), written
\[
v\models\varphi,
\]
if
\[
\widehat v(\varphi)=\mathrm{True}.
\]

nodes.name: Satisfaction of a Set of Formulas
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\Gamma\subseteq\WFF\). A truth assignment \(v\) satisfies \(\Gamma\), written
\[
v\models\Gamma,
\]
if \(v\models\gamma\) for every \(\gamma\in\Gamma\).

nodes.name: Truth Table
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\) and suppose the variables occurring in \(\varphi\) are among \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\). A truth table for \(\varphi\) is a finite table with one row for each assignment
\[
a:\{P_1,\ldots,P_n\}\to\mathbb{B}
\]
and a final column recording the value of \(\widehat v(\varphi)\) for any truth assignment \(v\) extending \(a\).

nodes.statement_display:
A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a tautology, written
\[
\models\varphi,
\]
if every truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).

nodes.name: Contradiction
nodes.statement_display: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contradiction if no truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).
nodes.statement_tex: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contradiction if no truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).
nodes.source_text: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contradiction if no truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).
nodes.text_preview: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contradiction if no truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).

nodes.name: Satisfiable Formula
nodes.statement_display: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is satisfiable if at least one truth assignment satisfies it.
nodes.statement_tex: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is satisfiable if at least one truth assignment satisfies it.
nodes.source_text: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is satisfiable if at least one truth assignment satisfies it.
nodes.text_preview: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is satisfiable if at least one truth assignment satisfies it.

nodes.name: Contingency
nodes.statement_display: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contingency if it is satisfiable but not a tautology.
nodes.statement_tex: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contingency if it is satisfiable but not a tautology.
nodes.source_text: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contingency if it is satisfiable but not a tautology.

nodes.name: Logical Consequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\Gamma\subseteq\WFF\) and \(\varphi\in\WFF\). The formula \(\varphi\) is a logical consequence of \(\Gamma\), written
\[
\Gamma\models\varphi,
\]
if every truth assignment satisfying \(\Gamma\) also satisfies \(\varphi\).
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Logical Equivalence
nodes.statement_display:
Formulas \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) are logically equivalent, written
\[
\varphi\equiv\psi,
\]
if they have the same truth value under every truth assignment.

nodes.name: Coincidence Lemma for Propositional Logic
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(v,w:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}\) be truth assignments. If \(v\) and \(w\) agree on every propositional variable occurring in \(\varphi\), then they assign the same truth value to \(\varphi\):
\[
v|_{\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)}
=
w|_{\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)}
\Longrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\widehat w(\varphi).
\]

nodes.name: Finite Truth-Table Theorem
nodes.statement_display: Every propositional formula has a finite truth table. More precisely, if \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)\) has \(n\) elements, then \(\varphi\) has a truth table with \(2^n\) rows.

An \(n\)-ary Boolean function is a function
\[
f:\mathbb{B}^n\to\mathbb{B}.
\]

nodes.name: Truth Function Induced by a Formula
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\), and suppose \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)\subseteq\{P_1,\ldots,P_n\}\). The truth function induced by \(\varphi\) is the Boolean function
\[
f_\varphi:\mathbb{B}^n\to\mathbb{B}
\]
defined by
\[
f_\varphi(b_1,\ldots,b_n)=\widehat v(\varphi),
\]
where \(v(P_i)=b_i\) for each \(i\).

nodes.name: Truth Value
nodes.statement_display:
The set of truth values is
\[
\mathbb{B} := \{\mathrm{False},\mathrm{True}\}.
\]
Some sources identify this set with \(\{0,1\}\), with \(0\) read as false and \(1\) read as true.

nodes.name: Propositional Language
nodes.text_preview: A propositional language consists of: \begin{enumerate} \item a set \(\Prop\) of propositional variables; \item logical connectives; \item punctuation symbols such as parentheses; \item recursive formation rules specifying the well-formed f

nodes.name: Propositional Variable
nodes.statement_display:
A propositional variable is an atomic symbol intended to stand for a proposition. A standard supply of variables is
\[
\Prop=\{P_1,P_2,P_3,\ldots\}.
\]
Informally, variables are often written \(P,Q,R,S,\ldots\).

nodes.name: Logical Connective
nodes.statement_display:
The standard primitive propositional connectives are
\[
\neg,\qquad \land,\qquad \lor,\qquad \to,\qquad \leftrightarrow.
\]
The connective \(\neg\) is unary. The connectives \(\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\) are binary.

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c}
\text{Symbol} & \text{Name} & \text{Arity} & \text{Formation pattern} & \text{Reading}\\
\hline
\neg & \text{negation} & \text{unary} & \neg\varphi & \text{not }\varphi\\
\land & \text{conjunction} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\land\psi) & \varphi\text{ and }\psi\\
\lor & \text{disjunction} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\lor\psi) & \varphi\text{ or }\psi\\
\to & \text{conditional} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\to\psi) & \text{if }\varphi,\text{ then }\psi\\
\leftrightarrow & \text{biconditional} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\leftrightarrow\psi) & \varphi\text{ iff }\psi
\end{array}
\]

nodes.name: Well-Formed Formula
nodes.statement_display:
The set \(\WFF\) of well-formed formulas is the smallest set of strings satisfying the following formation rules:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Every propositional variable \(P\in\Prop\) belongs to \(\WFF\).
\item If \(\varphi\in\WFF\), then \(\neg\varphi\in\WFF\).
\item If \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) and \(\circ\in\{\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\}\), then \((\varphi\circ\psi)\in\WFF\).
\item No string is a well-formed formula unless it is obtained by finitely many applications of the previous clauses.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Proper Subformula
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\). A \emph{proper subformula} of \(\varphi\) is a
formula \(\psi\) such that
\[
\psi\in\operatorname{Sub}(\varphi)
\quad\text{and}\quad
\psi\neq\varphi.
\]

nodes.name: Same Cardinality
nodes.statement_display:
Two sets $A$ and $B$ have the \emph{same cardinality}, written $A \sim B$,
if there exists a bijection $f : A \to B$.

nodes.name: Finite and Infinite Sets
nodes.statement_display:
A set $A$ is \emph{finite} if $A = \varnothing$ or $A \sim \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$
for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$. A set is \emph{infinite} if it is not finite.

nodes.name: Countable and Uncountable Sets
nodes.statement_display:
A set $A$ is \emph{countably infinite} if $A \sim \mathbb{N}$. A set is
\emph{countable} if it is finite or countably infinite. A set is
\emph{uncountable} if it is infinite but not countable.

nodes.name: Comparing Cardinalities

nodes.name: Cantor's Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
For any set $A$, there is no surjection $A \to \mathcal{P}(A)$. In
particular, $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$.

nodes.name: Function Space $B^A$
nodes.statement_display:
For sets $A$ and $B$, the \emph{function space} $B^A$ denotes the set of
all functions from $A$ to $B$:
\[
B^A \;:=\; \{\, f : A \to B \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Schröder--Bernstein Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
If $|A| \leq |B|$ and $|B| \leq |A|$, then $A \sim B$.

Equivalently: if there exist injections $f : A \hookrightarrow B$ and
$g : B \hookrightarrow A$, then there exists a bijection $h : A \to B$.

nodes.name: Equivalence Class
nodes.statement_display:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For $a \in A$, the
\emph{equivalence class} of $a$ is:
\[
[a]_R \;:=\; \{\, x \in A \mid (a,x) \in R \,\}.
\]
When $R$ is clear from context, we write $[a]$.

nodes.name: Quotient Set

\[
A / R \;:=\; \{\, [a]_R \mid a \in A \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Representative Independence Lemma
nodes.statement_display:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For any $a,b \in A$,
\[
[a] = [b]
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(a,b) \in R.
\]

nodes.name: Equivalence Relations and Partitions
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A$ be a set.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
\item If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation
$R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by
\[
(a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P
\]
is an equivalence relation on $A$.
\item These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.
\end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Universal Property of the Quotient Map
nodes.statement_display:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$, let $\pi : A \to A/R$ be the
canonical surjection, and let $f : A \to B$ be any function. Then $f$ is
\emph{constant on equivalence classes} — meaning $(a,b) \in R$ implies
$f(a) = f(b)$ — if and only if there exists a unique function
$\bar{f} : A/R \to B$ such that
\[
f = \bar{f} \circ \pi.
\]
When it exists, $\bar{f}$ is defined by $\bar{f}([a]) = f(a)$.

nodes.name: Cover
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A$ be a set and $\mathcal{C}$ a collection of sets.
$\mathcal{C}$ is a \emph{cover} of $A$ if every element of $A$ belongs to
at least one member of $\mathcal{C}$:
\[
A \;\subseteq\; \bigcup_{C \in \mathcal{C}} C.
\]

nodes.name: Subcover
nodes.statement_display:
$\mathcal{C}'$ is a \emph{subcover} of $\mathcal{C}$ if $\mathcal{C}'
\subseteq \mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{C}'$ is still a cover of $A$.

nodes.name: Finite Cover
nodes.statement_display:
A cover is \emph{finite} if it has finitely many members. A \emph{finite
subcover} is a subcover $\mathcal{C}' \subseteq \mathcal{C}$ with
$|\mathcal{C}'| < \infty$.

nodes.name: Open Cover
nodes.statement_display:
In a topological space $(X, \tau)$, an \emph{open cover} of a subset $A
\subseteq X$ is a cover $\mathcal{C}$ of $A$ all of whose members are open
sets: $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \tau$.

nodes.name: Finite Intersection Property
nodes.statement_display:
A collection $\mathcal{F}$ of sets has the \emph{finite intersection
property} (FIP) if every finite subcollection has nonempty intersection:
for all finite $\mathcal{F}' \subseteq \mathcal{F}$,
\[
\bigcap_{F \in \mathcal{F}'} F \;\neq\; \varnothing.
\]

nodes.name: FIP--Cover Duality
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X$ be a set, $A \subseteq X$, and $\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ a
family of subsets of $X$. Let $F_\alpha = U_\alpha^c = X \setminus U_\alpha$.
Then:
\[
\{U_\alpha\} \text{ covers } A
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
\{F_\alpha \cap A\}_{\alpha \in I} \text{ does \emph{not} have the FIP}.
\]
More precisely: the family $\{U_\alpha\}$ does \emph{not} cover $A$ if and
only if the family of closed complements $\{F_\alpha\}$ has the FIP relative
to $A$.

nodes.name: Indexed Family of Sets
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I$ be a set (the \emph{index set}) and $U$ a universe. An
\emph{indexed family of sets} is a function
\[
F : I \to \mathcal{P}(U),
\]
with $F(i)$ typically written $A_i$. The family is denoted $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$.

nodes.name: Indexed Union
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{union} of the indexed family $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is:
\[
\bigcup_{i \in I} A_i
\;:=\;
\{\, x \mid \exists i \in I \text{ such that } x \in A_i \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Indexed Intersection
nodes.statement_display:
For $I \neq \varnothing$, the \emph{intersection} of $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is:
\[
\bigcap_{i \in I} A_i
\;:=\;
\{\, x \mid \forall i \in I,\; x \in A_i \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Pairwise Disjoint Family
nodes.statement_display:
The family $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is \emph{pairwise disjoint} if
\[
\forall i,j \in I,\;
i \neq j \rightarrow A_i \cap A_j = \varnothing.
\]

i \neq j \rightarrow A_i \cap A_j = \varnothing.
\]

i \neq j \rightarrow A_i \cap A_j = \varnothing.
\]

nodes.name: Cover
nodes.statement_display:
A collection $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{P}(A)$ is a \emph{cover} of $A$ if
\[
\bigcup_{C \in \mathcal{C}} C = A.
\]

nodes.name: Arbitrary Cartesian Product
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ be an indexed family of sets. The \emph{Cartesian
product} is:
\[
\prod_{i \in I} A_i
\;:=\;
\Bigl\{
f : I \to \bigcup_{i \in I} A_i
\;\Bigm|\;
\forall i \in I,\; f(i) \in A_i
\Bigr\}.
\]

nodes.name: Composition
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$. The \emph{composition} $g \circ f : A \to C$ is:
\[
(g \circ f)(a) := g\bigl(f(a)\bigr)
\quad\text{for all } a \in A.
\]

nodes.name: Associativity of Composition
nodes.statement_display:
For $f : A \to B$, $g : B \to C$, $h : C \to D$:
\[
h \circ (g \circ f) = (h \circ g) \circ f.
\]

nodes.name: Identity and Composition
nodes.statement_display:
For any $f : A \to B$:
\[
f \circ \id_A = f
\quad\text{and}\quad
\id_B \circ f = f.
\]

nodes.name: Injectivity and Surjectivity Under Composition
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $f$ and $g$ are injective, then $g \circ f$ is injective.
\item If $f$ and $g$ are surjective, then $g \circ f$ is surjective.
\item If $g \circ f$ is injective, then $f$ is injective.
\item If $g \circ f$ is surjective, then $g$ is surjective.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Inverse Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. The \emph{inverse function} is
$f^{-1} : B \to A$ defined by: $f^{-1}(b) = a$ iff $f(a) = b$.

nodes.name: Characterization of Inverse Functions
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. Then
\[
f^{-1} \circ f = \id_A
\quad\text{and}\quad
f \circ f^{-1} = \id_B.
\]
Conversely, a function admits an inverse iff it is bijective.

nodes.name: Inverse of a Composition
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$ be bijective. Then $g \circ f$ is
bijective and
\[
(g \circ f)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}.
\]

nodes.name: Left and Right Inverses
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$.

A \emph{left inverse} of $f$ is a function $g : B \to A$ with $g \circ f = \id_A$.

A \emph{right inverse} (or \emph{section}) of $f$ is a function $h : B \to A$
with $f \circ h = \id_B$.

nodes.name: One-Sided Inverses and Function Properties
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f$ has a left inverse $\iff$ $f$ is injective (and $A \neq \varnothing$ or $A=B=\varnothing$).
\item $f$ has a right inverse $\iff$ $f$ is surjective.
\item If $f$ has both a left inverse $g$ and a right inverse $h$, then $g = h$
and $f$ is bijective.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Restriction and Extension
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $C \subseteq A$. The \emph{restriction} of $f$ to $C$
is $f|_C : C \to B$, $f|_C(a) = f(a)$ for all $a \in C$.

Let $A \subseteq A'$, and let $f : A \to B$. A function $g : A' \to B$ is an
\emph{extension} of $f$ if $g|_A = f$.

nodes.name: Preimages Preserve Set Operations
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq B$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f^{-1}(S \cup T) = f^{-1}(S) \cup f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S \cap T) = f^{-1}(S) \cap f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S \setminus T) = f^{-1}(S) \setminus f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S^c) = (f^{-1}(S))^c$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Images and Set Operations
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq A$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f(S \cup T) = f(S) \cup f(T)$,
\item $f(S \cap T) \subseteq f(S) \cap f(T)$,
\item $f(S \setminus T) \supseteq f(S) \setminus f(T)$.
\end{enumerate}
Equality holds in (ii) and (iii) for all $S,T$ iff $f$ is injective.

nodes.name: Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation
$f \subseteq A \times B$ such that:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$
  with $(a,b) \in f$;
\item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$
  then $b_1 = b_2$.
\end{enumerate}
If $(a,b) \in f$ we write $f(a) = b$.

nodes.name: Domain and Codomain
nodes.statement_display:
If $f$ is a function from $A$ to $B$, we write $f : A \to B$, where $A$ is
the \emph{domain} $\dom(f)$ and $B$ the \emph{codomain} $\cod(f)$.

nodes.name: Image of a Function
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{image} (or \emph{range}) of $f : A \to B$ is:
\[
\im(f) \;:=\; \{\, b \in B \mid \exists a \in A,\ f(a)=b \,\}.
\]
The image may be a proper subset of the codomain.

nodes.name: Image of a Set
nodes.statement_display:
For $S \subseteq A$, the \emph{image of $S$ under $f$} is:
\[
f(S) \;:=\; \{\, f(a) \mid a \in S \,\}.
\]
Note $f(A) = \im(f)$.

\[
f^{-1}(T) \;:=\; \{\, a \in A \mid f(a) \in T \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Fiber
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{fiber} of $f$ over $b \in B$ is the preimage of the singleton:
\[
f^{-1}(\{b\}) \;=\; \{\, a \in A \mid f(a) = b \,\}.
\]

nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{graph} of $f : A \to B$ is:
\[
\operatorname{Graph}(f)
\;:=\;
\{\, (a,b) \in A \times B \mid b = f(a) \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Injective Function
nodes.statement_display:
$f : A \to B$ is \emph{injective} (one-to-one) if distinct elements of $A$
have distinct images:
\[
\forall a_1,a_2 \in A,\;
f(a_1)=f(a_2) \;\Longrightarrow\; a_1=a_2.
\]

nodes.name: Surjective Function
nodes.statement_display:
$f : A \to B$ is \emph{surjective} (onto) if every element of $B$ is achieved:
\[
\forall b \in B,\; \exists a \in A \text{ such that } f(a)=b.
\]
Equivalently, $\im(f) = B$.

nodes.name: Bijective Function
nodes.statement_display: $f : A \to B$ is \emph{bijective} if it is both injective and surjective.

nodes.name: Identity and Inclusion
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{identity function} on $A$ is $\id_A : A \to A$, $\id_A(a) = a$.

If $A \subseteq B$, the \emph{inclusion map} is $\iota : A \hookrightarrow B$,
$\iota(a) = a$.

nodes.name: Constant Function
nodes.statement_display:
$f : A \to B$ is \emph{constant} if there exists $b_0 \in B$ with
$f(a) = b_0$ for all $a \in A$.

nodes.name: Symmetric and Asymmetric Parts
nodes.text_preview: For any binary relation $R$ on $X$, define: \begin{itemize} \item the \emph{symmetric part}: $xIy \iff xRy \text{ and } yRx$ \item the \emph{asymmetric part}: $xPy \iff xRy \text{ but not } yRx$ \end{itemize} Note that $R = P \cup I$.

nodes.name: Maximal Elements and Upper Bounds
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\succsim$ be a binary relation on $X$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item The set of \emph{maximal elements} of $X$ is
    \[
      \mathrm{Max}(X, \succsim)
      = \{ x \in X \mid y \succsim x \text{ for no } y \in X \text{ with }
          y \neq x \}.
    \]
  \item The set of \emph{upper bounds} of $X$ is
    \[
      M(X, \succsim)
      = \{ x \in X \mid x \succsim y \text{ for all } y \in X \}.
    \]
\end{itemize}

nodes.name: Transitive Closure
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{transitive closure} $T(R)$ of a binary relation $R$ on $X$ is the
smallest transitive relation containing $R$: that is, $T(R)$ is transitive,
$xRy \Rightarrow xT(R)y$, and no strictly smaller relation is both transitive
and contains $R$.

and contains $R$.

nodes.name: Extension of a Preorder
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\succsim$ be a preorder on $X$. A preorder $\succsim'$ is an
\emph{extension} of $\succsim$ if
\[
  x \succsim y \;\Rightarrow\; x \succsim' y,
  \qquad
  x \succ y \;\Rightarrow\; x \succ' y.
\]
A \emph{complete extension} is an extension that is also complete.

A \emph{complete extension} is an extension that is also complete.

nodes.name: Complete preorder extension
nodes.statement_display:
For any nonempty set $X$ and preorder $\succsim$ on $X$, there exists a
complete preorder that is an extension of $\succsim$.

nodes.name: and Proposition (Only Weak Cycles)
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\succsim$ be a binary relation on $X$ with asymmetric part $\succ$ and
transitive closure $T(\succsim)$. We say $\succsim$ satisfies \emph{only weak
cycles} (OWC) if
\[
  xT(\succsim)y \;\Rightarrow\; \lnot(y \succ x).
\]

\textbf{Proposition.} A binary relation $\succsim$ on a nonempty
set $X$ can be extended to a complete preorder if and only if it satisfies
OWC.

nodes.name: Cover Relation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(A, \leq)$ be a poset and $a, b \in A$.  We say $b$ \emph{covers} $a$,
written $a \lessdot b$, if $a < b$ and there is no $c \in A$ with
$a < c < b$.

$a < c < b$.

nodes.name: Hasse Diagram
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{Hasse diagram} of a finite poset $(A, \leq)$ is the directed graph
whose vertices are the elements of $A$, with an upward edge from $a$ to $b$
whenever $a \lessdot b$.  Three graphical conventions apply:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item Reflexive loops ($a \leq a$) are suppressed.
  \item Edges implied by transitivity are suppressed: if $a \lessdot b$ and
        $b \lessdot c$, no direct edge from $a$ to $c$ is drawn.
  \item Direction is encoded by height: $a \lessdot b$ is drawn with $b$
        strictly above $a$, so arrowheads are unnecessary.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Dual Poset
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(A, \leq)$ be a poset.  The \emph{dual poset} is $(A, \geq)$, where
$a \geq b$ is defined to mean $b \leq a$.  The dual is also written
$(A, \leq^{\mathrm{op}})$.

$a \geq b$ is defined to mean $b \leq a$.  The dual is also written
$(A, \leq^{\mathrm{op}})$.

nodes.name: Order Duality
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\Phi$ be any first-order statement about a poset $(A, \leq)$ expressed
using only the relation $\leq$.  Let $\Phi^*$ be the statement obtained from
$\Phi$ by replacing every occurrence of $\leq$ with $\geq$ (equivalently,
working in the dual poset).  Then:
\[
  (A, \leq) \models \Phi
  \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
  (A, \geq) \models \Phi.
\]
In particular, if $\Phi$ is a theorem about all posets, then so is $\Phi^*$.

nodes.name: Induced Order
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(B, \leq')$ be a partially ordered set and let $f : A \to B$ be a
function. The \emph{order induced by $f$} (or \emph{pullback order along
$f$}) is the relation $\leq_f$ on $A$ defined by:
\[
x \;\leq_f\; y
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
f(x) \;\leq'\; f(y).
\]

nodes.name: Induced Preorder
nodes.statement_display:
For any function $f : A \to B$ and partial order $(B, \leq')$, the relation
$\leq_f$ is a preorder on $A$: it is reflexive and transitive.

nodes.name: $f$-Indistinguishable Elements
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $\leq_f$ be the induced order. Two elements
$x, y \in A$ are \emph{$f$-indistinguishable}, written $x \sim_f y$,
if both $x \leq_f y$ and $y \leq_f x$, i.e.\ if
\[
f(x) \leq' f(y) \quad \text{and} \quad f(y) \leq' f(x).
\]

nodes.name: Induced Poset
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(B, \leq')$ be a partially ordered set and $f : A \to B$.
The induced order $\leq_f$ is a partial order on $A$ if and only if
$f$ is injective.

nodes.name: Suborder / Restriction
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(B, \leq')$ be a partially ordered set and $S \subseteq B$. The
\emph{suborder} on $S$ (or \emph{induced order on $S$}) is the relation
$\leq'_S$ defined by:
\[
x \;\leq'_S\; y
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
x \leq' y,
\quad \text{for } x, y \in S.
\]

nodes.name: Order Embedding
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(A, \leq)$ and $(B, \leq')$ be partially ordered sets. A function
$f : A \to B$ is an \emph{order embedding} if for all $x, y \in A$:
\[
x \;\leq\; y
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
f(x) \;\leq'\; f(y).
\]

nodes.name: Order embedding is isomorphism onto image
nodes.statement_display:
If $f : (A, \leq) \to (B, \leq')$ is an order embedding, then $f$ is an
order isomorphism from $(A, \leq)$ to the suborder $(f(A), \leq'_{f(A)})$.

nodes.name: Lattice
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{lattice} is a poset $(L, \leq)$ in which every two-element subset
$\{a, b\} \subseteq L$ has both a supremum and an infimum in $L$.

The supremum $\sup\{a,b\}$ is called the \emph{join} of $a$ and $b$,
written $a \vee b$.
The infimum $\inf\{a,b\}$ is called the \emph{meet} of $a$ and $b$,
written $a \wedge b$.

nodes.name: Complete Lattice
nodes.statement_display:
A lattice $(L, \leq)$ is a \emph{complete lattice} if every subset
$S \subseteq L$ (including the empty set and $L$ itself) has both a
supremum and an infimum in $L$:
\[
  \forall S \subseteq L,\quad \sup S \in L \;\text{ and }\; \inf S \in L.
\]

nodes.name: Distributive Lattice
nodes.statement_display:
A lattice $(L, \leq)$ is \emph{distributive} if for all $a, b, c \in L$:
\[
  a \wedge (b \vee c) = (a \wedge b) \vee (a \wedge c).
\]

\[
  a \wedge (b \vee c) = (a \wedge b) \vee (a \wedge c).
\]

nodes.name: Complement and Boolean Lattice
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(L, \leq)$ be a bounded distributive lattice with top element $\top$ and
bottom element $\bot$.  An element $a' \in L$ is a \emph{complement} of
$a \in L$ if
\[
  a \vee a' = \top \quad \text{and} \quad a \wedge a' = \bot.
\]
A \emph{Boolean lattice} (or \emph{Boolean algebra}) is a bounded distributive
lattice in which every element has a complement.

nodes.name: Sup Unique
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(A, \leq)$ be a poset and $S \subseteq A$.  If $\sup S$ exists, it is
unique.  If $\inf S$ exists, it is unique.

nodes.name: Sup Char
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(A, \leq)$ be a poset, $S \subseteq A$, and $u^* \in A$.  Then
$u^* = \sup S$ if and only if:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item $u^*$ is an upper bound of $S$; and
  \item for every $v \in A$ with $v < u^*$, there exists $s \in S$ with
        $v < s$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Sup Inf Duality
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(A, \leq)$ be a poset and $S \subseteq A$.  Then
$\inf_{(A,\leq)} S = \sup_{(A,\geq)} S$,
where $(A,\geq)$ denotes the dual poset.

In particular: if every nonempty bounded-above subset of $(A,\leq)$ has a
supremum, then every nonempty bounded-below subset of $(A,\leq)$ has an
infimum.

nodes.name: Ordered Set
nodes.statement_display:
An \emph{ordered set} (or \emph{partially ordered set}, \emph{poset}) is a
pair $(A,\leq)$ where $\leq$ is a partial order on $A$: a relation that is
reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive.

pair $(A,\leq)$ where $\leq$ is a partial order on $A$: a relation that is
reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive.

nodes.name: Strict Order
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(A,\leq)$ be an ordered set. The \emph{strict order} $<$ is defined by:
\[
a < b \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; (a \leq b \land a \neq b).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Comparable and Incomparable Elements
nodes.statement_display:
In an ordered set $(A,\leq)$, elements $a,b \in A$ are \emph{comparable} if
$a \leq b$ or $b \leq a$; they are \emph{incomparable} if neither holds.

nodes.name: Total / Linear Order
nodes.statement_display:
An ordered set $(A,\leq)$ is a \emph{total order} (or \emph{linear order})
if every pair of elements is comparable:
\[
\forall a,b \in A,\; a \leq b \lor b \leq a.
\]

if every pair of elements is comparable:

nodes.name: Upper and Lower Bounds
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(A,\leq)$ be an ordered set and $S \subseteq A$.

An element $u \in A$ is an \emph{upper bound} of $S$ if $\forall s \in S,\; s \leq u$.

An element $\ell \in A$ is a \emph{lower bound} of $S$ if $\forall s \in S,\; \ell \leq s$.

nodes.name: Well-Ordered Set
nodes.statement_display:
An ordered set $(A,<)$ is \emph{well-ordered} if every nonempty subset
$S \subseteq A$ has a least element:
\[
\forall S \subseteq A,\;
\bigl(S \neq \varnothing \;\Rightarrow\; \exists m \in S \text{ s.t.\ }
m \leq s \;\forall s \in S\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Chain and Antichain
nodes.statement_display:
A subset $C \subseteq A$ of a poset $(A,\leq)$ is a \emph{chain} if every
pair of elements in $C$ is comparable. It is an \emph{antichain} if no two
distinct elements of $C$ are comparable.

pair of elements in $C$ is comparable. It is an \emph{antichain} if no two
distinct elements of $C$ are comparable.

nodes.name: Initial Segment
nodes.statement_display:
A subset $I \subseteq A$ is an \emph{initial segment} of $(A,\leq)$ if
\[
a \in I \text{ and } b \leq a \;\Rightarrow\; b \in I.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Directed Set
nodes.statement_display:
A preorder $(I, \leq)$ is \emph{directed} (or a \emph{directed set}) if every
finite subset of $I$ has an upper bound in $I$: for all $i, j \in I$ there
exists $k \in I$ with $i \leq k$ and $j \leq k$.
nodes.statement_tex:

finite subset of $I$ has an upper bound in $I$: for all $i, j \in I$ there
exists $k \in I$ with $i \leq k$ and $j \leq k$.

nodes.name: Net
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(I, \leq)$ be a directed set and $X$ a set. A \emph{net} in $X$ is a
function $x : I \to X$. The value at $i \in I$ is written $x_i$, and the
net is written $(x_i)_{i \in I}$.

nodes.name: Reflexive Relation
nodes.statement_display:
A binary relation $R\subseteq A\times A$ is \emph{reflexive} on $A$ if every
element of the domain is related to itself:
\[
  \forall a\in A,\ (a,a)\in R.
\]
In infix notation, this is the condition $\forall a\in A,\ aRa$.

nodes.name: Irreflexive Relation
nodes.statement_display:
A binary relation $R\subseteq A\times A$ is \emph{irreflexive} on $A$ if no
element of the domain is related to itself:
\[
  \forall a\in A,\ (a,a)\notin R.
\]
In infix notation, this is the condition $\forall a\in A,\ \neg(aRa)$.

nodes.name: Symmetric Relation
nodes.statement_display:
A binary relation $R\subseteq A\times A$ is \emph{symmetric} on $A$ if every
related pair also appears in the reverse order:
\[
  \forall a,b\in A,\ (a,b)\in R \rightarrow (b,a)\in R.
\]
In infix notation, this is $\forall a,b\in A,\ aRb\rightarrow bRa$.

nodes.name: Antisymmetric Relation
nodes.statement_display:
A binary relation $R\subseteq A\times A$ is \emph{antisymmetric} on $A$ if any
two elements related in both directions must be equal:
\[
  \forall a,b\in A,\;
  \bigl((a,b)\in R\land(b,a)\in R\bigr)\rightarrow a=b.
\]
In infix notation, this is
$\forall a,b\in A,\ ((aRb\land bRa)\rightarrow a=b)$.

nodes.name: Asymmetric Relation
nodes.statement_display:
A binary relation $R\subseteq A\times A$ is \emph{asymmetric} on $A$ if every
related pair excludes the reverse pair:
\[
  \forall a,b\in A,\;
  (a,b)\in R \rightarrow (b,a)\notin R.
\]
In infix notation, this is

nodes.statement_tex:
A binary relation $R\subseteq A\times A$ is \emph{asymmetric} on $A$ if every
related pair excludes the reverse pair:
\[
  \forall a,b\in A,\;
  (a,b)\in R \rightarrow (b,a)\notin R.
\]
In infix notation, this is
$\forall a,b\in A,\ (aRb\rightarrow\neg(bRa))$.

nodes.name: Transitive Relation
nodes.statement_display:
A binary relation $R\subseteq A\times A$ is \emph{transitive} on $A$ if any
two composable related pairs collapse to a direct related pair:
\[
  \forall a,b,c\in A,\;
  \bigl((a,b)\in R\land(b,c)\in R\bigr)\rightarrow(a,c)\in R.
\]
In infix notation, this is
$\forall a,b,c\in A,\ ((aRb\land bRc)\rightarrow aRc)$.

(a,b) \in R \lor (b,a) \in R.

nodes.name: Ordered Pair
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a$ and $b$ be sets. The \emph{ordered pair} $(a,b)$ is defined (Kuratowski)
as
\[
(a,b) \;:=\; \{\{a\}, \{a,b\}\}.
\]

nodes.name: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is:
\[
A \times B
\;:=\;
\{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Relation
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{relation} from $A$ to $B$ is any subset $R \subseteq A \times B$.

If $(a,b) \in R$ we write $a \, R \, b$ and say ``$a$ is related to $b$.''
When $A = B$ we say $R$ is a \emph{relation on $A$}.

nodes.name: Topology
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{topology} on a set $X$ is a collection $\tau$ of subsets of $X$
satisfying:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $\varnothing \in \tau$ and $X \in \tau$;
\item if $\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ is any family of sets in $\tau$ (indexed
  by any set $I$), then $\bigcup_{\alpha \in I} U_\alpha \in \tau$;
\item if $U_1, \dots, U_n \in \tau$ (finitely many), then $U_1 \cap \cdots
  \cap U_n \in \tau$.
\end{enumerate}
The pair $(X, \tau)$ is a \emph{topological space}. Members of $\tau$ are
called \emph{open sets}.

nodes.name: Intersection of $\sigma$-algebras is a $\sigma$-algebra
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\{\mathcal{M}_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ be any family of $\sigma$-algebras
on $X$ (indexed by any set $I$). Then
\[
\mathcal{M} \;:=\; \bigcap_{\alpha \in I} \mathcal{M}_\alpha
\]
is a $\sigma$-algebra on $X$.

nodes.name: Generated $\sigma$-Algebra
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{F}$ be any collection of subsets of $X$. The
\emph{$\sigma$-algebra generated by $\mathcal{F}$}, written $\sigma(\mathcal{F})$,
is the smallest $\sigma$-algebra on $X$ that contains $\mathcal{F}$:
\[
\sigma(\mathcal{F})
\;:=\;
\bigcap\,\bigl\{\, \mathcal{M} \;\bigm|\;
\mathcal{M} \text{ is a $\sigma$-algebra on } X
\text{ and } \mathcal{F} \subseteq \mathcal{M} \,\bigr\}.
\]
The collection $\mathcal{F}$ is called a \emph{generating family} for
$\sigma(\mathcal{F})$.

nodes.name: Borel $\sigma$-Algebra
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(X, \tau)$ be a topological space. The \emph{Borel $\sigma$-algebra}
on $X$, written $\mathcal{B}(X)$, is the $\sigma$-algebra generated by the
open sets:
\[
\mathcal{B}(X) \;:=\; \sigma(\tau).
\]
Members of $\mathcal{B}(X)$ are called \emph{Borel sets}.

nodes.name: Axiom of Extensionality
nodes.statement_display:
Two sets are equal iff they have the same elements.
\[
\forall A \, \forall B \,
\Bigl( A = B \iff \forall x \, (x \in A \leftrightarrow x \in B) \Bigr).
\]
