There exists a set with no elements.

\exists A \, \forall x \, (x \notin A).

nodes.name: Axiom of Pairing
nodes.statement_display:
For any two sets, there exists a set containing exactly those two sets.
\[
\forall A \, \forall B \, \exists C \,
\forall x \, (x \in C \leftrightarrow (x = A \lor x = B)).
\]

nodes.name: Axiom of Union
nodes.statement_display:
For any family of sets, there exists a set containing exactly the elements of
those sets.
\[
\forall A \, \exists U \,
\forall x \,
\bigl( x \in U \leftrightarrow \exists B \, (B \in A \land x \in B) \bigr).
\]

nodes.statement_display:
For any set, there exists a set of all its subsets.
\[
\forall A \, \exists P \,
\forall x \, (x \in P \leftrightarrow x \subseteq A).
\]

\]

nodes.name: Axiom Schema of Separation
nodes.statement_display:
Given a set and a property, there exists a subset containing exactly the
elements satisfying that property. For any formula $\varphi(x)$,
\[
\forall A \, \exists B \,
\forall x \, (x \in B \leftrightarrow (x \in A \land \varphi(x))).
\]

nodes.name: Axiom Schema of Replacement
nodes.statement_display:
If $\varphi(x,y)$ defines a functional relation on a set $A$, then the image
of $A$ under $\varphi$ is a set. For any formula $\varphi(x,y)$,
\[
\forall A\,
\Bigl(
\bigl(\forall x \in A\, \exists! y\, \varphi(x,y)\bigr)
\rightarrow
\exists B\, \forall y\,
\bigl(y \in B \leftrightarrow \exists x \in A\, \varphi(x,y)\bigr)
\Bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Axiom of Foundation
nodes.statement_display:
Every nonempty set has an $\in$-minimal element.
\[
\forall A \,
\Bigl( A \neq \varnothing \rightarrow
\exists x \in A \, (x \cap A = \varnothing) \Bigr).
\]

Every nonempty set has an $\in$-minimal element.
\[
\forall A \,
\Bigl( A \neq \varnothing \rightarrow
\exists x \in A \, (x \cap A = \varnothing) \Bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Axiom of Choice
nodes.statement_display:
For any family of nonempty sets, there exists a selection function.
\[
\forall A \,
\Bigl(
(\forall B \in A \, (B \neq \varnothing))
\rightarrow
\exists f \,
\forall B \in A \, (f(B) \in B)
\Bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Set and Membership
nodes.statement_display:
In axiomatic set theory, the notions of \emph{set} and \emph{membership} are
\emph{primitive}.

\begin{itemize}
  \item A \emph{set} is an object.
  \item \emph{Membership} is a binary relation, denoted by $\in$, between objects.
\end{itemize}

If $x$ is an object and $A$ is a set, the statement $x \in A$ is read as
``$x$ is an element of $A$.'' No definition of ``set'' or ``$\in$'' is given
in more basic terms. Their meaning is determined entirely by the axioms
governing them.

nodes.name: Commutativity of Union and Intersection
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A,B$ be sets. Then
\[
A \cup B = B \cup A
\quad\text{and}\quad
A \cap B = B \cap A.
\]

nodes.name: Associativity of Union and Intersection
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A,B,C$ be sets. Then
\[
(A \cup B) \cup C = A \cup (B \cup C)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(A \cap B) \cap C = A \cap (B \cap C).
\]

nodes.name: Distributive Laws
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A,B,C$ be sets. Then
\[
A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C),
\]
\[
A \cup (B \cap C) = (A \cup B) \cap (A \cup C).
\]

nodes.name: Identity and Absorption Laws
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A,B$ be sets and $U$ a universe with $A \subseteq U$. Then
\[
A \cup \varnothing = A,
\qquad
A \cap U = A,
\]
\[
A \cup (A \cap B) = A,
\qquad
A \cap (A \cup B) = A.
\]

nodes.name: Subset
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. We say $A$ is a \emph{subset} of $B$, written
$A \subseteq B$, if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$:
\[
A \subseteq B \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall x \, (x \in A \rightarrow x \in B).
\]

$A \subseteq B$ and $A \neq B$.

nodes.name: Set Equality
nodes.statement_display:
Two sets $A$ and $B$ are \emph{equal}, written $A = B$, if they have the same
elements:
\[
A = B \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall x \, (x \in A \leftrightarrow x \in B).
\]
Equivalently, $A = B \iff (A \subseteq B \land B \subseteq A)$.

nodes.name: Union
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{union} of $A$ and $B$, denoted $A \cup B$, is the set of all
elements belonging to at least one of the two sets:
\[
A \cup B \;=\; \{\, x \mid x \in A \lor x \in B \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Intersection
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{intersection} of $A$ and $B$, denoted $A \cap B$, is the set of all
elements common to both:
\[
A \cap B \;=\; \{\, x \mid x \in A \land x \in B \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Set Difference
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{set difference} $A \setminus B$ is the set of elements in $A$ but
not in $B$:
\[
A \setminus B \;=\; \{\, x \mid x \in A \land x \notin B \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Symmetric Difference
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{symmetric difference} $A \triangle B$ is the set of elements
belonging to exactly one of $A$ and $B$:
\[
A \triangle B
\;=\;
(A \setminus B) \cup (B \setminus A)
\;=\;
\{\, x \mid (x \in A \lor x \in B) \land x \notin A \cap B \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Complement

relative to $U$, denoted $A^c$, is:
\[
A^c \;=\; U \setminus A.
\]

nodes.name: Cartesian Product
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is:
\[
A \times B = \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Power Set
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{power set} of $A$, denoted $\mathcal{P}(A)$, is the set of all
subsets of $A$:
\[
\mathcal{P}(A) \;:=\; \{\, S \mid S \subseteq A \,\}.
\]

nodes.name: De Morgan's Laws
nodes.statement_display:
Let $U$ be a universe and $A,B \subseteq U$. Then
\[
(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c
\quad\text{and}\quad
(A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c.
\]

nodes.name: Set Duality
nodes.statement_display:
Two set-theoretic expressions over a fixed universe $U$ are \emph{dual} if one
is obtained from the other by simultaneously replacing
$\cup \leftrightarrow \cap$ and $\varnothing \leftrightarrow U$, with
complements unchanged.

all subsets of a universe remains valid when each operation and constant is
replaced by its dual.

x\otimes(y\oplus z)=(x\otimes y)\oplus(x\otimes z),

nodes.name: Ordered Field Trichotomy
nodes.statement_display:
In an ordered field, for all elements $a$ and $b$, exactly one of the following
holds:
\[
a=b,\qquad a<b,\qquad b<a.
\]

nodes.name: Ordered Field Multiplication Law
nodes.statement_display:
In an ordered field, for all elements $a$, $b$, and $c$,
\[
c>0 \Longrightarrow
\bigl(ac<bc \Longleftrightarrow a<b\bigr),
\]
and
\[
c<0 \Longrightarrow
\bigl(ac<bc \Longleftrightarrow b<a\bigr).
\]
Moreover, positive elements are closed under multiplication:
\[
0<a \land 0<b \Longrightarrow 0<ab.
\]

nodes.name: Positive cone
nodes.statement_display:
Let $F$ be a field. A subset $P\subset F$ is called a \emph{positive
cone} if it satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item if $a,b\in P$, then $a+b\in P$;
  \item if $a,b\in P$, then $ab\in P$;
  \item for every $a\in F$, exactly one of the following holds:
  \[
  a\in P,
  \qquad
  a=0,
  \qquad
  -a\in P.
  \]
\end{enumerate}
Define the order relation by
\[
a<b \iff b-a\in P.
\]

nodes.name: Operation Well-Definedness
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. A binary operation $O$ respects
$\sim$ if
\[
a\sim a' \land b\sim b' \Longrightarrow
a\mathbin{O}b \sim a'\mathbin{O}b'.
\]

nodes.name: Field Integer Power
nodes.statement_display:

\[
a^n:=(a^{-1})^{-n}.
\]

nodes.name: Trichotomy
nodes.statement_display:
The relation $<$ satisfies \emph{trichotomy} if exactly one of
\[
x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x
\]
holds for every pair $x,y$.

nodes.name: Homomorphism
nodes.statement_display:
A map $f$ is a \emph{homomorphism} for an operation $O$ when
\[
f(x\mathbin{O}y)=f(x)\mathbin{O'}f(y).
\]
For ring-like structures it preserves both addition and multiplication, and
preserves $1$ when the language includes a distinguished multiplicative
identity.

a=c\ \text{ and }\ b=d.
\]

\]

and multiplicative identity \([1]_n\).

nodes.name: \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) Has Zero Divisors
nodes.statement_display:
In \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\), the nonzero class \([2]_4\) satisfies
\[
[2]_4\cdot[2]_4=[0]_4.
\]
Therefore \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) is not a field.

nodes.name: Number-System Construction Target
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{number-system construction target} is the specified algebraic and
order structure that a proposed system is meant to satisfy. The target fixes
the required carrier, operations, relations, distinguished elements, and laws.

nodes.name: Construction Data

objects, the intended equality or equivalence relation, the distinguished
elements, and the operation formulas from which the system is built.

nodes.name: Construction Verification
nodes.statement_display:
\emph{Construction verification} is the proof that the construction data
actually satisfies the construction target. It includes, as applicable,
closure of operations, equivalence-relation proofs, well-definedness of
operations on classes, algebraic laws, order laws, and compatibility of
embeddings with earlier systems.

nodes.name: Addition on the Two-Element System

\[
\begin{array}{c|cc}
+_2 & 0 & 1\\ \hline
0 & 0 & 1\\
1 & 1 & 0
\end{array}
\]

nodes.name: \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\) Is the Two-Element System

classes, \([0]_2\) and \([1]_2\), and its addition and multiplication tables
are the tables of \(\mathbb{F}_2\).

nodes.name: Compatibility of the Embedding Chain
nodes.statement_display: Let $\iota_{1}:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{W}$, $\iota_{2}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{Z}$, $\iota_{3}:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Q}$, and $\iota_{4}:\mathbb{Q}\to\mathbb{R}$ be the canonical embeddings. For every $n\in\mathbb{N}$ the composite $(\iota_{4}\circ\iota_{3}\circ\iota_{2}\circ\iota_{1})(n)$ is the real number identified with $n$, and the composite preserves the arithmetic and order carried by the source system. Hence arithmetic computed in any system agrees with arithmetic on its image in $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Structure-Preserving Map
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A$ and $B$ be ordered arithmetic systems with a specified common source
structure: selected constants, selected operations, and a strict order. A map
$\varphi:A\to B$ is \emph{structure-preserving} exactly when it preserves each
selected constant and, for every selected binary operation $\star$ and all
$a,a'\in A$,
\[
\varphi(a\star_A a')=\varphi(a)\star_B\varphi(a'),
\]
and $a<_A a' \Longrightarrow \varphi(a)<_B\varphi(a')$.

nodes.name: Image of an Embedding
nodes.statement_display: Let $\varphi:A\to B$ be an embedding. The \emph{image} of $\varphi$ is the set $\varphi(A)=\{\,\varphi(a) : a\in A\,\}\subseteq B$, equipped with the selected constants, operations, and order of $B$ restricted to $\varphi(A)$.

nodes.name: Embeddings Identify a System with Its Image
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\varphi:A\to B$ be an embedding of ordered arithmetic systems relative to
a selected source structure. Then the corestriction $\varphi:A\to\varphi(A)$
is an isomorphism onto its image: it is a bijection that preserves and reflects
the selected constants, operations, and order. Consequently $A$ may be
identified with the corresponding substructure $\varphi(A)\subseteq B$.

nodes.name: Identity
nodes.statement_display:
Two expressions $x$ and $y$ are \emph{identical} when they denote the same
object.
nodes.statement_tex:
Two expressions $x$ and $y$ are \emph{identical} when they denote the same

nodes.name: Leibniz's Law
nodes.statement_display:
Identical objects have exactly the same properties.
\[
\forall x\,\forall y\,
\Bigl(x=y \Longrightarrow \forall P\,\bigl(P(x)\Longleftrightarrow P(y)\bigr)\Bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Equality on a Domain
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A$ be a domain. \emph{Equality on $A$} is the relation, written $=$,
that holds exactly when two terms or constructions denote the same element of
$A$.

nodes.statement_display:
\emph{Definitional equality} records an introduced meaning, written with
$:=$. \emph{Propositional equality} is a statement of sameness inside the
theory, written with $=$.

nodes.name: Equivalence Relation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A$ be a set. A relation $\sim$ on $A$ is an \emph{equivalence relation}
if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.

nodes.name: Equality Is the Finest Equivalence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For all $a,b\in A$,
\[
a=b \Longrightarrow a\sim b.
\]

nodes.name: Equivalence Class
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. The \emph{equivalence class}
of $a\in A$ is
\[
[a] := \{x\in A \mid x\sim a\}.
\]

$A$, and are pairwise disjoint.

nodes.name: Canonical Projection
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. The \emph{canonical projection}
is the map
\[
\pi:A\to A/{\sim},
\qquad
\pi(a):=[a].
\]

nodes.statement_display:

nodes.name: Existence of a Quotient
nodes.statement_display:
For any equivalence relation $\sim$ on $A$, the quotient set $A/{\sim}$ exists
as the set of equivalence classes, and the canonical projection
\[
\pi:A\to A/{\sim}
\]
is well-defined.
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Operation Respects an Equivalence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $O:A\times A\to A$ be a binary operation and let $\sim$ be an equivalence
relation on $A$. The operation $O$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ if
\[
\forall a,a',b,b'\in A,\,
\bigl(a\sim a'\land b\sim b'\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\bigl(a\mathbin{O}b\bigr)\sim\bigl(a'\mathbin{O}b'\bigr).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

\]

nodes.name: Left and Right Respect
nodes.statement_display:
The operation $O$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ on the left if
\[
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow (a\mathbin{O}b)\sim(a'\mathbin{O}b),
\]
and respects $\sim$ on the right if
\[
b\sim b'\Longrightarrow (a\mathbin{O}b)\sim(a\mathbin{O}b').
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
The operation $O$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ on the left if
\[

\]
and respects $\sim$ on the right if
\[
b\sim b'\Longrightarrow (a\mathbin{O}b)\sim(a\mathbin{O}b').
\]

nodes.name: Respect Splits
nodes.statement_display:
A binary operation $O$ respects $\sim$ if and only if it respects $\sim$ on
the left and on the right.

nodes.name: Induced Operation on a Quotient
nodes.statement_display:
If $O:A\times A\to A$ respects $\sim$, then
\[
[a]\mathbin{\overline{O}}[b]:=[a\mathbin{O}b]
\]
defines a well-defined binary operation on $A/{\sim}$.

nodes.name: Induced Unary Operation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f:A\to A$. If
\[
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow f(a)\sim f(a'),
\]
then
\[
\overline{f}([a]):=[f(a)]
\]
defines a well-defined unary operation on $A/{\sim}$.

\forall a,a'\in A,\,
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow \bigl(P(a)\Longleftrightarrow P(a')\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Relation Respects an Equivalence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $R$ be a binary relation on $A$. The relation $R$ \emph{respects} $\sim$
if
\[
\forall a,a',b,b'\in A,\,
\bigl(a\sim a'\land b\sim b'\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\bigl(R(a,b)\Longleftrightarrow R(a',b')\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Induced Map out of a Quotient
nodes.statement_display:
Let $g:A\to B$. If
\[
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow g(a)=g(a'),
\]
then there is a unique well-defined map
\[
\overline{g}:A/{\sim}\to B
\]
such that
\[
\overline{g}([a])=g(a)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\overline{g}\circ\pi=g,
\]
where $\pi:A\to A/{\sim}$ is the canonical projection.

nodes.name: Induced Partial Operation on a Quotient
nodes.statement_display:
Let $D\subseteq A$ be saturated under $\sim$, meaning
\[
a\in D\land a\sim a'\Longrightarrow a'\in D.
\]
If $f:D\to A$ respects $\sim$ on $D$, then
\[
\overline{f}([a]):=[f(a)]
\]
is a well-defined partial operation on $A/{\sim}$ with domain
\[
D/{\sim}:=\{[a]\in A/{\sim}:a\in D\}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: $\mathbb{Z}$ Has No Zero Divisors

\]

nodes.name: $\mathbb{Z}$ Is an Integral Domain
nodes.statement_display:
The integers form an integral domain: $\mathbb{Z}$ is a commutative ring,
$0\ne 1$, and $\mathbb{Z}$ has no zero divisors.

0\le r<|b|.
\]

nodes.name: Canonical Form of Integers
nodes.statement_display:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Z}$, exactly one of the following holds:
\[
x=[n,0]\text{ for some nonzero }n\in\mathbb{W},\qquad
x=[0,0],\qquad
x=[0,n]\text{ for some nonzero }n\in\mathbb{W}.
\]

nodes.name: Addition on a Peano System
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $m \in P$, define the function
\[
f_m : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
f_m(1)=S(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))).
\]
For $m,n \in P$, define
\[
m+n \coloneqq f_m(n).
\]

nodes.name: Addition Is Well-Defined on a Peano System
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$, there exists a unique
function
\[
f_m : P \to P
\]
such that
\[
f_m(1)=S(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))).
\]
Consequently the rule
\[
(m,n) \longmapsto m+n \coloneqq f_m(n)
\]
defines a unique binary operation on $P$.

\[
a+b=a+c\Longrightarrow b=c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
b+a=c+a\Longrightarrow b=c.
\]

nodes.name: $\mathbb{N}$ Is Closed, Associative, and Commutative Under Addition
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $+$ on $P$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b \in P$.
  \item \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$,
  $(a+b)+c = a+(b+c)$.
  \item \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b = b+a$.
  \item \textbf{Left cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $a+b = a+c$ then $b=c$.
  \item \textbf{Right cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $b+a = c+a$ then $b=c$.
\end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: $\mathbb{N}$ Is a Commutative Monoid Under Multiplication
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $\cdot$ on $P$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a \cdot b \in P$.
  \item \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$,
  $(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c)$.
  \item \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$,
  $a \cdot b = b \cdot a$.
  \item \textbf{Identity.} For all $a \in P$,
  $1 \cdot a = a = a \cdot 1$.
\end{enumerate}
Consequently $(P, \cdot, 1)$ is a commutative monoid.

nodes.name: $\mathbb{N}$ Is a Semiring
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Then $(P,+,\cdot)$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item $(P,+)$ is a commutative semigroup.
  \item $(P,\cdot,1)$ is a commutative monoid.
  \item Multiplication distributes over addition: for all $a,b,c \in P$,
  \[
  a \cdot (b+c) = (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c).
  \]
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Induction from an Arbitrary Base
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $m \in P$, and let $\varphi$ be a
predicate on $P$. If
\[
\varphi(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \land \varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))\bigr),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \Longrightarrow \varphi(n)\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Multiplication on a Peano System
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $m \in P$, define the function
\[
g_m : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
g_m(1)=m
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m).
\]
For $m,n \in P$, define
\[
m \cdot n \coloneqq g_m(n).
\]

nodes.name: Cancellation for Multiplication
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Multiplication on $P$ satisfies cancellation
on both sides:
\[
a\cdot b=a\cdot c\Longrightarrow b=c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
b\cdot a=c\cdot a\Longrightarrow b=c.
\]

\]

nodes.name: The Natural Numbers
nodes.statement_display:
The natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ are the canonical Peano system
\[
(\mathbb{N},1,S),
\]
where $1 \in \mathbb{N}$ is the distinguished base element and
$S:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is the successor operation.

nodes.name: N5, Induction
nodes.statement_display:
For every predicate $\varphi$ on $\mathbb{N}$,
\[
\Bigl(\varphi(1)\land
\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;(\varphi(n)\Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n)))\Bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\forall n\in\mathbb{N}\;\varphi(n).
\]

nodes.name: Strict Less Than on a Peano System
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes
\[
a<b
\]
exactly when there exists $c \in P$ such that
\[
b=a+c.
\]

nodes.name: Natural-Number Order Is Total
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
b \le a.
\]
Consequently $\le$ is a total order on $P$.

nodes.name: Exponentiation on a Peano System
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $a \in P$, define the function
\[
h_a : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).
\]
For $a,n \in P$, define
\[
a^n \coloneqq h_a(n).
\]

nodes.name: Exponentiation Is Well-Defined on a Peano System
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a \in P$, there exists a unique
function
\[
h_a:P\to P
\]
such that
\[
h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).
\]
Consequently the rule
\[
(a,n)\longmapsto a^n\coloneqq h_a(n)
\]
defines a unique binary operation on $P$.

nodes.name: Induction, Strong Induction, and Well-Ordering Are Equivalent
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system equipped with the order $\le$. The following
are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item The induction principle: for every predicate $\varphi$ on $P$, if
  $\varphi(1)$ holds and the successor step holds, then $\varphi(n)$ holds
  for all $n \in P$.
  \item The strong induction principle: for every predicate $\varphi$ on $P$,
  if $\varphi(k)$ holds for every $k<n$ implies $\varphi(n)$, then
  $\varphi(n)$ holds for every $n \in P$.
  \item The well-ordering principle: every nonempty subset of $P$ has a least
  element with respect to $\le$.
\end{enumerate}

\[
(a,b)=\{\,x\in\mathbb{R} : a<x<b\,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Ray
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a\in\mathbb{R}$. The four \emph{rays} determined by $a$ are
\[
(a,\infty)=\{x:x>a\},\ [a,\infty)=\{x:x\ge a\},\ (-\infty,a)=\{x:x<a\},\ (-\infty,a]=\{x:x\le a\}.
\]

nodes.name: Number Line
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{number line} for a linearly ordered system $(A,<_A)$ is a linearly ordered set of points $L$ together with an order isomorphism $\lambda:A\to L$, so that for all $a,a'\in A$,
\[
a<_A a' \Longleftrightarrow \lambda(a) \text{ lies to the left of } \lambda(a').
\]

nodes.name: Peano System
nodes.statement_display:
A triple $(P,S,1)$ is called a \emph{Peano system} exactly when $P$ is a set,
$1 \in P$, $S : P \to P$ is a function, and the following axioms hold:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item[\textbf{P1.}] $1 \in P$.
  \item[\textbf{P2.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \in P$.
  \item[\textbf{P3.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \neq 1$.
  \item[\textbf{P4.}] For all $m,n \in P$, if $S(m)=S(n)$ then $m=n$.
  \item[\textbf{P5.}] If $A \subseteq P$, $1 \in A$, and
  \[
    \forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A),
  \]
  then $A=P$.
\end{enumerate}

  \[
    \forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A),
  \]
  then $A=P$.
\end{enumerate}

\[
\varphi(1)

\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]

nodes.name: Strong Induction on a Peano System
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $\varphi$ be a predicate on $P$.
If
\[
\forall n \in P\;
\bigl(\forall k \in P\;(k < n \Longrightarrow \varphi(k))\bigr)
\Longrightarrow \varphi(n),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]

nodes.source_text:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $\varphi$ be a predicate on $P$.
If
\[
\forall n \in P\;
\bigl(\forall k \in P\;(k < n \Longrightarrow \varphi(k))\bigr)
\Longrightarrow \varphi(n),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]

\[
\forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A).
\]

nodes.name: Minimality of a Peano System
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. If $A\subseteq P$ contains $1$ and is closed
under successor, then $A=P$.
\[
\bigl(1\in A \land
\forall n\in P\;(n\in A \Longrightarrow S(n)\in A)\bigr)
\Longrightarrow A=P.
\]

examples.label: Why the base case is forced
examples.title: Why the base case is forced
examples.body_tex:
For the first element, the theorem says
\[
S(1)\neq 1.
\]
This is exactly the axiom that the distinguished element is not a successor:
there is no element $x\in P$ with $S(x)=1$. In particular, taking $x=1$
would be impossible, so $S(1)\neq 1$.

examples.label: Why the induction step uses injectivity
examples.title: Why the induction step uses injectivity
examples.body_tex:
Suppose the induction hypothesis gives $S(n)\neq n$. To prove the next case,
we must show
\[
S(S(n))\neq S(n).
\]
If equality held, injectivity of $S$ would let us cancel the outer successor
and conclude $S(n)=n$, contradicting the induction hypothesis. Thus the
property propagates from $n$ to $S(n)$.

nodes.name: Presburger Signature
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{Presburger signature} is the first-order signature
\[
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Pres}} = \{0, S, +, <, =\},
\]
where $0$ is a constant symbol, $S$ is a unary function symbol, $+$ is a
binary function symbol, and $<$ and $=$ are binary relation symbols.

nodes.name: Presburger Arithmetic
nodes.statement_display:
\emph{Presburger arithmetic} is the first-order theory of the natural numbers
in the Presburger signature. Its intended structure is
\[
(\mathbb{N},0,S,+,<,=),
\]
and its axioms describe the behavior of zero, successor, addition, order, and
induction for formulas written in the language
$\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Pres}}$.

nodes.name: Iterator Data
nodes.text_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let $g:W\to W$ be a function. The triple $(W,c,g)$ is called \emph{iterator data} for $(P,S,1)$.

nodes.name: Iterator Relation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it. A
relation $R\subseteq P\times W$ is called an \emph{iterator relation} for
$(W,c,g)$ exactly when
\[
(1,c)\in R
\]
and
\[
\forall n\in P\;\forall w\in W\;
\bigl((n,w)\in R\Longrightarrow (S(n),g(w))\in R\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Minimal Iterator Relation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it.
The \emph{minimal iterator relation} for $(W,c,g)$ is
\[
R^*
=
\bigcap\{R\subseteq P\times W : R \text{ is an iterator relation for }
(W,c,g)\}.
\]

nodes.name: Forced Values Are Unique

$R^*$ be the minimal iterator relation. If $(n,w_0)\in R^*$ and
$(n,w_1)\in R^*$, then $w_0=w_1$.

nodes.name: Determinism of the Minimal Iterator Relation

Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it, and let
$R^*$ be the minimal iterator relation. For each $n\in P$, there is at most one
$w\in W$ such that $(n,w)\in R^*$.

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Iterator Functions
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it. If
$f,h:P\to W$ satisfy
\[
f(1)=c,\qquad h(1)=c,
\]
and
\[
\forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=g(f(n)))
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n\in P\;(h(S(n))=g(h(n))),
\]
then $f=h$.

nodes.name: Peano Iterator Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it.

\[
f(1)=c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=g(f(n))).
\]

nodes.name: Iterator-Generated Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it.
The unique function $f:P\to W$ satisfying
\[
f(1)=c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=g(f(n)))
\]
is called the \emph{iterator-generated function determined by $(W,c,g)$}.

nodes.name: Iteration of a Self-Map
nodes.statement_display:

$g:W\to W$. The iterator-generated function determined by $(W,c,g)$ is called
the \emph{iteration of $g$ from $c$ along $P$}.

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Binary Iterator Operations
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $A$ be a set, let $W$ be a set, and
suppose each $a\in A$ determines iterator data $(W,c_a,g_a)$ for $(P,S,1)$.
If $F,G:A\times P\to W$ satisfy
\[
F(a,1)=c_a,\qquad G(a,1)=c_a
\]
and
\[
F(a,S(n))=g_a(F(a,n)),
\qquad
G(a,S(n))=g_a(G(a,n))
\]
for all $a\in A$ and all $n\in P$, then $F=G$.

\emph{stage-dependent step rule} on $W$ over $P$ is a function
\[
\Phi:P\times W\to W.
\]

nodes.name: General Recursive Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let
$\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. A function $f:P\to W$
is called the \emph{general recursive function determined by $(W,c,\Phi)$}
exactly when
\[
f(1)=c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n))).
\]

nodes.name: Uniqueness of General Recursive Functions
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let
$\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. If $f,h:P\to W$ satisfy
\[
f(1)=c,\qquad h(1)=c,
\]
and
\[
\forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n)))
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n\in P\;(h(S(n))=\Phi(n,h(n))),
\]
then $f=h$.

nodes.name: General Recursion by State Encoding
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let
$\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. There exists a function
$H:P\to P\times W$ such that
\[
H(1)=(1,c)
\]
and
\[
\forall n\in P\;
\bigl(H(S(n))=(S(\pi_1(H(n))),\Phi(\pi_1(H(n)),\pi_2(H(n))))\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: General Recursion Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let
$\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. Then there exists a
unique function $f:P\to W$ such that
\[
f(1)=c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n))).
\]

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Peano Systems up to Isomorphism
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P,S_P,1_P)$ and $(Q,S_Q,1_Q)$ be Peano systems. Then there exists a
unique bijection $\theta:P\to Q$ such that
\[
\theta(1_P)=1_Q
\]
and
\[
\forall n\in P\;(\theta(S_P(n))=S_Q(\theta(n))).
\]
Thus any two Peano systems are isomorphic.
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Midpoint Lies Strictly Between Rationals
nodes.statement_display:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
x<y
\Longrightarrow
x< (x+y)\cdot 2_{\mathbb{Q}}^{-1}<y.
\]

\Longrightarrow
\exists n\in\mathbb{N}\ (y<n\cdot x).
\]

nodes.name: Arbitrarily Close Distinct Points
nodes.statement_display:
A number system has arbitrarily close distinct points if for every point $x$
and every positive tolerance $\varepsilon$, there is a point $y\ne x$ such that
\[
|x-y|<\varepsilon.
\]

\exists y\in\mathbb{Q}\ (y\ne x\ \text{and}\ |x-y|<\varepsilon).
\]

\]

nodes.name: One-Sided Rational Approximation
nodes.statement_display:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ and every $\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon
\Longrightarrow
\exists y,z\in\mathbb{Q}\ (x-\varepsilon<y<x<z<x+\varepsilon).
\]

nodes.name: Cauchy Sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$
nodes.statement_display:
A sequence $(x_n)$ in $\mathbb{Q}$ is Cauchy if
\[
\forall\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}\,
\left(
0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon
\Longrightarrow
\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\,
\forall m,n\ge N\,
\left|x_m-x_n\right|<\varepsilon
\right).
\]

\]

For $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$, define
\[
-\alpha:=\{p\in\mathbb{Q}:\exists r\in\mathbb{Q}\,(r>0\land -p-r\notin\alpha)\}.
\]

nodes.name: Upper Bound in $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.statement_display:
Let $S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. A cut $\gamma\in\mathbb{R}$ is an upper bound of $S$
if
\[
\forall\alpha\in S\,(\alpha\le\gamma).
\]
The set $S$ is bounded above if it has an upper bound in $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Supremum in $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.statement_display:
For $S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, a cut $\sigma$ is the supremum of $S$ if $\sigma$
is an upper bound of $S$ and every upper bound $\gamma$ of $S$ satisfies
$\sigma\le\gamma$.

nodes.name: Least-Upper-Bound Property on $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.statement_display:
The ordered set $\mathbb{R}$ has the \emph{least-upper-bound property} if every
nonempty subset of $\mathbb{R}$ that is bounded above has a supremum in
$\mathbb{R}$.

\[
\bigcup_{\alpha\in S}\alpha
\]
is a Dedekind cut.

nodes.name: Least-Upper-Bound Property
nodes.statement_display:
Every nonempty subset $S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ that is bounded above has a least
upper bound, namely
\[
\sup S=\bigcup_{\alpha\in S}\alpha.
\]

\iota(1_{\mathbb{Q}})=1_{\mathbb{R}}.
\]

\iota(p)=\iota(q) \Longrightarrow p=q.

\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\iota(p\cdot q)=\iota(p)\cdot\iota(q).
\]

nodes.name: Nonnegative Cut
nodes.statement_display:
A cut $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$ is nonnegative if $0_{\mathbb{R}}\le\alpha$ and
positive if $0_{\mathbb{R}}<\alpha$.

nodes.name: Product of Nonnegative Cuts
nodes.statement_display:
For nonnegative cuts $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, define
\[
\alpha\cdot\beta
:=
\{r\in\mathbb{Q}:r<0_{\mathbb{Q}}\}
\cup
\{p\cdot q:p\in\alpha,\ q\in\beta,\ p\ge 0_{\mathbb{Q}},\ q\ge 0_{\mathbb{Q}}\}.
\]

nodes.name: Absolute Value on $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.statement_display:
For $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$, define
\[
|\alpha|:=
\begin{cases}
\alpha, & 0_{\mathbb{R}}\le\alpha,\\
-\alpha, & \alpha<0_{\mathbb{R}}.
\end{cases}
\]

\]

nodes.name: Multiplication of Cuts
nodes.statement_display:
For cuts $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, define $\alpha\cdot\beta$ by sign cases:
use $|\alpha|\cdot|\beta|$ when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ have the same sign, use
$-(|\alpha|\cdot|\beta|)$ when they have opposite signs, and use
$0_{\mathbb{R}}$ when either factor is $0_{\mathbb{R}}$.

nodes.name: Reciprocal of a Positive Cut
nodes.statement_display:
For $\alpha>0_{\mathbb{R}}$, define
\[
\alpha^{-1}:=\{r\in\mathbb{Q}:r\le 0_{\mathbb{Q}}\}
\cup
\{r\in\mathbb{Q}:r>0_{\mathbb{Q}}\land \exists s\in\mathbb{Q}\,(s>r\land s^{-1}\notin\alpha)\}.
\]

\]

\[

\]
where $2_{\mathbb{R}}:=\iota(2_{\mathbb{Q}})$.

nodes.name: $\mathbb{R}$ Contains the Supremum $\mathbb{Q}$ Was Missing
nodes.statement_display:
Let
\[
S=\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:0_{\mathbb{Q}}<q\land q\cdot q<2_{\mathbb{Q}}\}.
\]
Then
\[
\sqrt{2}=\sup \iota(S).
\]
Thus the bounded-above rational set with no rational supremum has a supremum
in $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Compact Set in $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.statement_display:
A set $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{compact} exactly when every open cover of $K$ admits a finite subcover:
\[
\forall \{U_i\}_{i\in I}\text{ open cover of }K\;\exists \text{finite } J\subseteq I\;\Bigl(K\subseteq\bigcup_{i\in J}U_i\Bigr).
\]

nodes.statement_display: If $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact, then $K$ is closed and bounded.

nodes.name: Open Cover
nodes.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. An \emph{open cover} of $E$ is a family $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ with
\[
E\subseteq\bigcup_{i\in I} U_{i}.
\]

\]

nodes.name: Subcover and Finite Subcover
nodes.statement_display: Let $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ be an open cover of $E$. A \emph{subcover} is a subfamily $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in J}$ with $J\subseteq I$ that still covers $E$. It is a \emph{finite subcover} when $J$ is finite.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ be an open cover of $E$. A \emph{subcover} is a subfamily $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in J}$ with $J\subseteq I$ that still covers $E$. It is a \emph{finite subcover} when $J$ is finite.

nodes.statement_display: A set $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact if and only if $K$ is closed and bounded.

nodes.name: Interior Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is an interior point of $E$ exactly when
\[
\exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq E).
\]

nodes.name: Exterior Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is an exterior point of $E$ exactly when
\[
\exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq \mathbb{R}\setminus E).
\]

nodes.name: Boundary Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is a boundary point of $E$ exactly when
\[
\forall r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\cap E\neq\emptyset \;\land\; B_{r}(x)\cap(\mathbb{R}\setminus E)\neq\emptyset).
\]

nodes.name: Bounded Subset of the Real Line
nodes.statement_display: A set $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{bounded} exactly when there exists $M\ge 0$ such that $|x|\le M$ for all $x\in E$; equivalently, $E$ is contained in some closed interval $[-M,M]$.

nodes.name: Union of Intervals
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. Their \emph{union} is the set
\[
I\cup J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ or }x\in J\}.
\]

\[
I\cap J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ and }x\in J\}.
\]

nodes.name: Complement of an Interval
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval. Its \emph{complement} in
$\mathbb{R}$ is the set
\[
I^{c}=\mathbb{R}\setminus I
=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\notin I\}.
\]

=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\notin I\}.
\]

nodes.name: Difference of Intervals
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. The \emph{difference} of $I$ by
$J$ is the set
\[
I\setminus J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ and }x\notin J\}
=I\cap J^{c}.
\]

nodes.name: Limit Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is a \emph{limit point} of $E$ exactly when
\[
\forall r>0\;\exists y\in E\;\bigl(0<|y-x|<r\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Isolated Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in E$. Then $x$ is an \emph{isolated point} of $E$ exactly when
\[
\exists r>0\;\forall y\in E\;\bigl(|y-x|<r \Rightarrow y=x\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Open Ball on the Real Line
nodes.statement_display:
Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and $r>0$. The \emph{open ball} of radius $r$ about $x$ is
\[
B_{r}(x)=\{\,y\in\mathbb{R} : |y-x|<r\,\}=(x-r,\,x+r).
\]

\]

nodes.statement_display:

\[
\forall x\in U\;\exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq U).
\]

nodes.name: Distance on the Real Line
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{distance} on $\mathbb{R}$ is the map $d:\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ given by
\[
d(x,y)=|x-y|.
\]

nodes.name: Length of a Bounded Interval
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a\le b$. The \emph{length} of any bounded interval with endpoints $a$ and $b$ is
\[
\ell=b-a.
\]

nodes.name: The Distance Function Is a Metric
nodes.statement_display: The map $d(x,y)=|x-y|$ satisfies, for all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{R}$: (i) $d(x,y)\ge 0$ with $d(x,y)=0$ iff $x=y$; (ii) $d(x,y)=d(y,x)$; and (iii) $d(x,z)\le d(x,y)+d(y,z)$. Hence $(\mathbb{R},d)$ is a metric space.
nodes.statement_tex: The map $d(x,y)=|x-y|$ satisfies, for all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{R}$: (i) $d(x,y)\ge 0$ with $d(x,y)=0$ iff $x=y$; (ii) $d(x,y)=d(y,x)$; and (iii) $d(x,z)\le d(x,y)+d(y,z)$. Hence $(\mathbb{R},d)$ is a metric space.

multiplication, and order.

nodes.name: $\mathbb{W}$ Has No Additive Inverses Except $0$
nodes.statement_display:
If $a,b\in\mathbb{W}$ and
\[
a+_{\mathbb{W}}b=0,

\[

\]

nodes.name: $(\mathbb{W},0,S_{\mathbb{W}})$ Satisfies the Successor Properties
nodes.statement_display:
The triple $(\mathbb{W},0,S_{\mathbb{W}})$ satisfies the successor-side
properties:
\[
0\in\mathbb{W},\qquad
S_{\mathbb{W}}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{W},
\]
\[
\neg\exists a\in\mathbb{W}\;(S_{\mathbb{W}}(a)=0),
\qquad
\forall a,b\in\mathbb{W}\;
(S_{\mathbb{W}}(a)=S_{\mathbb{W}}(b)\Longrightarrow a=b).
\]

a\leq_{\mathbb{N}}b.

nodes.name: Strict Order on $\mathbb{W}$
nodes.statement_display:
Define $<_{\mathbb{W}}$ on $\mathbb{W}$ by declaring
\[
0<_{\mathbb{W}}n\quad(n\in\mathbb{N}),
\]
and, for $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$,
\[
a<_{\mathbb{W}}b
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a<_{\mathbb{N}}b.
\]
No element of $\mathbb{W}$ is strictly less than $0$.

nodes.name: Order on $\mathbb{W}$ Extends Order on $\mathbb{N}$
nodes.statement_display:
For all $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$,
\[
a\leq_{\mathbb{W}}b\Longleftrightarrow a\leq_{\mathbb{N}}b,
\qquad
a<_{\mathbb{W}}b\Longleftrightarrow a<_{\mathbb{N}}b.
\]

\]

\Longrightarrow a=b.
\]

nodes.name: Upper Bound Comparison with the Supremum

nodes.name: Translation Invariance of the Supremum
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $c\in\mathbb{R}$. Then
\[
  \sup(A+c)=\sup A+c,
  \qquad
  A+c=\{a+c:a\in A\}.
\]

\]

  kA=\{ka:a\in A\}.
\]

nodes.name: Supremum of a Sum Set

\[
  \sup(A+B)=\sup A+\sup B,
  \qquad
  A+B=\{a+b:a\in A,\ b\in B\}.
\]

nodes.name: Supremum of a Difference Set
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty, with $A$ bounded above and $B$ bounded below. Then
\[
  \sup(A-B)=\sup A-\inf B,
  \qquad
  A-B=\{a-b:a\in A,\ b\in B\}.
\]

nodes.name: Supremum of a Dilation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded, and let $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$. Then
\[
  \sup(\lambda A)=\max\{\lambda\sup A,\lambda\inf A\},
  \qquad
  \lambda A=\{\lambda a:a\in A\}.
\]

\]

nodes.name: Rational Gap Example for Suprema
nodes.statement_display:
Let
\[
  A=\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:q^2<2\}.

nodes.name: Epsilon Characterization of Supremum

nodes.name: Bound
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A\subseteq S$, and let $b\in S$. The element $b$ is a \emph{bound} for $A$ if and only if $b$ is a uniform one-sided comparison point for all elements of $A$:
\[
\operatorname{Bound}(b,A)
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl[\forall x\in A\;(x\le b)\bigr]
\lor
\bigl[\forall x\in A\;(b\le x)\bigr].
\]

\[

\bigl[\forall x\in A\;(x\le b)\bigr]
\lor
\bigl[\forall x\in A\;(b\le x)\bigr].
\]

nodes.name: Minimum

nodes.name: Supremum
nodes.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A \subseteq S$, and let $s \in S$. The element $s$ is the supremum of $A$ if and only if $s$ is an upper bound of $A$ and every upper bound $u$ of $A$ satisfies $s \le u$.

nodes.name: Least Upper Bound Property Implies Existence of Suprema

nodes.name: Least Upper Bound Property
nodes.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set. The set $S$ has the \emph{Least Upper Bound Property} if and only if every nonempty subset of $S$ that is bounded above in $S$ has a supremum in $S$.

nodes.name: Dense Subset
nodes.statement_display: Let $D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The set $D$ is \emph{dense in} $S$ if and only if for every $x\in S$ and every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $d\in D$ such that $|x-d|<\varepsilon$.

nodes.name: Order Dense Subset
nodes.statement_display: Let $X$ be a linearly ordered set and let $A\subseteq X$. The subset $A$ is \emph{order dense in} $X$ if and only if for every $a,b\in X$ with $a<b$ there exists $c\in A$ such that $a<c<b$.

nodes.name: Nested Interval Property
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(I_n)$ be a sequence of nonempty closed intervals in $\mathbb{R}$. If $I_{n+1}\subseteq I_n$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n\neq\varnothing.
\]

\]

nodes.name: Step Function Approximation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$.
Then there exists a step function $s_\varepsilon:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
\sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)-s_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon.
\]

nodes.name: Piecewise Linear Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $g:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$.
The function $g$ is piecewise linear if and only if there exist
$m \in \mathbb{N}$ with $m \ge 1$ and points
$a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_m=b$ such that $g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]}$ is affine
for every $k\in\{1,\ldots,m\}$.

nodes.name: Piecewise Linear Approximation
nodes.statement_display: Let $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$. Then there exists a continuous piecewise linear function $\ell_\varepsilon\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f(x)-\ell_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon$.

nodes.name: Weierstrass Approximation Theorem

nodes.name: Bernstein Polynomial
nodes.statement_display:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$.
A function $B_n:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is called the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$
if and only if, for every $x \in [0,1]$,
\[
B_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{n} f\!\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\binom{n}{k}x^k(1-x)^{n-k}.
\]

nodes.name: Partition
nodes.statement_display: Let $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A finite ordered set $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ with $n\in\mathbb{N}$ and $n\ge 1$ is a partition of $I$ if and only if $x_0=a$, $x_n=b$, and $x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_n$. In this case the closed subintervals $[x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$ subdivide $I$.

nodes.name: Tagged Partition
nodes.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. A list $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ is a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ if and only if the subintervals determine a partition $P=\{[x_{i-1},x_i]\}_{i=1}^n$ of $[a,b]$ with $a=x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_n=b$, and each tag satisfies $t_i \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$. Equivalently, a tagged partition is exactly a partition $P$ of $[a,b]$ together with one tag chosen inside every subinterval of $P$.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. nodes.name: $\delta$-Fine Partition
nodes.statement_display: Let $[a,b]$ be a closed interval, let $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ be a gauge, and let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$. The tagged partition $\dot{\mathcal{P}}$ is $\delta$-fine if and only if for every $i \in \{1,\dots,n\}$ and every $x \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ the inequality $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$ holds.
nodes.statement_tex: Let $[a,b]$ be a closed interval, let $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ be a gauge, and let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$. nodes.name: Cousin's Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Every gauge $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ on a closed interval $[a,b]$
admits a $\delta$-fine tagged partition of $[a,b]$.

nodes.name: Mesh of a Partition
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ be a partition of $[a,b]$. A real number $m$ is the \emph{mesh} (or \emph{norm}) of $P$ if and only if
\[
m=\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1}).
\]
The mesh of $P$ is denoted by $\|P\|$ and is the unique $m$ satisfying this equivalence.

nodes.name: Refinement
nodes.statement_display: Let $[a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $P$ and $Q$ be partitions of $[a,b]$. Then $Q$ is a refinement of $P$ if and only if every point of $P$ is also a point of $Q$; equivalently, $P \subseteq Q$.

nodes.name: Common Refinement
nodes.statement_display: Let $a<b$ and let $P$ and $Q$ be partitions of $[a,b]$. Their common refinement is the partition $R$ obtained by taking the union of their node sets, so $R = P \cup Q$ as sets of points in $[a,b]$.

nodes.name: Bounded on a Set
nodes.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is bounded on $A$ if and only if there exists a constant $M>0$ such that $|f(x)| \le M$ for every $x \in A$.

nodes.statement_display: Let $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then there exists $M>0$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for every $x\in[a,b]$.

nodes.name: Absolute Maximum and Absolute Minimum
nodes.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. A point $x^{*} \in A$ is an absolute maximum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x^{*}) \ge f(x)$ for every $x \in A$. A point $x_{*} \in A$ is an absolute minimum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x_{*}) \le f(x)$ for every $x \in A$.

nodes.name: Extreme Value Theorem
nodes.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. There exist points $x_m,x_M \in [a,b]$ such that $f(x_m) \le f(x) \le f(x_M)$ for every $x \in [a,b]$. Equivalently, $f$ attains both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on $[a,b]$.

nodes.name: Location of Roots
nodes.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. If either $f(a)<0<f(b)$ or $f(a)>0>f(b)$, then there exists $c\in(a,b)$ such that $f(c)=0$.

nodes.name: Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem
nodes.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f \colon I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$, and choose $a,b \in I$ with $f(a) < k < f(b)$. Then there exists $c \in I$ such that $\min\{a,b\} < c < \max\{a,b\}$ and $f(c) = k$.

nodes.name: Darboux Property
nodes.statement_display: Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ satisfy $a<b$, let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous, and fix $c\in\mathbb{R}$. If $f(x)\neq c$ for every $x\in[a,b]$, then either $f(x)>c$ for all $x\in[a,b]$ or $f(x)<c$ for all $x\in[a,b]$.

nodes.name: Infinite Adherent Points
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The point $+\infty$ is adherent to $X$ if
and only if for every $M\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $x\in X$ such that
$x>M$. The point $-\infty$ is adherent to $X$ if and only if for every
$M\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $x\in X$ such that $x<M$.

nodes.name: Limit at Infinity
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be such that for every $M \in \mathbb{R}$ there exists $x \in X$ with $x > M$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The limit of $f$ at $+\infty$ equals $L$ if and only if
for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $M \in \mathbb{R}$ such that for every $x \in X$, $x > M$ implies $|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$.

nodes.name: Limits Superior and Inferior of a Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f:E\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $x_0$ be an accumulation point of $E$. For each $\delta>0$ set
\[
U(\delta)=\{\,f(x): x\in E,\ 0<|x-x_0|<\delta\,\}.
\]
The extended real numbers $\limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x)$ and $\liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x)$ are defined by
\[
\limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x)=\inf_{\delta>0}\sup U(\delta),
\qquad
\liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x)=\sup_{\delta>0}\inf U(\delta),
\]
where each inner supremum or infimum is taken in $\overline{\mathbb{R}}$ and the outer infimum or supremum ranges over all $\delta>0$.

nodes.name: $\limsup\geq\liminf$
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a limit point of $A$, and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. Then
\[
\limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x) \ge \liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x).
\]

nodes.name: Limit Exists iff $\limsup=\liminf$

nodes.name: One-Sided Limits of Monotone Functions
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $c$ be interior to $I$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be increasing. Define
\[
L_- \coloneqq \sup\{f(x): x\in I,\; x<c\},
\qquad
L_+ \coloneqq \inf\{f(x): x\in I,\; c<x\}.
\]
Then both one-sided limits exist and satisfy
\[
\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x) = L_-,
\qquad
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x) = L_+.
\]

nodes.name: Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions
nodes.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone increasing, and let $c$ be an interior point of $I$. Then $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if its one-sided limits satisfy $f(c^-)=f(c)=f(c^+)$, where $f(c^-)=\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x)$ and $f(c^+)=\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)$.

nodes.name: Jump of a Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be
increasing, and let $c$ be an interior point of $I$. If the one-sided
limits $f(c^-)$ and $f(c^+)$ exist, the jump of $f$ at $c$ is
\[
j_f(c)\coloneqq f(c^+)-f(c^-).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

\]

nodes.name: Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are of First Kind
nodes.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone, and let $a \in I$ be such that there exist points of $I$ strictly less and strictly greater than $a$. If $f$ is discontinuous at $a$, then both one-sided limits $\lim_{x\uparrow a} f(x)$ and $\lim_{x\downarrow a} f(x)$ exist in $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Jump Intervals Are Disjoint
nodes.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be nondecreasing. If $a,b \in I$ are distinct points of discontinuity of $f$, then the open intervals $(f(a^-),f(a^+))$ and $(f(b^-),f(b^+))$ are disjoint.

nodes.name: Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are Countable
nodes.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be monotone. Then the set $D_f \coloneqq \{c \in I : f \text{ is discontinuous at } c\}$ is at most countable.

nodes.name: Continuous Injective Is Strictly Monotone

nodes.name: Continuous Inverse Theorem
nodes.statement_display: Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be strictly monotone and continuous on $I$. Then the inverse function $f^{-1}:f(I)\to I$ exists, is strictly monotone, and is continuous on $f(I)$.

nodes.name: Relative Neighborhood
nodes.statement_display: Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $a \in E$. A set $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is a neighborhood of $a$ in $E$ if and only if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $U = E \cap (a-\delta, a+\delta)$. Equivalently, every neighborhood of $a$ in $E$ is a set of the form $U_E(a) := E \cap (a-\delta, a+\delta)$ for some $\delta > 0$.

nodes.name: Continuity at a Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is \textbf{continuous at $c$} if and only if
\[
\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists \delta > 0 \;\forall x \in A :
\bigl(|x-c| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)| < \varepsilon\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Continuity at a Point --- Neighbourhood Form
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f\bigl(A \cap (c-\delta, c+\delta)\bigr) \subseteq (f(c)-\varepsilon, f(c)+\varepsilon).
\]

\[
f\bigl(A \cap (c-\delta, c+\delta)\bigr) \subseteq (f(c)-\varepsilon, f(c)+\varepsilon).
\]

nodes.name: Continuity at a Point --- Sequential Form
nodes.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in A$, and let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if for every sequence $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ in $A$, the convergence $\lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = c$ implies $\lim_{n\to\infty} f(x_n) = f(c)$.

nodes.name: Neighbourhood Characterization of Discontinuity
nodes.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is discontinuous at $c$ if and only if there exists a neighborhood $V$ of $f(c)$ such that for every neighborhood $U$ of $c$ relative to $A$ there exists $x \in U \cap A$ with $f(x) \notin V$.

nodes.name: Types of Discontinuity
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_0 \in A$ with $f$ discontinuous at $x_0$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item \emph{Removable} iff $\exists L \in \mathbb{R}:\;\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L$ and $L \ne f(x_0)$.
  \item \emph{Jump} iff $\exists L_{-}, L_{+} \in \mathbb{R}:\;\lim_{x \to x_0^-} f(x) = L_{-}$, $\lim_{x \to x_0^+} f(x) = L_{+}$, and $L_{-} \ne L_{+}$.
  \item \emph{Essential} iff it is not removable.
\end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Oscillation on a Set
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, and let
$E\subseteq A$. The oscillation of $f$ on $E$ is the extended real number
\[
\omega(f;E)\coloneqq
\sup\{\,|f(x_1)-f(x_2)|:x_1,x_2\in E\,\}
=\mathrm{diam}(f(E)).
\]

nodes.name: Oscillation at a Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:E \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ satisfy $U_\delta(x_0)\cap E \neq \varnothing$ for every $\delta>0$, where $U_\delta(x_0)=\{x \in \mathbb{R}: |x-x_0|<\delta\}$. The oscillation of $f$ at $x_0$ is the extended real number
\[
\omega(f;x_0)
=
\lim_{\delta \to 0^+}
\omega\bigl(f;\,U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr),
\]
with $\omega(f;A)$ denoting the oscillation of $f$ on the set $A$. Because the function $\delta \mapsto \omega\bigl(f;\,U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr)$ is nonincreasing, this right-hand limit exists (possibly $+\infty$).

nodes.text_preview: Let $f:E\to\mathbb{R}$. Then \[ D(f):=\{x\in E: f \text{ is discontinuous at } x\} =\{x\in E: \omega(f;x)>0\} =\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} \{x\in E: \omega(f;x)\ge 1/n\}. \]

nodes.name: Uniform Continuity
nodes.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is uniformly continuous on $A$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all $x,u \in A$ the implication $|x-u| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(u)| < \varepsilon$ holds.

nodes.name: Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a General Domain
nodes.statement_display:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item For every set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, every pair of functions $f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}$ that are uniformly continuous on $A$, and every scalar $c \in \mathbb{R}$, the functions $f+g$, $f-g$, and $cf$ are uniformly continuous on $A$.
  \item There exist a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and uniformly continuous functions $f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}$ such that the product $fg$ is not uniformly continuous on $A$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a Bounded Domain
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be bounded, and let $f,g:A \to \mathbb{R}$ be uniformly continuous on $A$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item The product $x \mapsto f(x)g(x)$ is uniformly continuous on $A$.
\item If there exists $k>0$ such that $|g(x)| \ge k$ for all $x \in A$, then the quotient $x \mapsto f(x)/g(x)$ is uniformly continuous on $A$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences
nodes.statement_display: Let $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:S \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $(x_n)$ be a sequence in $S$. If $f$ is uniformly continuous on $S$ and $(x_n)$ is a Cauchy sequence in $S$, then $(f(x_n))$ is a Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Lipschitz Condition
nodes.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $K>0$. The function $f$ satisfies the Lipschitz condition on $A$ with constant $K$ if and only if $|f(x)-f(u)| \le K|x-u|$ for every $x,u \in A$.

nodes.name: Algebra of Lipschitz Functions
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ be Lipschitz with constants $K_f,K_g\ge 0$ respectively.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item For every $c\in\mathbb{R}$, the function $cf$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $|c|K_f$.
\item The functions $f+g$ and $f-g$ are Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_f+K_g$.
\item If $B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, $h:B\to\mathbb{R}$ is Lipschitz with constant $K_h\ge 0$, and $f(A)\subseteq B$, then $h\circ f$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_hK_f$.
\item If $|f(x)|\le M_f$ and $|g(x)|\le M_g$ for all $x\in A$, then $fg$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $M_gK_f+M_fK_g$.
\item If $|f(x)|\le M_f$ for all $x\in A$, if $|g(x)|\ge k>0$ for all $x\in A$, and if $g$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_g$, then $f/g$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_f/k + M_fK_g/k^2$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ be Lipschitz with constants $K_f,K_g\ge 0$ respectively.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item For every $c\in\mathbb{R}$, the function $cf$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $|c|K_f$.
\item The functions $f+g$ and $f-g$ are Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_f+K_g$.
\item If $B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, $h:B\to\mathbb{R}$ is Lipschitz with constant $K_h\ge 0$, and $f(A)\subseteq B$, then $h\circ f$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_hK_f$.
\item If $|f(x)|\le M_f$ and $|g(x)|\le M_g$ for all $x\in A$, then $fg$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $M_gK_f+M_fK_g$.
\item If $|f(x)|\le M_f$ for all $x\in A$, if $|g(x)|\ge k>0$ for all $x\in A$, and if $g$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_g$, then $f/g$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_f/k + M_fK_g/k^2$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Bi-Lipschitz Map
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The map $f$ is bi-Lipschitz on $A$ if and only if there exist constants $0<\alpha\le K$ such that for every $x,u\in A$,
\[
\alpha|x-u|\le |f(x)-f(u)|\le K|x-u|.
\]
Any such pair $(\alpha,K)$ is called a bi-Lipschitz constant pair for $f$ on $A$.

nodes.name: Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and suppose there exist constants $\alpha,K>0$ such that
$\alpha |x-y| \le |f(x)-f(y)| \le K |x-y|$ for all $x,y \in A$. Then $f$ is injective, the inverse mapping $f^{-1}:f(A) \to A$ is well defined, and
\[
\frac{1}{K}|u-v| \le |f^{-1}(u)-f^{-1}(v)| \le \frac{1}{\alpha}|u-v|
\quad\text{for all } u,v \in f(A).
\]

nodes.name: Constant Multiple Rule

\[
(\alpha f)'(c) = \alpha f'(c).
\]

\[
(f+g)'(c) = f'(c) + g'(c).
\]

nodes.name: Quotient Rule
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. Suppose that $f$ and $g$ are differentiable at $c$ and that $g(c) \neq 0$. Then $f/g$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
\left(\frac{f}{g}\right)'(c) = \frac{f'(c)g(c) - f(c)g'(c)}{(g(c))^2}.
\]

nodes.name: Finite Sum Rule
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f_1, \dots, f_n : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $\alpha_1, \dots, \alpha_n \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. If each $f_i$ is differentiable at $c$, then $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i\right)'(c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i'(c).
\]

nodes.name: Extended Product Rule
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f_1, \dots, f_n : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. If each $f_i$ is differentiable at $c$, then $\prod_{i=1}^{n} f_i$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
\left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} f_i\right)'(c)
=
\sum_{k=1}^{n}
f_k'(c) \prod_{\substack{i=1\\i\neq k}}^{n} f_i(c).
\]

nodes.statement_display:

\[
(f^n)'(c) = n(f(c))^{n-1}f'(c).
\]

\]

Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f_1, \dots, f_n : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $\alpha_1, \dots, \alpha_n \in \mathbb{R}$. If each $f_i$ is differentiable on $I$, then $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i$ is differentiable on $I$, and
\[
\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i\right)'(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i'(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]

nodes.name: Extended Product Rule on an Interval
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, and let $f_1, \dots, f_n : I \to \mathbb{R}$. If each $f_i$ is differentiable on $I$, then $\prod_{i=1}^{n} f_i$ is differentiable on $I$, and
\[
\left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} f_i\right)'(x)
= \sum_{k=1}^{n} f_k'(x) \prod_{\substack{i=1\\i\neq k}}^{n} f_i(x)
\qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]

nodes.name: Power Rule for Integer Powers of a Function on an Interval
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $n \in \mathbb{N}$. If $f$ is differentiable on $I$, then $f^n$ is differentiable on $I$, and
\[
(f^n)'(x) = n(f(x))^{n-1}f'(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]

nodes.name: Carathéodory Factorization
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. We say that $f$ admits a \emph{Carathéodory factorization} at $c$ if there exists a function $\phi : I \to \mathbb{R}$, continuous at $c$, such that
\[
f(x) = f(c) + (x-c)\,\phi(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]
In this case, $f$ is differentiable at $c$ and $f'(c) = \phi(c)$.

nodes.name: Carathéodory Characterization of Differentiability
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f$ is differentiable at $c$.
\item There exists a function $\phi : I \to \mathbb{R}$, continuous at $c$, such that
\[
f(x) = f(c) + (x-c)\,\phi(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]
\end{enumerate}
In this case, $\phi(c) = f'(c)$.

nodes.name: Chain Rule
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I, J \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be intervals, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ and $g : J \to \mathbb{R}$, and suppose that $f(I) \subseteq J$. Let $c \in I$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$ and $g$ is differentiable at $f(c)$, then the composition $g \circ f$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
(g \circ f)'(c) = g'(f(c))\,f'(c).
\]

nodes.name: Derivative at a Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f: D \to \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function and $c \in \operatorname{int}(D)$. We say $f$ is \emph{differentiable at $c$} with \emph{derivative} $L = f'(c)$ if and only if:
\[
\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists \delta > 0 \;\forall x \in D
\Bigl(0 < |x - c| < \delta \implies \left| \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} - L \right| < \varepsilon\Bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Topological Definition of Derivative at a Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f: D \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in \operatorname{int}(D)$. $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if for every neighbourhood $V$ of $L$, there exists a neighbourhood $U$ of $c$ such that for all $x \in (U \setminus \{c\}) \cap D$:
\[
\frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} \in V.
\]

nodes.name: Sequential Definition of Derivative at a Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. We say that $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if
\[
\forall (x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}\;
\bigl(x_n \to c \Rightarrow \tfrac{f(x_n)-f(c)}{x_n-c} \to L\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Derivative: $h$-Form Equivalence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in \operatorname{int}(A)$. Then $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if and only if
\[
\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} = L.
\]

nodes.name: Relative Minimum
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{relative minimum} at $c$ if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f(c) \leq f(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta).
\]
If, in addition,
\[
f(c) < f(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in \bigl(A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta)\bigr)\setminus\{c\},
\]
then $f$ has a \emph{strict relative minimum} at $c$.

nodes.name: Interior Extremum Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$.
If $f$ has a relative extremum at $c$ and $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then
\[
f'(c) = 0.

nodes.name: Increasing at a Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ is \emph{increasing at $c$} if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all
\[
x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c)
\quad \text{and} \quad
y \in A \cap (c,c+\delta),
\]
we have $f(x) \leq f(c) \leq f(y)$.

nodes.name: Zero Derivative Implies Constant
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be a closed interval, and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. If
\[
f'(x) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } x \in (a,b),
\]
then $f$ is constant on $I$.

nodes.name: Equal Derivatives Imply Difference Is Constant
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, and let $f, g : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. If
\[
f'(x) = g'(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in (a,b),
\]
then there exists $C \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(x) = g(x) + C$ for all $x \in I$.

nodes.name: Nondecreasing Iff Nonnegative Derivative
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable on $I$. Then $f$ is nondecreasing on $I$ if and only if
\[
f'(x) \geq 0 \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]

nodes.name: Positive Derivative Implies Locally Increasing
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in I$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. If $f'(c) > 0$, then there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f(x) < f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c - \delta < x < c,
\]
and
\[
f(x) > f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c < x < c + \delta.
\]

nodes.name: First Derivative Test: Relative Maximum
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$, and let $c \in (a,b)$. Suppose $f$ is differentiable on $(a,c)$ and $(c,b)$. If there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $(c-\delta,c+\delta) \subseteq I$ and
\[
f'(x) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in (c-\delta,c),
\qquad
f'(x) \leq 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in (c,c+\delta),
\]
then $f$ has a relative maximum at $c$.

nodes.name: Darboux's Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable on $I$. If $k$ is a real number strictly between $f'(a)$ and $f'(b)$, then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that
\[
f'(c) = k.
\]

nodes.name: Rolle's Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. Suppose that $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, differentiable on $(a,b)$, and satisfies $f(a) = f(b)$. Then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that
\[
f'(c) = 0.
\]

nodes.name: Mean Value Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. Suppose that $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. Then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that
\[
f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Left-Hand Derivative
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$ be such that $c$ is a left-hand limit point of $I$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{left-hand derivative} at $c$ if the limit
\[
\lim_{x \to c^-} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c}
\]
exists. In that case, the value of this limit is denoted $f'_-(c)$:
\[
f'_-(c) := \lim_{x \to c^-} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c}.
\]

nodes.name: Right-Hand Derivative
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$ be such that $c$ is a right-hand limit point of $I$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{right-hand derivative} at $c$ if the limit
\[

\]
exists. In that case, the value of this limit is denoted $f'_+(c)$:
\[
f'_+(c) := \lim_{x \to c^+} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c}.
\]

nodes.name: Differentiability and One-Sided Derivatives
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$. Then $f$ is differentiable at $c \in I$ if and only if both one-sided derivatives $f'_-(c)$ and $f'_+(c)$ exist and are equal. In that case,
\[
f'(c) = f'_-(c) = f'_+(c).
\]

nodes.name: Secant Line
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_1, x_2 \in A$ with $x_1 \neq x_2$. The \emph{secant line} determined by the points $(x_1, f(x_1))$ and $(x_2, f(x_2))$ is the unique line passing through these two points. Its slope is
\[
m_{\mathrm{sec}}(x_1, x_2) := \frac{f(x_2) - f(x_1)}{x_2 - x_1}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Tangent Line
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in A$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. The \emph{tangent line} to the graph of $f$ at the point $(c, f(c))$ is the line through $(c, f(c))$ with slope $f'(c)$. Its equation is
\[
y = f(c) + f'(c)(x - c).
\]

\]

nodes.name: Taylor Polynomial at a Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let
$n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have derivatives
$f^{(k)}(a)$ for $0\le k\le n$. The \textbf{Taylor polynomial of order}
$n$ for $f$ at $a$ is
\[
  T_{n,a}f(x)
  :=
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k.
\]

nodes.name: Taylor Remainder
nodes.statement_display:
Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let
$n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have a Taylor polynomial
$T_{n,a}f$. The \textbf{Taylor remainder of order} $n$ at $a$ is the
function $R_{n,a}f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ defined by
\[
  R_{n,a}f(x):=f(x)-T_{n,a}f(x).
\]

nodes.name: Maclaurin Polynomial
nodes.statement_display:
Let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f$ be defined on an interval containing
$0$ with derivatives $f^{(k)}(0)$ for $0\le k\le n$. The
\textbf{Maclaurin polynomial of order} $n$ for $f$ is
\[
  T_{n,0}f(x)
  :=
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(0)}{k!}x^k.
\]

nodes.name: Taylor's Theorem with Lagrange Remainder
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let
$f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$. Suppose that $f,f',\ldots,f^{(n)}$ are continuous
on $[a,b]$ and that $f^{(n+1)}$ exists on $(a,b)$. Then, for every
$x\in(a,b)$, there exists $c$ strictly between $a$ and $x$ such that
\[
  f(x)
  =
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k
  +
  \frac{f^{(n+1)}(c)}{(n+1)!}(x-a)^{n+1}.
\]

nodes.name: Taylor Expansion with Peano Remainder

Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let
$n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have derivatives up to order
$n$ in a neighbourhood of $a$. If $f^{(n)}$ is continuous at $a$, then
\[
  f(x)
  =
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k
  +
  o\bigl((x-a)^n\bigr)
  \qquad\text{as }x\to a.
\]

nodes.name: Hyperbolic Functions
nodes.statement_display:
For all $x \in \mathbb{R}$:
\[
  \cosh x \;:=\; \frac{e^x + e^{-x}}{2},
  \qquad
  \sinh x \;:=\; \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{2},
  \qquad
  \tanh x \;:=\; \frac{\sinh x}{\cosh x}.
\]
Additional: $\operatorname{sech} x := 1/\cosh x$,\;
$\operatorname{csch} x := 1/\sinh x$,\;
$\coth x := \cosh x / \sinh x$.

nodes.name: Addition Formulas for Hyperbolic Functions
nodes.statement_display:
For all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$:
\[
  \sinh(x+y) = \sinh x \cosh y + \cosh x \sinh y,
  \qquad
  \cosh(x+y) = \cosh x \cosh y + \sinh x \sinh y.
\]

nodes.name: Inverse Hyperbolic Functions
nodes.statement_display:
Since $\sinh$ is strictly increasing and bijective $\mathbb{R} \to
\mathbb{R}$, its inverse $\operatorname{arcsinh}$ is defined on all
of $\mathbb{R}$. Since $\cosh$ is strictly increasing on
$[0,\infty)$ with range $[1,\infty)$, its inverse
$\operatorname{arccosh}$ is defined on $[1,\infty)$. Since $\tanh$
is strictly increasing with range $(-1,1)$, $\operatorname{arctanh}$
is defined on $(-1,1)$.

Explicit logarithmic formulas:
\begin{align*}
  \operatorname{arcsinh} x
  &= \ln\!\left(x + \sqrt{x^2+1}\right),
  \quad x \in \mathbb{R}. \\
  \operatorname{arccosh} x
  &= \ln\!\left(x + \sqrt{x^2-1}\right),
  \quad x \geq 1. \\
  \operatorname{arctanh} x
  &= \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right),
  \quad |x| < 1.
\end{align*}

nodes.name: Natural Logarithm
nodes.statement_display:
The \textbf{natural logarithm} $\ln : (0,\infty) \to \mathbb{R}$ is
the inverse of the exponential function: for $x > 0$,
\[
  \ln x \;:=\; \text{the unique } y \in \mathbb{R}
  \text{ such that } e^y = x.
\]
Equivalently, $\ln x$ is defined by the integral
\[
  \ln x \;:=\; \int_1^x \frac{1}{t}\, dt.
\]

nodes.name: Every Power Dominates the Logarithm
nodes.statement_display:
For every $r > 0$:
\[
  \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{\ln x}{x^r} = 0
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  \lim_{x \to 0^+} x^r \ln x = 0.
\]

nodes.name: Power Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{integer power function} is
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$, $f(x) := x^n$. For $r \in \mathbb{R}$
and $x > 0$, the \textbf{real power} is defined by
\[
  x^r \;:=\; e^{r \ln x}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{integer power function} is
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$, $f(x) := x^n$. For $r \in \mathbb{R}$
and $x > 0$, the \textbf{real power} is defined by
\[
  x^r \;:=\; e^{r \ln x}.
\]
nodes.source_text:

and $x > 0$, the \textbf{real power} is defined by
\[
  x^r \;:=\; e^{r \ln x}.
\]

nodes.name: The Number $e$
nodes.statement_display:
The \textbf{Euler number} is
\[
  e \;:=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!}
  \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} \!\left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)^{\!n}
  \;\approx\; 2.71828\ldots
\]
Both expressions define the same real number.

nodes.name: Exponential Function
nodes.statement_display:
The \textbf{exponential function} $\exp : \mathbb{R} \to (0,\infty)$
is defined by the absolutely convergent power series
\[
  e^x \;:=\; \exp(x) \;:=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!},
  \qquad x \in \mathbb{R}.
\]

  \qquad x \in \mathbb{R}.
\]

nodes.name: Sine and Cosine via Power Series
nodes.text_preview: The \textbf{sine} and \textbf{cosine} functions are defined for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ by the absolutely convergent power series \[ \sin x \;:=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n x^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)!} \;=\; x - \frac{x^3}{3!} + \frac{x^5}{5!}

nodes.name: Pythagorean Identity
nodes.statement_display:
For all $x \in \mathbb{R}$:
\[
  \sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1.
\]

nodes.name: Addition Formulas
nodes.statement_display:
For all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$:
\[
  \sin(x+y) = \sin x \cos y + \cos x \sin y,
  \qquad
  \cos(x+y) = \cos x \cos y - \sin x \sin y.
\]

nodes.name: Tangent, Cotangent, Secant, Cosecant
nodes.statement_display:
\[
  \tan x := \frac{\sin x}{\cos x},\quad
  \cot x := \frac{\cos x}{\sin x},\quad
  \sec x := \frac{1}{\cos x},\quad
  \csc x := \frac{1}{\sin x}.
\]
$\tan x$ and $\sec x$ are defined where $\cos x \neq 0$,
i.e., $x \neq \pi/2 + k\pi$ for $k \in \mathbb{Z}$.

nodes.name: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
nodes.statement_display:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\arcsin : [-1,1] \to [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ is the inverse of
    $\sin\big|_{[-\pi/2,\pi/2]}$.
  \item $\arccos : [-1,1] \to [0,\pi]$ is the inverse of
    $\cos\big|_{[0,\pi]}$.
  \item $\arctan : \mathbb{R} \to (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ is the inverse
    of $\tan\big|_{(-\pi/2,\pi/2)}$.
\end{itemize}

nodes.name: Composition of Injections Is Injective
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f:A\to B$ and let $g:B\to C$. If $f$ is injective and $g$ is
injective, then $g\circ f:A\to C$ is injective.
nodes.statement_tex:

injective, then $g\circ f:A\to C$ is injective.

nodes.name: Preimage Distributes over Union and Intersection
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f:A\to B$, $S,T\subseteq B$. Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item $f^{-1}(S\cup T)=f^{-1}(S)\cup f^{-1}(T)$.
  \item $f^{-1}(S\cap T)=f^{-1}(S)\cap f^{-1}(T)$.
  \item $f^{-1}(S^c)=(f^{-1}(S))^c$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.statement_tex:

  \item $f^{-1}(S^c)=(f^{-1}(S))^c$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Sum Rule for Function Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, then
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(f+g)(x)=L+M.
\]

\]

nodes.name: Difference Rule for Function Limits

$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, then
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(f-g)(x)=L-M.
\]

nodes.name: Scalar Multiple Rule for Function Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and let
$\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(\alpha f)(x)=\alpha L.
\]

\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(fg)(x)=LM.
\]

nodes.name: Quotient Rule for Function Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, and $M\neq 0$, then
there exists $\delta_0>0$ such that $g(x)\neq 0$ whenever $x\in A$ and
$0<|x-c|<\delta_0$, and
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}=\frac{L}{M}.
\]

nodes.name: $\varepsilon$-Closeness of a Function to a Value
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let
$L \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $\varepsilon > 0$. The function $f$ is
\textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$} if $|f(x)-L|\leq\varepsilon$ for
all $x\in X$.
nodes.statement_tex:

$L \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $\varepsilon > 0$. The function $f$ is
\textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$} if $|f(x)-L|\leq\varepsilon$ for
all $x\in X$.

nodes.name: Local $\delta$-Closeness Near a Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let $L \in \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be
adherent to $X$, and let $\varepsilon, \delta > 0$. The function $f$
is \textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$ near $x_0$ within $\delta$} if
\[
  \forall x \in X\;\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|\leq\varepsilon\bigr).
\]
