nodes.name: Limit of a Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
A real number $L$ is the \textbf{limit of $f$ at $c$}, written
$\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$, if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\;
  0<|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\;
  0<|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.source_text:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
A real number $L$ is the \textbf{limit of $f$ at $c$}, written
$\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$, if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\;
  0<|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
used_by_titles: Sequential Definition of Derivative at a Point
Scalar Multiple Rule for Function Limits

nodes.name: Sequential Definition of Limit
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The limit $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ in the \textbf{sequential
sense} holds if for every sequence $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$
with $x_n \to c$, the sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Neighbourhood Form of Limit
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The limit $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ in the \textbf{neighbourhood
sense} holds if for every $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood
$V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists a deleted $\delta$-neighbourhood
$V_\delta^*(c)$ such that
\[
  x\in V_\delta^*(c)\cap A
  \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
  f(x)\in V_\varepsilon(L).
\]

nodes.name: Neighbourhood Form of the Limit
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if for every
$\varepsilon$-neighbourhood $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists a
$\delta$-neighbourhood $V_\delta(c)$ such that
$x\in V_\delta^*(c)\cap A$ implies $f(x)\in V_\varepsilon(L)$.

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $f$ has at most one limit at $c$.

nodes.name: Sequential Criterion for Function Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if for every sequence
$(x_n)\subseteq A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$, one has
$f(x_n)\to L$.

nodes.name: Three Equivalent Forms of the Function Limit
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ form of $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, the
neighbourhood form, and the sequential form are equivalent descriptions
of the same limit condition.

nodes.name: Limit Implies Local Boundedness
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ for some $L \in \mathbb{R}$,
then there exist $\delta, M > 0$ such that $|f(x)| \leq M$ for all
$x \in A$ with $0<|x-c|<\delta$.
nodes.statement_tex:

$x \in A$ with $0<|x-c|<\delta$.

nodes.name: Bounded Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and
let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$. If $a \leq f(x) \leq b$ for all $x \in A$
with $x \neq c$, and $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then
$a \leq L \leq b$.

nodes.name: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f, g, h : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of
$A$. Suppose $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L =
\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=L$, and suppose there exists
$\delta_0 > 0$ such that
\[
  \forall x \in A,\;
  0 < |x - c| < \delta_0
  \;\Rightarrow\;
  f(x) \leq h(x) \leq g(x).
\]
Then $\lim_{x\to c}h(x)=L$.

nodes.name: Limits Are Local
nodes.statement_display:
Let $E \subseteq X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a cluster
point of $E$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $\delta > 0$.
Then
\[
  \lim_{x\to x_0;\,x\in E}f(x)=L
  \;\iff\;
  \lim_{x\to x_0;\,x\in E\cap(x_0-\delta,x_0+\delta)}f(x)=L.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Limit of Absolute Value
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then
$\lim_{x\to c}|f(x)|=|L|$.

nodes.name: Composition of Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$, let $g : B \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c_1$ be a
cluster point of $A$, and let $c_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster
point of $B$. Suppose $\lim_{x\to c_1}f(x)=c_2$ and
$\lim_{y\to c_2}g(y)=L$. If $c_2 \in B$, assume also that
$g(c_2) = L$. Then $\lim_{x\to c_1}g(f(x))=L$.

nodes.name: Monotone Limit Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a < b$ and let $f : (a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone and
bounded. Then both one-sided limits exist at every interior point.
For increasing $f$ and every $x_0 \in (a,b)$:
\[
  f(x_0^-) = \sup\{\,f(x) : a < x < x_0\,\},
  \qquad
  f(x_0^+) = \inf\{\,f(x) : x_0 < x < b\,\}.
\]

nodes.name: Right-Hand Limit
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c\in\mathbb{R}$,
and suppose $c$ is a cluster point of $A\cap(c,\infty)$. A real number
$L$ is the \textbf{right-hand limit} of $f$ at $c$ if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\;
  0<x-c<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]

nodes.name: Left-Hand Limit
nodes.statement_display:

and suppose $c$ is a cluster point of $A\cap(-\infty,c)$. A real number
$L$ is the \textbf{left-hand limit} of $f$ at $c$ if
\[

  0<c-x<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]

nodes.name: Sequential Criterion for Right-Hand Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, and suppose $c$ is a
cluster point of $A\cap(c,\infty)$. Then $\lim_{x\to c^+}f(x)=L$ if and
only if for every sequence $(x_n)\subseteq A\cap(c,\infty)$ with
$x_n\to c$, one has $f(x_n)\to L$.

nodes.name: Two-Sided Limit via One-Sided Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. If $c$ is a
cluster point of both $A\cap(c,\infty)$ and $A\cap(-\infty,c)$, then
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if both $\lim_{x\to c^+}f(x)=L$ and
$\lim_{x\to c^-}f(x)=L$.
nodes.statement_tex:

$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if both $\lim_{x\to c^+}f(x)=L$ and
$\lim_{x\to c^-}f(x)=L$.

nodes.name: Pointwise Sum of Functions
nodes.statement_display:

pointwise sum $f+g:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by
\[
  (f+g)(x)=f(x)+g(x)
  \qquad (x\in X).
\]

  \qquad (x\in X).
\]

nodes.name: Pointwise Maximum of Two Functions
nodes.statement_display:

pointwise maximum $\max(f,g):X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by
\[
  \max(f,g)(x)=\max\{f(x),g(x)\}
  \qquad (x\in X).
\]

nodes.name: Pointwise Quotient of Functions
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$.  Suppose
$g(x)\neq 0$ for every $x\in X$.  The pointwise quotient $f/g:X\to\mathbb{R}$
is the function defined by
\[
  (f/g)(x)=\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}
  \qquad (x\in X).
\]

$\alpha f+\beta g:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by
\[
  (\alpha f+\beta g)(x)=\alpha f(x)+\beta g(x)
  \qquad (x\in X).
\]

nodes.name: Real-Linear Rule
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a class of real-valued functions closed under real
linear combinations.  A rule $T$ on $\mathcal{C}$ is real-linear if,
whenever $f,g\in\mathcal{C}$ and $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$,
\[
  T(\alpha f+\beta g)=\alpha T(f)+\beta T(g)
\]
whenever both sides are defined.

\]
whenever both sides are defined.

nodes.name: Bounded Above Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is bounded above on $A$ if there exists $M\in\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
  f(x)\leq M
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Bounded Below Function

$f$ is bounded below on $A$ if there exists $m\in\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
  m\leq f(x)
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]

nodes.name: Bounded Function

$f$ is bounded on $A$ if there exists $B>0$ such that
\[
  |f(x)|\leq B
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]

nodes.name: Supremum of a Function on a Set
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The supremum of
$f$ on $A$ is
\[
  \sup_{x\in A} f(x) = \sup f(A),
\]
provided the supremum of $f(A)$ exists.

nodes.name: Infimum of a Function on a Set

$f$ on $A$ is
\[
  \inf_{x\in A} f(x) = \inf f(A),
\]
provided the infimum of $f(A)$ exists.

nodes.name: Maximum Point of a Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  A point
$x^*\in A$ is a maximum point of $f$ on $A$ if
\[
  f(x)\leq f(x^*)
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]

nodes.name: Minimum Point of a Function
nodes.statement_display:

$x_*\in A$ is a minimum point of $f$ on $A$ if
\[
  f(x_*)\leq f(x)
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]

nodes.name: Increasing Function
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is increasing on $A$ if
\[
  x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)\leq f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]

\]

nodes.name: Monotone Function

$f$ is monotone on $A$ if $f$ is increasing on $A$ or decreasing on $A$.

nodes.name: Constant Function

  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]

nodes.name: $\varepsilon$-Neighbourhood
nodes.statement_display:
Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and let $\varepsilon>0$. The
\textbf{$\varepsilon$-neighbourhood} of $x$ is
\[
  V_\varepsilon(x):=(x-\varepsilon,x+\varepsilon)
  =\{y\in\mathbb{R}: |y-x|<\varepsilon\}.
\]

nodes.name: Deleted $\varepsilon$-Neighbourhood
nodes.statement_display:
Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and let $\varepsilon>0$. The
\textbf{deleted $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood} of $x$ is
\[
  V_\varepsilon^*(x):=V_\varepsilon(x)\setminus\{x\}
  =\{y\in\mathbb{R}:0<|y-x|<\varepsilon\}.
\]

nodes.name: Cluster Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $x \in \mathbb{R}$. The point $x$
is a \textbf{cluster point} of $X$ if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon > 0,\;
  \exists\, y \in X \setminus \{x\} :\;
  |y-x|<\varepsilon.
\]

nodes.name: Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points
nodes.statement_display:
$c\text{ is a cluster point of }A$ if and only if there exists
$(a_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with $a_n \to c$.

\]

nodes.name: Isolated Point

\textbf{isolated point} of $X$ if
\[
  \exists\varepsilon>0\;\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X=\{x\}\bigr).
\]

\]

$X\text{ is closed} \iff \forall (a_n)\subseteq X,\;

nodes.name: Heine--Borel Theorem for $\mathbb{R}$

$\iff$ every $(a_n)\subseteq X$ has a subsequence converging to
a limit in $X$.

nodes.name: True Near a Point
nodes.statement_display:

$\exists\delta>0,\;\forall x : 0<|x-x_0|<
\delta) \Rightarrow P(f(x))$.

nodes.name: Newton Sequence for $\sqrt{2}$
nodes.statement_display:
The \textbf{Newton sequence for} $\sqrt{2}$ is the real sequence $(x_n)$
defined by
\[
  x_1:=\frac{3}{2},
  \qquad
  x_{n+1}:=\frac{1}{2}\left(x_n+\frac{2}{x_n}\right)
  \quad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

defined by

  \quad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

\]

nodes.name: Newton Approximation of $\sqrt{2}$
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be the Newton sequence for $\sqrt{2}$. Then $(x_n)$ converges
and
\[
  \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\sqrt{2}.
\]

nodes.name: Factorial Partial Sums
nodes.statement_display:
The \textbf{factorial partial-sum sequence} is the real sequence $(s_n)$
defined by
\[
  s_n:=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{1}{k!}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Factorial Partial Sums Approximate $e$
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(s_n)$ be the factorial partial-sum sequence. Then $(s_n)$ converges
to the Euler number $e$:
\[
  \lim_{n\to\infty}s_n=e.
\]

nodes.name: Compound-Interest Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
The \textbf{compound-interest sequence} is the real sequence $(c_n)$
defined by
\[
  c_n:=\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Decimal Truncation Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. The \textbf{decimal truncation sequence} of
$\alpha$ is the real sequence $(d_n)$ defined by
\[
  d_n:=\frac{\lfloor 10^n\alpha\rfloor}{10^n}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Cauchy Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a
\textbf{Cauchy sequence} if
\[
  \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N
  \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a
\textbf{Cauchy sequence} if
\[
  \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N
  \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr).
\]
nodes.source_text:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a
\textbf{Cauchy sequence} if
\[
  \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N
  \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Convergent Sequences Are Cauchy
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ converges, then $(x_n)$ is
Cauchy.

nodes.name: Cauchy Sequences Are Bounded
nodes.statement_display: Every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.
nodes.statement_tex: Every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.

nodes.text_preview: Every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.

nodes.name: Cauchy Sequence with a Convergent Subsequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If a subsequence of $(x_n)$
converges to $L\in\mathbb{R}$, then $(x_n)$ converges to $L$.
nodes.statement_tex:

converges to $L\in\mathbb{R}$, then $(x_n)$ converges to $L$.

nodes.name: Cauchy Criterion for Real Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
$(x_n)$ is Cauchy.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
$(x_n)$ is Cauchy.

$(x_n)$ is Cauchy.

nodes.name: Cauchy Criterion via Tails
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if
for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that the
$N$-tail has pairwise term distances less than $\varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if
for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that the
$N$-tail has pairwise term distances less than $\varepsilon$.

nodes.name: Cauchy Tail Diameter Criterion
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if
every sufficiently late tail has arbitrarily small pairwise diameter.

Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if

nodes.name: Cauchy Sequences Have Vanishing Successive Differences
nodes.statement_display:
If $(x_n)$ is a Cauchy real sequence, then
\[
  |x_{n+1}-x_n|\to 0.
\]

If $(x_n)$ is a Cauchy real sequence, then

  |x_{n+1}-x_n|\to 0.
\]

nodes.name: Reciprocals of Cauchy Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If there exist $c>0$ and
$N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then
$(1/x_n)$ is Cauchy.

$(1/x_n)$ is Cauchy.

nodes.name: Cluster Value of a Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. The number $L$ is
a \textbf{cluster value} of $(x_n)$ if for every $\varepsilon>0$ and every
$N\in\mathbb{N}$ there exists $n\ge N$ such that
\[
  |x_n-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. The number $L$ is
a \textbf{cluster value} of $(x_n)$ if for every $\varepsilon>0$ and every
$N\in\mathbb{N}$ there exists $n\ge N$ such that
\[
  |x_n-L|<\varepsilon.
\]

\[
  |x_n-L|<\varepsilon.
\]

nodes.name: Cluster Values Are Subsequential Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $L$ is a
cluster value of $(x_n)$ if and only if $L$ is a subsequential limit of
$(x_n)$.

cluster value of $(x_n)$ if and only if $L$ is a subsequential limit of
$(x_n)$.

nodes.name: Bounded Sequences Have Cluster Values
nodes.statement_display: Every bounded real sequence has at least one cluster value.
nodes.statement_tex: Every bounded real sequence has at least one cluster value.
nodes.source_text: Every bounded real sequence has at least one cluster value.
nodes.text_preview: Every bounded real sequence has at least one cluster value.

nodes.name: Limsup and Liminf Are Extremal Cluster Values
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then
$\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n$ is the largest cluster value of $(x_n)$, and
$\liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n$ is the smallest cluster value of $(x_n)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then
$\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n$ is the largest cluster value of $(x_n)$, and
$\liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n$ is the smallest cluster value of $(x_n)$.

nodes.name: Pointwise Sum of Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise sum}
of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n+y_n)$ defined by
\[
(x+y)_n := x_n+y_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Scalar Multiple of a Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. The
\textbf{scalar multiple} $\alpha(x_n)$ is the real sequence
$(\alpha x_n)$ defined by
\[
(\alpha x)_n := \alpha x_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Linear Combination of Real Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences, and let
$\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$.  The real linear combination
$\alpha(x_n)+\beta(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(\alpha x_n+\beta y_n)$
defined by
\[
(\alpha x+\beta y)_n := \alpha x_n+\beta y_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

\qquad

\]

nodes.name: Pointwise Negation of a Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The \textbf{pointwise negation} of
$(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(-x_n)$ defined by
\[
(-x)_n := -x_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Pointwise Product of Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise product}
of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_ny_n)$ defined by
\[
(xy)_n := x_ny_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Reciprocal Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence such that $x_n\neq 0$ for every
$n\in\mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{reciprocal sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real
sequence $(1/x_n)$ defined by
\[
\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)_n := \frac{1}{x_n}
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Pointwise Quotient of Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences such that $y_n\neq 0$ for every
$n\in\mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{pointwise quotient} of $(x_n)$ by $(y_n)$ is
the real sequence $(x_n/y_n)$ defined by
\[
\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)_n := \frac{x_n}{y_n}
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Square Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The \textbf{square sequence} of $(x_n)$ is
the real sequence $(x_n^2)$ defined by
\[
(x^2)_n := x_n^2
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Absolute Value Sequence
nodes.statement_display:

$(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(|x_n|)$ defined by
\[
(|x|)_n := |x_n|
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Square Root Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence such that $0\le x_n$ for every
$n\in\mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{square root sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real
sequence $(\sqrt{x_n})$ defined by
\[
(\sqrt{x})_n := \sqrt{x_n}
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Limit of a Sum
nodes.statement_display:

If $x_n\to L$ and $y_n\to M$, then $x_n+y_n\to L+M$.

nodes.name: Nonzero Limit is Eventually Nonzero
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$ with $L\neq 0$.
If $x_n\to L$, then there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n\neq 0$
for every $n\ge N$.

$p(x_n)\to p(L)$.

nodes.name: Rational Sequence Limit
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, let $L\in\mathbb{R}$, and let
$p,q\in\mathbb{R}[t]$ be polynomials. Suppose $q(L)\neq 0$ and
$q(x_n)\neq 0$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$. If $x_n\to L$, then
\[
\frac{p(x_n)}{q(x_n)}\to \frac{p(L)}{q(L)}.
\]

nodes.name: Convergence of a Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{converges}
to $L \in \mathbb{R}$ if
\[
    \forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge N
    \;\bigl(|x_n - L| < \varepsilon\bigr).
\]
The value $L$ is called the \textbf{limit} of $(x_n)$, written
$x_n \to L$ or $\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = L$.
A sequence that converges to some $L \in \mathbb{R}$ is called
\textbf{convergent}; otherwise it is called \textbf{divergent}.

nodes.name: Convergence --- Neighborhood Formulation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The sequence
$(x_n)$ \textbf{converges} to $L$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ the
$\varepsilon$-neighborhood
\[
    V_\varepsilon(L) \;:=\; (L - \varepsilon,\, L + \varepsilon)
\]
contains all but finitely many terms of $(x_n)$; that is,
\[
    \forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge N
    \;\bigl(x_n \in V_\varepsilon(L)\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Equivalence of Convergence Formulations
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The following
statements are equivalent.
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\alph*)}]
    \item $x_n \to L$.
    \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K
          \;\bigl(|x_n - L| < \varepsilon\bigr)$.
    \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K
          \;\bigl(L - \varepsilon < x_n < L + \varepsilon\bigr)$.
    \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K
          \;\bigl(x_n \in V_\varepsilon(L)\bigr)$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Limit Preserves Eventual Order
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences, and let $L,M\in\mathbb{R}$.
Suppose that $x_n\to L$ and $y_n\to M$. If there exists
$N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n\le y_n$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then
$L\le M$.

$L\le M$.
nodes.source_text:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences, and let $L,M\in\mathbb{R}$.
Suppose that $x_n\to L$ and $y_n\to M$. If there exists
$N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n\le y_n$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then
$L\le M$.

nodes.name: Strict Limit Separation Gives Eventual Order
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences, and let $A,B\in\mathbb{R}$.
Suppose that $x_n\to A$ and $y_n\to B$. If $A<B$, then there exists
$N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n<y_n$ for every $n\ge N$.

nodes.name: Eventual Strict Comparison Preserves Weak Limit Order
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences, and let $A,B\in\mathbb{R}$.
Suppose that $x_n\to A$ and $y_n\to B$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n<y_n$ for every
  $n\ge N$, then $A\le B$.
  \item If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n>y_n$ for every
  $n\ge N$, then $A\ge B$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Constant Comparison for Sequence Limits
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $A,B\in\mathbb{R}$. Suppose
that $x_n\to A$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n\le B$ for every
  $n\ge N$, then $A\le B$.
  \item If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n<B$ for every
  $n\ge N$, then $A\le B$.
  \item If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n\ge B$ for every
  $n\ge N$, then $A\ge B$.
  \item If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n>B$ for every
  $n\ge N$, then $A\ge B$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Constant Squeeze Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. If there
exists $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $L\le x_n\le L$ for every
$n\ge N_0$, then $x_n\to L$.

nodes.name: Sequence Squeeze Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(a_n)$, $(x_n)$, and $(b_n)$ be real sequences, and let
$L\in\mathbb{R}$. Suppose that $a_n\to L$ and $b_n\to L$. If there
exists $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $a_n\le x_n\le b_n$ for every
$n\ge N_0$, then $x_n\to L$.

nodes.name: Absolute-Value Squeeze Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(u_n)$ be real sequences, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$.
Suppose that $u_n\to 0$. If there exists $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
$|x_n-L|\le u_n$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then $x_n\to L$.

nodes.name: Constant Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{constant} exactly when there exists $c \in \mathbb{R}$ such that
$x_n = c$ for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

nodes.name: Null Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a
\textbf{null sequence} exactly when for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists
$N \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n| < \varepsilon$ for every $n \ge N$.

nodes.name: Constant Null Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$. If $x_n = c$
for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$, then $(x_n)$ is a null sequence if and only if
$c = 0$.

nodes.name: Ultimately Constant Sequence
nodes.statement_display:

\textbf{ultimately constant} exactly when there exist $N \in \mathbb{N}$ and
$c \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $x_n = c$ for every $n \ge N$.

nodes.name: Difference from the Limit is Null
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The sequence
$(x_n)$ converges to $L$ if and only if the sequence $(x_n - L)$ is a null
sequence.

nodes.name: Tail Equality Preserves Convergence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. If there exists
$N_0 \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n = y_n$ for every $n \ge N_0$, then
for every $L \in \mathbb{R}$, $(x_n)$ converges to $L$ if and only if
$(y_n)$ converges to $L$.

\textbf{bounded above} exactly when there exists $M \in \mathbb{R}$ such
that $x_n \le M$ for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

nodes.name: Absolute Bound Gives Upper and Lower Bounds
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $K > 0$. If $|x_n| \le K$ for every
$n \in \mathbb{N}$, then $-K \le x_n \le K$ for every
$n \in \mathbb{N}$.

nodes.name: Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function
\[
    x : \mathbb{N} \to X.
\]
The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the
sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or
simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or
simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

nodes.name: Divergent Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{divergent} if there is no $L\in\mathbb{R}$ such that
$x_n\to L$.

nodes.name: Divergence to Positive Infinity
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{diverges to}
$+\infty$ if
\[
  \forall M\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall n\ge N
  \;\bigl(M<x_n\bigr).
\]

  \;\bigl(M<x_n\bigr).
\]

  \;\bigl(x_n<M\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Oscillatory Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{oscillatory} if it is divergent, does not diverge to $+\infty$,
and does not diverge to $-\infty$.

nodes.name: Divergence to Infinity Implies Real Divergence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $x_n\to+\infty$ or $x_n\to-\infty$,
then $(x_n)$ is divergent.

nodes.name: Two Subsequential Limits Force Divergence

$L,K\in\mathbb{R}$ with $L\ne K$, then $(x_n)$ is divergent.

$x_{\sigma(k)}\to+\infty$.

subsequential limits of $(x_n)$.

nodes.name: Tail Supremum Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. The \textbf{tail supremum
sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(s_n)$ defined by
\[
  s_n:=\sup\{x_k:k\ge n\}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Limit Superior of a Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $(s_n)$ be its tail
supremum sequence. The \textbf{limit superior} of $(x_n)$ is
\[
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n:=\lim_{n\to\infty}s_n.
\]

nodes.name: Convergence iff Liminf Equals Limsup
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. Then
$x_n\to L$ if and only if
\[
  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n
  =
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
  =
  L.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:

$x_n\to L$ if and only if

  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
  =
  L.
\]

nodes.name: Limsup Is the Largest Subsequential Limit
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let
$S=\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n$. Then $S$ is a subsequential limit of
$(x_n)$, and every subsequential limit of $(x_n)$ is less than or equal
to $S$.

  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n
  <
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
\]
if and only if $(x_n)$ has two distinct subsequential limits.

$N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n\le y_n$ for every $n\ge N$, then

  \le

nodes.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and bounded above, then $(x_n)$ converges and
  \[
    \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\sup\{x_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}.
  \]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing and bounded below, then $(x_n)$ converges and
  \[
    \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\inf\{x_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}.
  \]
\end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

  \item If $(x_n)$ is strictly decreasing, then $(x_n)$ is decreasing.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Bounded Monotone Sequence Equivalences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing, then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
  $(x_n)$ is bounded above.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing, then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
  $(x_n)$ is bounded below.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is monotone, then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
  $(x_n)$ is bounded.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Unbounded Monotone Divergence
nodes.statement_display:

\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and is not bounded above, then
  $x_n\to+\infty$.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing and is not bounded below, then
  $x_n\to-\infty$.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Algebraic Transformations Preserve Monotonicity
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $c,\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing, then $(x_n+c)$ is increasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing, then $(x_n+c)$ is decreasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and $\alpha>0$, then $(\alpha x_n)$ is increasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and $\alpha<0$, then $(\alpha x_n)$ is decreasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing, then $(-x_n)$ is decreasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing, then $(-x_n)$ is increasing.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Tail Suprema and Infima Converge

\[

  \qquad
  i_n=\inf\{x_k:k\ge n\}.
\]
Then $(s_n)$ and $(i_n)$ converge.

nodes.name: Strictly Increasing Index Map
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ be a function. The function $\sigma$
is a \textbf{strictly increasing index map} if
\[
  k<\ell \Longrightarrow \sigma(k)<\sigma(\ell)
  \qquad
  \text{for all } k,\ell\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

nodes.name: Subsequential Limit
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. The real number
$L$ is a \textbf{subsequential limit} of $(x_n)$ if there exists a
subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ such that
\[
  \lim_{k\to\infty} x_{\sigma(k)}=L.
\]

nodes.name: Eventual Properties Pass to Subsequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $P(n)$ be a property of indices. If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such
that $P(n)$ holds for every $n\ge N$, then for every strictly increasing
index map $\sigma$ there exists $K\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $P(\sigma(k))$
holds for every $k\ge K$.

$(x_n)$. If $m\le x_n\le M$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then
$m\le L\le M$.

nodes.name: Squeeze Passes to Subsequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(a_n)$, $(x_n)$, and $(b_n)$ be real sequences. If
$a_n\le x_n\le b_n$ eventually, then for every subsequence
$(x_{\sigma(k)})$ there exists $K\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
  a_{\sigma(k)}\le x_{\sigma(k)}\le b_{\sigma(k)}
  \qquad
  \text{for every } k\ge K.
\]

nodes.name: $M$-Tail of a Sequence
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $M \in \mathbb{N}$. The
\textbf{$M$-tail} of $(x_n)$ is the sequence
\[
    (x_n)_{n \ge M} \;:=\; \bigl(x_M,\, x_{M+1},\, x_{M+2},\, \ldots\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Convergence of a Tail
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. The
$m$-tail $(x_{m+n})_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ converges if and only if
$(x_n)$ converges. Moreover, in this case,
\[
    \lim_{n \to \infty} x_{m+n} \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} x_n.
\]

nodes.name: Convergence by Domination
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. Let
$(a_n)$ be a sequence of positive real numbers with
$\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = 0$. If there exist a constant
$c > 0$ and an index $m \in \mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
    |x_n - L| \;\le\; c\, a_n \qquad \forall n \ge m,
\]
then $\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = L$.

nodes.name: Chain
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  A subset $C \subseteq S$ is a
\emph{chain} if every pair of elements in $C$ is comparable:
\[
\forall x, y \in C, \quad x \leq y \;\lor\; y \leq x.
\]
Equivalently, the restriction of $\leq$ to $C$ is a
\hyperref[def:lattice-order-total-order]{total order} on $C$.

nodes.name: Consistency
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  Elements $x, y \in S$ are
\emph{consistent} (or \emph{compatible}) if they admit a common upper
bound:
\[
\exists z \in S, \quad x \leq z \;\land\; y \leq z.
\]

nodes.name: Covering Relation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $x, y \in P$ with
$x < y$.  The element $y$ \emph{covers} $x$, written $x \prec y$, if
there is no element of $P$ strictly between them:
\[
x \prec y
\;\coloneqq\;
x < y
\;\land\;
\neg\,\bigl(\exists z \in P,\; x < z < y\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: Hasse Diagram Characterization for Finite Posets
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be finite partially ordered sets.
Then $P$ and $Q$ are order-isomorphic if and only if their Hasse diagrams
are isomorphic as directed graphs — that is, identical up to relabeling
of elements.

of elements.

nodes.name: Down-Set
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  A subset $Q \subseteq P$ is
a \emph{down-set} (or \emph{decreasing set}, or \emph{order ideal}) if it
is closed downward:
\[
\forall x \in Q,\; \forall y \in P,\quad y \leq x \;\Longrightarrow\; y \in Q.
\]

nodes.name: Up-Set
nodes.statement_display:

an \emph{up-set} (or \emph{increasing set}, or \emph{order filter}) if it
is closed upward:
\[
\forall x \in Q,\; \forall y \in P,\quad x \leq y \;\Longrightarrow\; y \in Q.
\]

nodes.name: Dual Ordered Set
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  The \emph{dual} (or
\emph{opposite}) of $P$, denoted $P^{\mathrm{op}}$, is the set $P$
equipped with the reversed order:
\[
x \leq_{P^{\mathrm{op}}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; y \leq_P x.
\]

nodes.name: Duality Principle
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\Phi$ be a statement about partially ordered sets, expressed purely
in terms of $\leq$, that holds in every partially ordered set.  Then the
dual statement $\Phi^{\mathrm{op}}$, obtained by replacing every
occurrence of $\leq$ with $\geq$, also holds in every partially ordered
set.

set.

nodes.name: Bottom Element
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  An element $\bot \in P$ is
a \emph{bottom element} (or \emph{least element}) of $P$ if
\[
\forall x \in P, \quad \bot \leq x.
\]
If a bottom element exists it is unique; it is denoted $\bot_P$ or
simply $\bot$.

nodes.name: Maximal Element
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $Q \subseteq P$.  An
element $a \in Q$ is \emph{maximal in $Q$} if no element of $Q$ strictly
exceeds it:
\[
\forall x \in Q, \quad a \leq x \;\Longrightarrow\; a = x.
\]
The set of maximal elements of $Q$ is denoted $\operatorname{Max}(Q)$.

nodes.name: Greatest Element
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $Q \subseteq P$.  An
element $m \in Q$ is the \emph{greatest element} (or \emph{maximum}) of
$Q$ if it dominates every element of $Q$:
\[
\forall x \in Q, \quad x \leq m.
\]
If it exists it is unique; it is denoted $\max Q$.

nodes.name: Every Nonempty Finite Poset Has a Maximal Element
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a finite partially ordered set.  Then every nonempty
subset $Q \subseteq P$ has at least one maximal element.  Furthermore,
for every $x \in P$ there exists $y \in \operatorname{Max}(P)$ with
$x \leq y$.

nodes.name: Lifting
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  The \emph{lifting} of $P$
is the poset $P_{\bot} \coloneqq P \cup \{\bot\}$, where $\bot \notin P$,
with the order:
\[
\bot \leq x \quad \text{for all } x \in P_{\bot},
\qquad
x \leq_{P_{\bot}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x \leq_P y
\quad \text{for all } x, y \in P.
\]
Thus $P_{\bot}$ is $P$ with a new
\hyperref[def:bottom-element]{bottom element} $\bot$ adjoined.

nodes.name: Order-Preserving Map
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets.  A map
$\varphi : P \to Q$ is \emph{order-preserving} (or \emph{monotone}) if
\[
\forall x, y \in P, \quad
x \leq_P y \;\Longrightarrow\; \varphi(x) \leq_Q \varphi(y).
\]

nodes.name: Order-Embedding
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets.  A map
$\varphi : P \to Q$ is an \emph{order-embedding} if
\[
\forall x, y \in P, \quad
x \leq_P y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \varphi(x) \leq_Q \varphi(y).
\]
When $\varphi$ is an order-embedding, one writes $\varphi : P \hookrightarrow Q$.

Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets.  A map
$\varphi : P \to Q$ is an \emph{order-embedding} if
\[
\forall x, y \in P, \quad
x \leq_P y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \varphi(x) \leq_Q \varphi(y).
\]
When $\varphi$ is an order-embedding, one writes $\varphi : P \hookrightarrow Q$.

nodes.name: Order-Isomorphism
nodes.statement_display:
An \hyperref[def:lattice-order-embedding]{order-embedding} $\varphi : P \to Q$
that is surjective is an \emph{order-isomorphism}.  When an
order-isomorphism exists, $P$ and $Q$ are said to be
\emph{order-isomorphic}, written $P \cong Q$.

nodes.text_preview: A relation $\leq$ on a set $S$ is a \emph{partial order} if it satisfies, for all $a, b, c \in S$: \begin{enumerate} \item \textbf{Reflexivity:} $a \leq a$. \item \textbf{Anti-symmetry:} $a \leq b \land b \leq a \Longrightarrow a = b$. \ite

nodes.text_preview: A relation $<$ on a set $S$ is a \emph{strict partial order} if it satisfies, for all $a, b, c \in S$: \begin{enumerate} \item \textbf{Irreflexivity:} $\neg(a < a)$. \item \textbf{Asymmetry:} $a < b \Longrightarrow \neg(b < a)$. \item \text

nodes.name: Total Order
nodes.statement_display:
A \hyperref[def:lattice-order-partial-order]{partial order} $\leq$ on a set $S$ is a
\emph{total order} (or \emph{linear order}) if it additionally satisfies
the \emph{law of trichotomy}: for all $a, b \in S$, exactly one of
\[
a < b, \qquad a = b, \qquad b < a
\]
holds.  The pair $(S, \leq)$ is called a \emph{totally ordered set}
or a \emph{chain}.

nodes.name: Partial Map
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A$ and $B$ be sets.  A \emph{partial map} $f : A \rightharpoonup B$
is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that every element of $A$ has
at most one image:
\[
\forall x \in A,\; \forall y_1, y_2 \in B,\quad
(x, y_1) \in f \;\land\; (x, y_2) \in f
\;\Longrightarrow\; y_1 = y_2.
\]

nodes.name: Total Map
nodes.statement_display:
A \hyperref[def:partial-map]{partial map} $f : A \rightharpoonup B$ is a
\emph{total map} if it is defined on every element of $A$:
\[
\forall x \in A,\; \exists y \in B,\quad (x, y) \in f.
\]
A total map is written $f : A \to B$ and is precisely a function in the
usual sense.

nodes.name: $\mathbf{F}^n$
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathbf{F}$ denote $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$, and let $n$ be a positive integer.
Then $\mathbf{F}^n$ is defined to be the set of all lists of length $n$ of elements of $\mathbf{F}$:
\[
\mathbf{F}^n
=
\{(x_1,\dots,x_n) : x_1,\dots,x_n \in \mathbf{F}\}.
\]
The entries in a list in $\mathbf{F}^n$ are called the coordinates of the list.

nodes.name: Subspace
nodes.statement_display:

A subset $U \subseteq V$ is called a \textbf{subspace} of $V$ if $U$ is itself
a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$ with the same addition and scalar multiplication
as on $V$.

nodes.name: Sum of Subspaces

U_1+\cdots+U_m
=

\]

nodes.name: Sum of Subspaces Is the Smallest Containing Subspace
nodes.statement_display:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$.
Then $U_1+\cdots+U_m$ is a subspace of $V$ containing each of
$U_1,\dots,U_m$.
Moreover, if $W$ is a subspace of $V$ containing each of
$U_1,\dots,U_m$, then
\[
U_1+\cdots+U_m \subseteq W.
\]

nodes.name: Direct Sum
nodes.statement_display:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$ such that
\[
V=U_1+\cdots+U_m.
\]
Then $V$ is called the \textbf{direct sum} of $U_1,\dots,U_m$ if each
vector $v \in V$ can be written in the form
\[
v=u_1+\cdots+u_m,
\qquad
u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m,
\]
in exactly one way.
In this case, we write
\[
V=U_1\oplus\cdots\oplus U_m.
\]

nodes.name: Condition for a Direct Sum
nodes.statement_display:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$ such that
\[
V=U_1+\cdots+U_m.
\]
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item \(V=U_1\oplus\cdots\oplus U_m\);
  \item if \(u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m\) and
        \[
        u_1+\cdots+u_m=0,
        \]
        then
        \[
        u_1=\cdots=u_m=0.
        \]
\end{enumerate}

U \cap W = \{0\}.

nodes.name: Point-slope form
nodes.statement_display:
Let $P_1=(x_1,y_1)$ be a point in the Euclidean plane and let
$m\in\mathbb{R}$. The point-slope form of the line through $P_1$ with slope
$m$ is
\[
  y-y_1=m(x-x_1).
\]

nodes.name: Two-point form
nodes.statement_display:
Let $P_1=(x_1,y_1)$ and $P_2=(x_2,y_2)$ be distinct points with
$x_1\neq x_2$. The two-point form of the line through $P_1$ and $P_2$ is
\[
  y-y_1=\left(\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}\right)(x-x_1).
\]

\]
where $A,B,C\in\mathbb{R}$ and $A^2+B^2\neq 0$.

\[

\]
where $\mathbf r_0\in\mathbb{R}^2$ is a point on the line and
$\mathbf v\in\mathbb{R}^2$ is a nonzero direction vector.

nodes.name: Intercept form
nodes.statement_display:
If a line meets the $x$-axis at $(a,0)$ and the $y$-axis at $(0,b)$ with
$a,b\neq 0$, its intercept form is
\[
  \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1.
\]

nodes.name: Affine parametric line in $\mathbb{R}^n$
nodes.statement_display:
Given distinct points $\mathbf x_1,\mathbf x_2\in\mathbb{R}^n$, the line
through them is
\[
  \mathbf x(t)=\mathbf x_1+t(\mathbf x_2-\mathbf x_1),
  \qquad t\in\mathbb{R}.
\]

nodes.name: Normal form

\[
  x\cos\alpha+y\sin\alpha-p=0,
\]
where $p\ge 0$ is the distance from the origin to the line.

nodes.name: Symmetric form
nodes.statement_display:
Given a point $(x_1,y_1)$ and direction cosines
$(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$, the symmetric form of a line is
\[
  \frac{x-x_1}{\cos\theta}
  =
  \frac{y-y_1}{\sin\theta}
  =
  r.
\]

nodes.name: Right-angled, obtuse-angled, and acute-angled triangles
nodes.statement_display:
Further, of trilateral figures, a right-angled triangle is that which has a
right angle, an obtuse-angled triangle that which has an obtuse angle, and an
acute-angled triangle that which has its three angles acute.
nodes.statement_tex:

acute-angled triangle that which has its three angles acute.

nodes.name: Quadrilateral classes
nodes.statement_display:
Of quadrilateral figures, a square is that which is both equilateral and
right-angled; an oblong that which is right-angled but not equilateral; a
rhombus that which is equilateral but not right-angled; and a rhomboid that
which has its opposite sides and angles equal to one another but is neither
equilateral nor right-angled. And let quadrilaterals other than these be called
trapezia.

nodes.name: Parallel straight lines
nodes.statement_display:
Parallel straight lines are straight lines which, being in the same plane and
being produced indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in
either direction.
nodes.statement_tex:

either direction.

nodes.statement_display: Let it have been postulated that all right angles are equal to one another.

nodes.name: Euclid Postulate V: Parallel postulate
nodes.statement_display:
Let it have been postulated that, if a straight line falling on two straight
lines makes the interior angles on the same side less than two right angles,
the two straight lines, if produced indefinitely, meet on that side on which are
the angles less than the two right angles.

nodes.name: Convex Set
nodes.statement_display:
A set \(S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2\) is \emph{convex} if for every pair of points
\(a,b\in S\) and every \(\lambda\in[0,1]\),
\[
  \lambda a+(1-\lambda)b\in S.
\]
Equivalently, \(S\) contains the line segment between any two of its points.

nodes.name: Convex Combination
nodes.statement_display:
A \emph{convex combination} of points
\(q_1,\dots,q_n\in\mathbb{R}^2\) is a point of the form
\[
  \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i,
  \qquad
  \lambda_i\ge 0 \text{ for all } i,
  \qquad
  \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i=1.
\]
The numbers \(\lambda_i\) are called the weights.

nodes.name: Convex Hull
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{convex hull} of a set \(P\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\), written
\(\conv(P)\), is the set of all convex combinations of finitely many points of
\(P\):
\[
  \conv(P)
  =
  \left\{
    \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i
    \;\middle|\;
    n\ge 1,\ q_i\in P,\ \lambda_i\ge 0,\ \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i=1
  \right\}.
\]
Equivalently, \(\conv(P)\) is the smallest convex set containing \(P\), i.e. the
intersection of all convex sets that contain \(P\).

nodes.name: Convex hull as the smallest convex set
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(P\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\). Then \(\conv(P)\) is convex, contains \(P\), and
is contained in every convex set that contains \(P\). Hence
\[
  \conv(P)
  =
  \bigcap\{K\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2 : K\text{ is convex and }P\subseteq K\}.
\]

\]

nodes.name: Extreme Point
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(S\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\) be convex. A point \(p\in S\) is an
\emph{extreme point} of \(S\) if whenever
\[
  p=\lambda a+(1-\lambda)b,
  \qquad
  a,b\in S,\quad \lambda\in(0,1),
\]
it follows that \(a=b=p\). For a finite point set \(P\), the extreme points of
\(\conv(P)\) are called the vertices of the convex hull.

nodes.name: Supporting Line
nodes.statement_display:
A line \(\ell\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\) is a \emph{supporting line} of a convex set
\(S\) at a point \(p\in S\) if \(p\in\ell\) and \(S\) lies entirely in one of the
two closed half-planes bounded by \(\ell\). Equivalently, there exists
\(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}\) such that
\[
  \ell=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^2:\langle d,x\rangle=\langle d,p\rangle\}
\]
and
\[
  \langle d,q\rangle\le \langle d,p\rangle
  \qquad
  \text{for all } q\in S.
\]

nodes.name: Linear functionals preserve convex combinations
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\), and let
\[
  z=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i
\]
be a convex combination of points \(q_1,\dots,q_n\in\mathbb{R}^2\). Then
\[
  \langle d,z\rangle
  =
  \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i\langle d,q_i\rangle.
\]
Consequently, if \(\langle d,q_i\rangle\le M\) for every \(i\), then
\[
  \langle d,z\rangle\le M.
\]

nodes.name: Supporting functional characterization of hull vertices
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. A point \(p\in P\)
is a vertex of \(\conv(P)\) if and only if there exists a direction
\(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}\) such that
\[
  \langle d,p\rangle>\langle d,q\rangle
  \qquad
  \text{for all } q\in P\setminus\{p\}.
\]
Equivalently, \(p\) is the unique point of \(P\) attaining
\[
  \max_{q\in P}\langle d,q\rangle.
\]
Thus every hull vertex is a maximum of \(P\) under some linear functional.

nodes.name: Monotone slope characterization of the upper hull
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. Let
\[
  v_0,v_1,\dots,v_m
\]
be the vertices of the upper hull of \(P\), listed in increasing
\(x\)-coordinate, and define
\[
  s_k
  =
  \frac{y(v_{k+1})-y(v_k)}{x(v_{k+1})-x(v_k)},
  \qquad
  k=0,1,\dots,m-1.
\]
Then
\[
  s_0>s_1>\cdots>s_{m-1}.
\]
Conversely, if points \(w_0,\dots,w_m\) satisfy
\[
  x(w_0)<x(w_1)<\cdots<x(w_m)
\]
and their consecutive slopes are strictly decreasing, then every \(w_k\) is a
vertex of
\[
  \conv(\{w_0,\dots,w_m\}).
\]
The lower hull is characterized similarly, with strictly increasing consecutive
slopes.

nodes.name: Greedy slope construction of the upper hull
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. Define
\[
  v_0=\argmin_{p\in P}x(p).
\]
Given \(v_k\) with
\[
  x(v_k)<\max_{p\in P}x(p),
\]
define
\[
  v_{k+1}
  =
  \argmax_{\substack{q\in P\\x(q)>x(v_k)}}
  \frac{y(q)-y(v_k)}{x(q)-x(v_k)}.
\]
Terminate once
\[
  v_k=\argmax_{p\in P}x(p).
\]
Then the points
\[
  v_0,v_1,\dots,v_m
\]
produced by this construction are exactly the vertices of the upper hull of
\(P\), and the slopes encountered along the way are strictly decreasing.

  v_0,v_1,\dots,v_m
\]
produced by this construction are exactly the vertices of the upper hull of
\(P\), and the slopes encountered along the way are strictly decreasing.

nodes.name: Malthusian Population Growth Model
nodes.statement_display:
The \textbf{Malthusian population growth model} assumes that the rate of change of a population is proportional to the current population.

If $P(t)$ denotes the population at time $t$, then the model is
\[
P'(t)=kP(t),
\]
where $k$ is a constant called the \textbf{growth rate} or \textbf{proportionality constant}.

nodes.name: Decay
nodes.statement_display:
A quantity is said to undergo \textbf{decay} if it decreases over time.

In a differential equation model, if $Q(t)$ denotes the quantity at time $t$, then decay is often modeled by an equation of the form
\[
Q'(t)=-kQ(t),
\]
where $k>0$ is a constant.

nodes.name: Newton's Law of Cooling
nodes.statement_display:
\textbf{Newton's Law of Cooling} states that the rate at which an object's temperature changes is proportional to the difference between the object's temperature and the surrounding ambient temperature.

If $T(t)$ denotes the temperature of the object at time $t$, and $T_a$ denotes the ambient temperature, then the model is
\[
T'(t)=-k\bigl(T(t)-T_a\bigr),
\]
where $k>0$ is a constant called the \textbf{cooling constant}.

nodes.name: General Solution
nodes.statement_display:
A \textbf{general solution} of a differential equation is a family of solutions containing one or more arbitrary constants.

For an $n$th-order ordinary differential equation, the general solution typically contains $n$ arbitrary constants:
\[
y(t)=\Phi(t,C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n).
\]
Each choice of the constants $C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n$ gives a particular solution of the differential equation.

nodes.name: Family of Solutions
nodes.statement_display:
A \textbf{family of solutions} of a differential equation is a collection of solution functions described by one or more parameters.

For example, a family of solutions may be written in the form
\[
y(t)=\Phi(t,C),
\]
or more generally,
\[
y(t)=\Phi(t,C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n),
\]
where $C,C_1,\dots,C_n$ are parameters. Each choice of the parameters gives one particular solution.

nodes.name: Initial Condition
nodes.statement_display:
An \textbf{initial condition} specifies the value of the unknown function at a particular value of the independent variable.

For a first-order differential equation involving an unknown function $y=y(t)$, an initial condition has the form
\[
y(t_0)=y_0,
\]
where $t_0$ is the initial time and $y_0$ is the initial value.

nodes.name: Initial Value Problem
nodes.statement_display:
An \textbf{initial value problem}, or \textbf{IVP}, is a differential equation together with one or more initial conditions.

For a first-order differential equation, an initial value problem has the form
\[
y'=f(t,y),
\qquad
y(t_0)=y_0.
\]
The differential equation describes how the function changes, while the initial condition specifies the value of the solution at the initial time $t_0$.

nodes.name: Existence
nodes.statement_display:
An initial value problem is said to have \textbf{existence} if there is at least one function that satisfies both the differential equation and the initial condition.

For a first-order initial value problem
\[
y'=f(t,y),
\qquad
y(t_0)=y_0,
\]
existence means that there is a function $y(t)$, defined on some interval containing $t_0$, such that
\[
y'(t)=f(t,y(t))
\]
and
\[
y(t_0)=y_0.
\]

nodes.name: Uniqueness
nodes.statement_display:
An initial value problem is said to have \textbf{uniqueness} if it has at most one solution on the interval under consideration.

For a first-order initial value problem
\[
y'=f(t,y),
\qquad
y(t_0)=y_0,
\]
uniqueness means that if $y_1(t)$ and $y_2(t)$ are both solutions on the same interval $I$ containing $t_0$, then
\[
y_1(t)=y_2(t)
\]
for every $t\in I$.

nodes.name: Interval of Existence
nodes.statement_display:
An \textbf{interval of existence} for a solution of an initial value problem is an interval on which the solution is defined and satisfies the differential equation.

For an initial value problem
\[
y'=f(t,y),
\qquad
y(t_0)=y_0,
\]
an interval $I$ is an interval of existence if $t_0\in I$ and there is a solution $y:I\to\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
y'(t)=f(t,y(t))
\]
for every $t\in I$, and
\[
y(t_0)=y_0.
\]

nodes.name: Differential Equation
nodes.statement_display:
A \textbf{differential equation} is an equation whose unknown is a function and whose terms involve one or more derivatives of that function.

For an unknown function $y=y(t)$, a differential equation may be written in the form
\[
F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0.
\]
Here $t$ is the independent variable, $y(t)$ is the unknown function, and
\[
y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)}
\]
are derivatives of $y$.
nodes.statement_tex:

nodes.name: Order of a Differential Equation
nodes.statement_display:
The \textbf{order} of a differential equation is the highest derivative of the unknown function that appears in the equation.

For example, if an equation involving an unknown function $y=y(t)$ can be written in the form
\[
F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0
\]
and $y^{(n)}$ is the highest derivative that appears, then the differential equation has \textbf{order} $n$.

nodes.name: Solution of a Differential Equation
nodes.statement_display:
A \textbf{solution} of a differential equation is a function that satisfies the equation on a specified interval.

More precisely, if a differential equation is written in the form
\[
F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0,
\]
then a solution on an interval $I$ is a function $y:I\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $y$ has all derivatives required by the equation and
\[
F(t,y(t),y'(t),y''(t),\dots,y^{(n)}(t))=0
\]
for every $t\in I$.

nodes.name: Autonomous Differential Equation
nodes.statement_display:
An \textbf{autonomous differential equation} is a differential equation in which the independent variable does not appear explicitly.

For a first-order ordinary differential equation, an autonomous equation has the form
\[
y'=f(y),
\]
where the rate of change of $y$ depends only on the current value of $y$, not directly on time $t$.

nodes.name: Non-Autonomous Differential Equation
nodes.statement_display:
A \textbf{non-autonomous differential equation} is a differential equation in which the independent variable appears explicitly.

For a first-order ordinary differential equation, a non-autonomous equation has the form
\[
y'=f(t,y),
\]
where the rate of change of $y$ may depend both on the current value of $y$ and on the independent variable $t$.

nodes.name: Linear Differential Equation
nodes.statement_display:
A differential equation is called \textbf{linear} if the unknown function and its derivatives appear only to the first power and are not multiplied together.

An $n$th-order linear ordinary differential equation for an unknown function $y=y(t)$ has the form
\[
a_n(t)y^{(n)}+a_{n-1}(t)y^{(n-1)}+\cdots+a_1(t)y'+a_0(t)y=g(t),
\]
where the coefficient functions
\[
a_0(t),a_1(t),\dots,a_n(t)
\]
and the forcing term $g(t)$ are given functions of the independent variable $t$.

nodes.name: IEEE 754 Binary Floating-Point Datum
nodes.statement_display:
An \emph{IEEE 754 binary floating-point datum} is a binary encoding partitioned into
three fields:

\begin{itemize}
    \item a \emph{sign field} consisting of one bit,
    \item an \emph{exponent field} consisting of a fixed number of bits,
    \item a \emph{fraction field} consisting of a fixed number of bits.
\end{itemize}

Its interpretation depends on the exponent-field pattern and the fraction-field pattern.

nodes.name: Normalized IEEE 754 Binary Number
nodes.statement_display:
Let an IEEE 754 binary floating-point format have exponent bias $\mathrm{bias}$.
A finite encoded datum is called \emph{normalized} if its exponent field is neither
all zeros nor all ones.

If $s$ is the sign bit, if $e$ is the unsigned integer determined by the exponent field,
and if $f$ is the binary fraction determined by the fraction field, then the represented
value is
\[
(-1)^s (1.f)_2 2^{e-\mathrm{bias}}.
\]

all zeros nor all ones.

If $s$ is the sign bit, if $e$ is the unsigned integer determined by the exponent field,
and if $f$ is the binary fraction determined by the fraction field, then the represented
value is
\[
(-1)^s (1.f)_2 2^{e-\mathrm{bias}}.
\]

nodes.name: Subnormal IEEE 754 Binary Number
nodes.statement_display:
Let an IEEE 754 binary floating-point format have exponent bias $\mathrm{bias}$.
A finite encoded datum is called \emph{subnormal} if its exponent field is all zeros
and its fraction field is not all zeros.

If $s$ is the sign bit and if $f$ is the binary fraction determined by the fraction field,
then the represented value is
\[
(-1)^s (0.f)_2 2^{1-\mathrm{bias}}.
\]

nodes.name: IEEE 754 Infinity Encoding
nodes.statement_display:
An IEEE 754 binary floating-point datum encodes \emph{infinity} if its exponent field
is all ones and its fraction field is all zeros.

The sign bit determines whether the represented value is $+\infty$ or $-\infty$.

nodes.name: IEEE 754 Binary16 Format
nodes.statement_display:
The \emph{IEEE 754 binary16 format} is the 16-bit binary floating-point interchange format
with the following field layout:

\begin{itemize}
    \item $1$ sign bit,
    \item $5$ exponent bits,
    \item $10$ fraction bits,
    \item exponent bias $15$.
\end{itemize}

For a normalized finite binary16 datum, the represented value is
\[
(-1)^s (1.f)_2 2^{e-15}.
\]

nodes.name: Box / Product Interval
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}^n$ with $a\le b$ coordinatewise (i.e., $a_i\le b_i$ for all $i$).
The \emph{box} (or \emph{interval vector}) determined by $(a,b)$ is
\[
[a,b] \;:=\; \{x\in\mathbb{R}^n : a\le x \le b\}
\;=\;\prod_{i=1}^n [a_i,b_i].
\]

nodes.name: Box Containment
nodes.statement_display:
For $a\le b$ and $c\le d$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, we write $[a,b]\subseteq[c,d]$ iff
\[
(\forall x\in\mathbb{R}^n)\ \bigl(x\in[a,b]\Rightarrow x\in[c,d]\bigr).
\]
Equivalently (and most useful computationally),
\[
[a,b]\subseteq[c,d]\quad \Longleftrightarrow\quad c\le a\ \land\ b\le d
\qquad\text{(coordinatewise).}
\]

nodes.name: Minkowski Addition of Boxes
nodes.statement_display:
For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, their \emph{Minkowski sum} is
\[
X\oplus Y \;:=\; \{x+y : x\in X,\ y\in Y\}.
\]
For boxes, this closes in the class of boxes and satisfies the endpoint rule
\[
[a,b]\oplus[c,d] \;=\; [a+c,\; b+d].
\]

nodes.name: Box Subtraction / Difference
nodes.statement_display:
For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, define
\[
X\ominus Y \;:=\; \{x-y : x\in X,\ y\in Y\}.
\]
Then
\[
[a,b]\ominus[c,d] \;=\; [a-d,\; b-c].
\]

nodes.name: Scalar Multiplication of a Box
nodes.statement_display:
For $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$ and a box $[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$, define
\[
\lambda[a,b] \;:=\; \{\lambda x : x\in[a,b]\}.
\]
Then
\[
\lambda[a,b]=
\begin{cases}
[\lambda a,\ \lambda b], & \lambda\ge 0,\\[4pt]
[\lambda b,\ \lambda a], & \lambda<0,
\end{cases}
\]
where the inequalities are coordinatewise.

nodes.name: Coordinatewise / Hadamard Product of Boxes
nodes.statement_display:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{R}^n$, write $(x\odot y)_i := x_i y_i$.
For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$, define
\[
X\odot Y \;:=\; \{x\odot y : x\in X,\ y\in Y\}.
\]
Then $X\odot Y$ is again a box, computed coordinatewise:
\[
[a,b]\odot[c,d] \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^n \Bigl[\min S_i,\ \max S_i\Bigr],
\qquad
S_i:=\{a_ic_i,\ a_id_i,\ b_ic_i,\ b_id_i\}.
\]

nodes.name: Interval Addition
nodes.statement_display:
For closed intervals $[a,b]$ and $[c,d]$, their \emph{sum} is
\[
  [a,b] + [c,d] \;=\; [a+c,\; b+d].
\]

\]

\[
  [a,b] \cdot [c,d] \;=\;
  \bigl[\min(ac, ad, bc, bd),\; \max(ac, ad, bc, bd)\bigr].
\]

nodes.name: Boolean Ring
nodes.statement_display:
A \textbf{Boolean ring} is an idempotent ring with unit.

That is, a Boolean ring is a ring $(R, +, \cdot, -, 0, 1)$
satisfying the additional axiom:
\[
p \cdot p = p \qquad \text{for all } p \in R. \tag{11}
\]
An element $p$ satisfying $p \cdot p = p$ is called
\textbf{idempotent}.
A ring in which every element is idempotent is called an
\textbf{idempotent ring}.

The official axiom set for a Boolean ring consists of
axioms~(1)--(3), (5), (6), (8)--(11), where axiom~(7)
$($the additive inverse law$)$ is replaced by
axiom~(10) $($characteristic~$2)$, which is derived as a theorem.
Axiom numbering follows Halmos--Givant~\cite{HalmosGivant2009}.

Axiom numbering follows Halmos--Givant~\cite{HalmosGivant2009}.
nodes.text_preview: A \textbf{Boolean ring} is an idempotent ring with unit. That is, a Boolean ring is a ring $(R, +, \cdot, -, 0, 1)$ satisfying the additional axiom: \[ p \cdot p = p \qquad \text{for all } p \in R. \tag{11} \] An element $p$ satisfying $p \

nodes.name: Idempotent Element
nodes.statement_display:
An element $p$ of a ring $R$ is called \textbf{idempotent} if
\[
p \cdot p = p.
\]
A ring in which every element is idempotent is called an
\textbf{idempotent ring}.

nodes.name: Characteristic 2
nodes.statement_display:
A ring $R$ is said to have \textbf{characteristic~$2$} if
\[
p + p = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p \in R. \tag{10}
\]

p + p = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p \in R.

nodes.text_preview: A \textbf{ring} is a non-empty set $R$ together with two binary operations $+$ and $\cdot$ on $R$, a unary operation $-$ on $R$, and two distinguished elements $0, 1 \in R$, satisfying the following axioms for all $p, q, r \in R$: \begin{en

nodes.name: Lambda Terms
nodes.statement_display:
The set $\Lambda$ of $\lambda$-terms is defined inductively as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Every variable $x$ is a $\lambda$-term.
    \item If $M$ is a $\lambda$-term and $x$ is a variable, then
    \[
    \lambda x.\,M
    \]
    is a $\lambda$-term.
    \item If $M$ and $N$ are $\lambda$-terms, then
    \[
    MN
    \]
    is a $\lambda$-term.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: First-Order Language $\mathcal{L}$
nodes.statement_display:
A \textbf{first-order language} $\mathcal{L}$ consists of three (possibly
empty) sets of non-logical symbols:
\begin{itemize}
  \item a set $\mathcal{R}$ of \textbf{relation symbols} (also called
        \emph{predicate symbols}), each assigned a fixed arity $n \geq 1$;
  \item a set $\mathcal{F}$ of \textbf{function symbols}, each assigned a
        fixed \textbf{arity} $n \geq 0$ (0-ary function symbols are
        \emph{constant symbols});
  \item a set $\mathcal{C}$ of \textbf{constant symbols} (equivalently,
        0-ary function symbols — listed separately for emphasis).
\end{itemize}
Together with the logical symbols common to all first-order languages
(variables, connectives $\lnot, \land, \lor, \to, \leftrightarrow$,
quantifiers $\forall, \exists$, equality $=$, parentheses), the triple
$(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{C})$ determines $\mathcal{L}$.

nodes.text_preview: Let $\mathcal{L} = (\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{C})$ be a first-order language. An \textbf{$\mathcal{L}$-structure} $\mathcal{M}$ consists of: \begin{enumerate} \item a non-empty set $M$ called the \textbf{domain} (or \textbf{univers

nodes.name: Variable Assignment
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure with domain $M$, and let
$\mathrm{Var} = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, \ldots\}$ be a fixed countable set of
first-order variables.

A \textbf{variable assignment} (or \textbf{environment}) for $\mathcal{M}$
is a function $s : \mathrm{Var} \to M$.

For a variable assignment $s$, a variable $v_i$, and an element $a \in M$,
write $s[v_i \mapsto a]$ for the assignment that agrees with $s$ on all
variables except $v_i$, where it returns $a$:
\[
  s[v_i \mapsto a](v_j) =
  \begin{cases}
    a      & \text{if } j = i, \\
    s(v_j) & \text{if } j \neq i.
  \end{cases}
\]

nodes.name: Term Evaluation
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure and $s : \mathrm{Var} \to M$
a variable assignment.
The \textbf{evaluation} $s(t) \in M$ of an $\mathcal{L}$-term $t$ under
$s$ is defined inductively:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item If $t = v_i$ is a variable, then $s(t) = s(v_i)$.
  \item If $t = c$ is a constant symbol, then $s(t) = c^{\mathcal{M}}$.
  \item If $t = f(t_1, \ldots, t_n)$ for an $n$-ary function symbol $f$,
        then $s(t) = f^{\mathcal{M}}(s(t_1), \ldots, s(t_n))$.
\end{enumerate}
